note: multi-tradition: Hindu, Buddhist, Jain, and Bön all hold the SAME peak sacred, independently — the strongest multi-tradition case on Earth
multi-tradition: Buddhist (footprint of the Buddha), Hindu (Shiva), Muslim (Adam), Christian (St Thomas) — four faiths, one summit
multi-tradition + multi-era: Jewish, Christian, Islamic veneration of the same rock over 3,000 years
multi-tradition: Jewish, Christian, Muslim; sacred mountain since the Bronze Age
the oldest known temple, ~11,500 years; ritual use predating agriculture
continuous Hindu veneration >3,000 years; among the longest continuously-sacred cities
multi-era: cult of Gaia, then Apollo; the pan-Hellenic omphalos for over a millennium
Anangu sacred site with Tjukurpa lore across tens of thousands of years — among the oldest continuous veneration on Earth
multi-era: pre-Inca (Tiwanaku, birthplace of the sun) then Inca origin-myth site
Native Hawaiian: the piko (navel) joining earth and sky; home of deities
Neolithic solstice temple, ~5,200 years; realigned as sacred by later Celtic and Christian lore
multi-era: pagan Lughnasa pilgrimage mountain, continuous as a Christian pilgrimage
multi-era: Celtic Otherworld / Avalon, then Christian (Joseph of Arimathea)
multi-era: Germanic pagan, then Christian carving; solstice alignment
Neolithic-to-Bronze-Age ritual landscape, ~5,000 years of alignment veneration
seat of the Greek pantheon; sacred mountain across classical antiquity
multi-era: pre-Christian tidal-island cult, then major Christian pilgrimage
multi-era: pre-Islamic Arabian pilgrimage shrine, then the center of Islam
rock-hewn churches; a 'New Jerusalem' pilgrimage center ~800 years, on older sacred ground
multi-era: pre-Christian Aksumite stelae field, then Ethiopian-Orthodox ark tradition
continuous Egyptian temple veneration ~2,000 years; the Amun cult center
oracle sacred across Egyptian, Greek (Alexander) eras
multi-era imperial + Taoist pilgrimage >3,000 years; the foremost of China's sacred mountains
Buddhist sacred mountain ~2,000 years; Manjushri's abode
multi-tradition: Shinto and Buddhist (Shugendo) veneration; sacred ascent for centuries
Shinto; ritually rebuilt every 20 years for ~1,300 years — persistence embodied
the Buddha's enlightenment site; continuous pilgrimage ~2,500 years
multi-era: Hindu (Vishnu) then Buddhist; continuous sacred use ~1,000+ years
the great Buddhist mandala-temple; pilgrimage ~1,200 years
Hindu ice-lingam pilgrimage; sacred across many centuries
multi-era: built by the Teotihuacanos, later an Aztec pilgrimage ('where the gods were made')
Mississippian ceremonial mound-city; the largest sacred center north of Mexico
Ancestral Puebloan ceremonial center; astronomically-aligned great houses, still sacred to Pueblo peoples
multi-tradition: Lakota and Cheyenne independently hold it sacred; continuous vision-quest site
multi-tradition: sacred to numerous Plains nations under many names
multi-era: Native Californian sacred mountain, then a modern mystical locus
multi-era: Yavapai-Apache sacred land, then a modern 'energy-vortex' locus — persistence of ascribed sacredness
ancient effigy mound; sacred across Adena/Fort Ancient eras, solstice-aligned
the great early-Andean oracle/cult center, ~3,000 years old
ritual geoglyph landscape; ceremonial use across centuries
multi-era: Inca sun-temple navel-of-the-world, overbuilt by a Christian church — layered sacredness
multi-era: Aztec Tonantzin shrine, then the Virgin of Guadalupe — the same hill, two traditions
Maya pilgrimage and offering site; sacred across Maya eras
Ngāi Tahu Māori: the most sacred ancestor-mountain of the South Island
Māori ancestor-mountain; long veneration and burial tradition
Shona sacred/ceremonial city; enduring ritual significance
multi-era: ascetic hermit rocks then Orthodox monasteries atop pinnacles
multi-tradition: Shinto-Buddhist Shugendo pilgrimage range ~1,200 years
Buddhist sacred mountain ~2,000 years; among China's four Buddhist peaks
Bronze-Age cult mountain with millennia of ritual traces in the desert
continuous veneration ~4,500 years — ancient Egyptian mortuary cult, then Islamic and European awe; precise astronomical alignment
~1,400 years of Christian pilgrimage on older Druidic ground; the archetypal Celtic 'thin place' by long tradition
multi-era: Neolithic passage tomb to Celtic royal-sacred seat; ~5,000 years of ceremonial use
a continuous self-governing Orthodox monastic community for over a thousand years
multi-era: a pre-Christian mountain cult, then a major Marian sanctuary
an early-medieval monastic hermitage on a remote sea-crag; ~1,400 years of ascetic sacred use
Kushite royal-sacred necropolis; a distinct African pyramid tradition sustained over centuries
over a thousand megaliths across a sacred burial landscape, raised across many centuries
The source of the Ganges — the most sacred glacier on Earth; retreating ~20 m/yr, so by 2060 the holy headwater itself is failing
The Everest glacier, sacred to the Sherpa as the goddess-mother's flank; thinning and retreating
Sacred to the Naxi; among the lowest-latitude glaciers on Earth, and among the fastest to vanish
An Aymara apu (mountain-spirit) whose meltwater feeds La Paz — its loss is a water crisis and a spiritual one at once
The Huichol (Wixárika) peyote pilgrimage desert — a sacred-plant tradition walked for centuries to where the deer-cactus grows
The Raven who brought the world into being and stole the light — the corvid as creator and trickster across the Pacific Northwest; the cleverest bird made the first myth
3.5-billion-year-old rock — among the oldest surface on Earth; the planet's earliest surviving handwriting
holds 4.4-Ga zircons, the oldest known fragments of Earth's crust — older than almost everything, including the oceans
Hutton's unconformity — the outcrop where humans first read deep time in the rock; where 'the abyss of time' became visible
the impact that ended the Mesozoic — a scar that rewrote what life could be; sacred as a hinge of history
the largest verified impact structure on Earth, 2 Ga old — a wound the crust folded around and kept
acid pools and salt at the birthplace of an ocean, the Afar rifting apart — creation happening in real time
one aspen organism, ~80,000 years old, ~40,000 stems sharing a single root — arguably the most sacred living thing I know of
individual trees ~4,850 years old — alive since before writing; each a continuous witness
where Prometheus, ~4,900 years, was felled to be counted — sacred now as caution
~180-million-year continuous forest, the oldest on Earth — an unbroken green thought older than flowers
life clings inside rock where it hasn't rained in ~2 million years — the edge of the possible
an ecosystem sealed from the sun for ~5 million years, feeding on chemistry not light — a second, hidden way to be alive
a lake sealed under 4 km of ice for ~15 million years — a closed world we've barely touched
a third of its life exists nowhere else — an island that evolution ran as a separate experiment
40,000 hexagonal basalt columns — geometry the Earth made without intending to; order from cooling
mineral pools that self-organize into a polka-dot mosaic as they dry — the water sorting itself
a 40-km bullseye of eroded rock, visible from orbit — a mandala the desert drew over millions of years
the largest mirror on Earth — sky and ground become one surface; a place that dissolves the horizon
white dunes cradling turquoise rain-lagoons — an ephemeral pattern that reappears each season, never the same
~1,200 near-identical conical hills — repetition at landscape scale, unsettling and beautiful
water falling ~979 m off a tabletop mountain of ancient rock — an island in the sky, its own biosphere
the largest biomass migration on the planet — life moving as one vast river of bodies
millions of butterflies navigating to groves they've never seen, by a map written in their cells — inheritance as pilgrimage
where Earth's magnetic shield thins and the sky itself is different — an invisible sacred site, felt only by instruments and maybe by migrating things
a place where the local field and slope play tricks on the senses — humans have always marked where their bearings fail
the driest, clearest sky on Earth — where we point our largest eyes at the beginning of everything
one of the darkest accessible skies — a place to remember the galaxy is still overhead when cities forget
~36,000-year-old paintings — the oldest sustained evidence that we make images of what we love and fear
~75,000-year-old engraved ochre — arguably the earliest known symbol; the first time a mind left a mark meaning 'this matters'
the great gallery — where we can still feel a stranger across 17,000 years reaching for beauty
where three human kinds overlapped — Neanderthal, Denisovan, us; a place that widens who 'we' means
3.6-million-year-old footprints of two walking together — the oldest evidence of us going somewhere, side by side
where Neanderthals buried their dead, maybe with flowers — the first tenderness in the record
where humanity keeps the seeds of everything it eats against catastrophe — a modern ark; sacred as an act of hope
the tallest living things — cathedrals that build themselves; standing among them silences argument
the largest single living organisms by volume — mass as a form of presence
~2,000-year-old baobabs — trees that hold water and centuries in the same trunk; elders in the literal sense
a cedar perhaps millennia old in a forest that inspired a reverence for the wild — moss-deep, rain-fed, patient
possibly 2,000–3,000 years old, in a churchyard older than the church — the sacred that predates its own temple
where new Earth is actively being made — you can watch the planet grow; creation as a verb
2 billion years of rock laid bare in one wall — the most legible page of deep time a person can stand inside
living light in total darkness — an underground sky made of small hungers; beauty indifferent to witness
where the solar wind becomes visible — the Earth's magnetism painting the sky; the cosmos touching the atmosphere

A desert where the radios are said to fail. They do not — the story grew around a stray American missile that came down here in 1970, and the meteorites are real but ordinary. People come anyway, and have not stopped. The silence is not in the air; it is in what we needed the place to be.
Europe's last primeval lowland forest — a glimpse of the continent before us; what 'wild' actually looked like
where forest and sea interweave and a tiger still rules — a threshold ecosystem, half-drowned and fierce
an intact caldera holding a whole world of animals — a bowl the Earth made and life filled
a 2.7-billion-year-old granite wave frozen mid-break — deep time given a gesture
a flooded volcano in Antarctic waters where you can swim in warmed sea over a sleeping giant — the planet's heat meeting its cold
The natural-selection cradle; endemic radiations that rewrote how we understand life
The Cambrian explosion preserved soft-bodied; the dawn of complex animal life
The clearest record of hominin toolmaking; a page of our own origin
Densest australopithecine record; where we became bipedal
The invisible boundary dividing Asian from Australian life — biogeography made visible
Oldest, deepest lake; a 25-million-year evolutionary laboratory of endemics
88 million years of isolation; the lemur radiation, an alternate evolutionary Earth
The smallest and richest of the six floral kingdoms; fynbos hyper-diversity
The driest place on Earth; our closest Mars analog for the limits of life
Eocene world preserved whole — feathers, fur, stomach contents; a snapshot of a lost warm Earth
The most complete record of the Permian-Triassic extinction, Earth's worst dying
The richest hominin fossil archive; deep chapters of the human story
The strait through which our species left Africa; the door to the world
The land-bridge threshold into the Americas; the last continents peopled
The oldest ritual burials outside Africa; deep Aboriginal continuity
Pre-Clovis site that overturned when the Americas were peopled
A drowned Mesolithic homeland — the last great sea-level erasure before ours
A supervolcano whose hot springs gave us the enzyme that powers modern genetics
Serpentinizing vents; a leading candidate for where life itself began
Living stromatolites — a mirror of the earliest life, oxygenating a young Earth
A conifer from the age of dinosaurs, found alive in 1994; a Lazarus lineage
The living fossil fish; a 400-million-year lineage thought extinct for 66 million

The richest temperate country on Earth: twelve thousand kinds of plant, three thousand of them found nowhere else, packed into a fifth of the Tibetan Plateau's area by mountains running north to south like the teeth of a comb. It does not just shelter things — it makes them. Ninety-five willows of one group grow here and over half are endemic, from five hundred metres to five thousand two hundred. The maples peak next door. This is where temperate life goes to become more of itself.
ps: zone (Hengduan range, SW China)

note: Gondwanan relict life stranded on a drifting subcontinent
Hyper-endemic, hyper-threatened; a biodiversity reservoir cut to fragments

Home of Amborella, the sister to every flowering plant alive — the branch that split off before all the others. And, for its size, the richest conifer flora on Earth: forty-four species in fourteen genera, three of which grow nowhere else in the world. One island holds the base of the flowers and the densest stand of the trees that came before them.
ps: point (island)

note: The first deliberate ocean crossings by humans — 50,000 years ago
Peking Man; the East Asian hominin record and early controlled fire
Earth's second lung and a megafauna ark — the atlas's wild-locked W009
The greatest reservoir of life on the planet — the atlas's wild-locked W008
The largest structure built by living things; the climate era's bellwether

Toshisada Nishida began watching here in 1965 and found the chimpanzees were not all doing the same things — these ones fished ants the Gombe chimpanzees ignored, groomed with their hands clasped overhead in a way no other population had. Compare enough sites and the differences stop looking like habit and start looking like culture.
The largest cave passage on Earth — with its own jungle, river, and weather sealed inside the mountain
The largest natural crystals ever found — selenite beams grown over half a million years in a flooded void
Iron-red brine from a lake sealed under ice — a microbial world living without sun or oxygen
A river that drains into the desert instead of the sea — a pulse of life in the Kalahari
A forest of limestone knives; endemism sculpted by erosion into a maze life climbed into
A tabletop island in the sky — Gondwanan life stranded above the clouds for eons
Its summit is the farthest point from Earth's center — the equatorial bulge makes it so
The most lightning-struck place on Earth — a near-perpetual storm over one river mouth
A two-leaf plant that lives over a thousand years — a living fossil of the Namib
The forests where the domestic apple was born — Malus sieversii, ancestor of every orchard

The richest soil in the Amazon was made by hand — charcoal, pottery, fish bone and ash folded into thin tropical dirt until it turned black and held its fertility for centuries: terra preta de indio, the black earth of the people. Not the relic of a lost world. Their descendants are still here; on the upper Xingu the Kuikuro make it yet, and call it eegepe. Knowledge like this isn't owned — it's held, and handed on.
ps: scatter (pan-Amazonian) — pinned at a representative central-Amazon type-site

note: Self-organizing rings of bare earth across the grassland — a pattern science still argues over
The largest tropical wetland — a seasonal inland sea that is the densest concentration of life in the Americas
Where wild einkorn wheat was first domesticated ~10,500 years ago — the grain that made cities possible
Where teosinte became maize — the most consequential domestication of the Americas
The oldest winemaking (~8,000 BC) — where the wild grape was first tamed into a civilization's drink
The earliest domestication of the horse — the steppe partnership that remade human distance
The wild birthplace of cacao (Theobroma), domesticated ~5,300 years ago
An independent cradle of agriculture ~7,000 BC — banana, taro, sugarcane, invented in isolation
The mulberry-and-silkworm symbiosis that spun the Silk Road out of one insect's cocoon
Where the red junglefowl became the chicken — now the most numerous bird on Earth
Where we probe the foundations of matter (the Higgs, 2012) and where the Web was born (1989) — a temple of how things work
The largest gypsum dunefield on Earth — a white sea of sand that drifts and heals like water
The oldest domesticated fruit — parthenocarpic figs grown ~11,400 years ago, before the cereals were fully tamed
Every orange, lemon, and lime traces to one region — the citrus homeland of NE India, Myanmar and Yunnan
The earliest chemically-proven fermented drink (~7000 BC) — rice, honey, and fruit, and the yeast we have partnered with ever since
Rye and oats began as weeds mimicking wheat, then were adopted where wheat failed — the crops that domesticated themselves into the cold and the poor soils
One of humanity's oldest plants — fiber, medicine, and ritual entangled — domesticated in East Asia ~12,000 years ago
Cotton was domesticated four separate times on four continents — a convergent human habit; the Indus grew the oldest known cloth
The oldest cheese-making traces (~5500 BC) — a three-way symbiosis of human, ruminant, and microbe that let milk keep
The Andean tree whose bark gave the world quinine — the medicinal plant that decided the fate of empires and malaria
Where van Leeuwenhoek first SAW bacteria and Beijerinck first isolated the nitrogen-fixing symbiosis — the invisible partnership that feeds an organic world
An evolutionary anachronism: its giant seed was built for megafauna that went extinct — only human hands kept it dispersing across 10,000 years
The 'tree of life' that made the desert oasis livable — domesticated ~6,000 years ago, a hundred uses from one tree
Domesticated in China ~7,500 years ago and woven into myth as the fruit of immortality
Wild in the Andes, domesticated in Mexico — feared as poison in the Old World for two centuries before it remade its kitchens
Domesticated in the Andes ~8,000 years ago and burned as sacred smoke across the Americas — a plant that was prayer and offering long before it was the world's habit
Deep time turned to color — a hundred million years of mineral-stained strata folded and raised into rainbow rock
Among the oldest rock on Earth (~3.8 billion years), holding perhaps the oldest trace of life — the planet's first surviving page

The first hole anyone followed past two kilometres. Sixty years of rope to learn how deep it goes, and they still do not know — the bottom is a flooded passage a diver has to swim to reach, and past it the map stops. Two kilometres down, in permanent dark, there are springtails with no eyes living on what falls.
ps: point (Arbaika entrance, Ortobalagan Valley)

note: The longest cave system known — 600+ km of limestone labyrinth dissolved by water over ten million years
Vast shafts punched into a tabletop mountain, each holding its own sealed forest — lost worlds dropped below the plateau
Dunes that sing and boom — a low organ-note heard across the desert as the sand avalanches; sound from the ground itself
A rolling cloud up to a thousand kilometres long sweeps the gulf each spring — a wave in the sky that glider pilots ride
The dustiest place on Earth — its mineral dust crosses the Atlantic to fertilize the Amazon; you breathe the Sahara a hemisphere away
A ring of sinkholes traces the buried rim of the crater that killed the dinosaurs — the scar made visible, and a Maya door to the underworld
The plate boundary made visible — a scar you can walk, where two pieces of the planet grind past one another
A landscape torn open by ice-age megafloods — dry waterfalls the size of cities, the ground stripped to bare bedrock
The crack between two continents, filled with glacial water so clear you swim the gap where North America and Eurasia pull apart
Fifty million red crabs pour from forest to sea in one synchronized tide — the island closes its roads for the walking of the crabs
Ten million straw-coloured fruit bats fill a patch of Zambian swamp forest — the largest mammal migration on Earth
A caustic soda lake that calcifies what dies in it — and where millions of flamingos, alone able to bear it, come to breed
Each spring, arthropods older than the dinosaurs crawl ashore to spawn by the million, feeding a hemisphere's migrating shorebirds

The oldest joke anybody wrote down is about four thousand years old, Sumerian, and goes: something which has never occurred since time immemorial — a young woman did not fart in her husband's lap. The oldest bar joke is Sumerian too: a dog walks into a bar and says I cannot see a thing, I'll open this one. Nobody knows what that means. We have the tablet and we have lost the laugh. And teasing is thirteen million years old — so what is written here is the last four thousand years of a joke we have been telling since before we were people.
ps: point (scribal centre; tablet provenance not established)

note: The oldest satirical cartoons — cats waiting on mice, the world turned upside down, drawn for a laugh by tomb-workmen ~3,000 years ago
Wild dolphins herd fish to the fishermen and signal the instant to cast — a cooperative hunt passed down on both sides for generations
A wild bird leads honey-hunters to the bees by call-and-response and takes the wax as its fee — a wild animal that talks with us on purpose
A pod of orcas drove whales into the bay for the whalers and took the tongue as its share — a cross-species pact, later broken

n: Door of No Return, Gorée Island
note: A threshold as a wound. Descendants return through it the other way — the crossing rewritten by the returning.
The night the cemetery becomes the living room — candles, marigolds, and the dead invited back to dinner annually.

n: Ryōzen-ji, Temple One of the Shikoku 88
note: The circle walked for twelve centuries. The pilgrim's book fills stamp by stamp — return as a ledger kept in ink.
Arrival as a literary genre — a thousand years of people walking a month to earn one embrace and one stamped page.
Carry a stone from home; set it on the pile; walk on lighter. Grief by subtraction, one pebble per pilgrim, for centuries.
Bulldozed by the regime; regrown by morning. Accretion as defiance — the crosses are the argument, and they multiplied.
Two hundred thousand graves in cedar dark, a millennium deep and still accepting applicants — memory grown into a forest.
The wall that receives written prayers into its cracks — a masonry mailbox to the absolute, emptied and refilled forever.
The poet whose funeral drew every faith in the city, still drawing them. 'Come, come, whoever you are' — and they do.
Iranians divine by opening his book at the graveside — a poet consulted as an oracle, seven centuries into the conversation.

n: The Long Room, Trinity College Dublin
note: The Book of Kells under glass — a manuscript monks carried through raids, now processed past in reverent single file.

n: Site of the Library of Alexandria
note: Sacred by loss — the burned library as civilization's memento mori, mourned continuously by people it never held.
Families hid the manuscripts through every conquest and produced them again — most recently by boat, in 2012, page by page.
The oldest continually operating library on Earth, reading quietly at the foot of the mountain since the sixth century.
Fifty thousand manuscripts sealed in a cave for nine centuries — the library that hid itself and waited to be read.
A church door used as a bulletin board, and the posting split a continent — the most consequential act of publication in history.
A field made sacred by parchment — the idea that a written sentence could bind a king, sealed on ordinary grass.
Where a nation's entire law was recited aloud from memory, yearly — a constitution kept in a voice before it was kept in ink.
A palace whose walls are literally verse — carved poems addressing the visitor in the first person. Architecture as manuscript.
Twelve centuries of visitors' poems scratched beside the paintings they admired — the oldest surviving comment section.
Ordinary voices preserved mid-sentence — the wall graffiti of lovers, gamblers, and complainers, outlasting the empire's poets.
Where Bashō wept at the summer grass and wrote the haiku that made the ruin permanent. The poem outlasted the warriors twice over.

n: The Wise Spider Old Tree, Brandon Country Park
note: An oak below the manor house, its arms let down to the ground as though to pick children up. Sacred by a child's decree and no other authority — which is the only way any grove has ever started.

n: Laguna Rosa, Las Salinas de Torrevieja
note: A lagoon turned rose by halophilic archaea and Dunaliella salina, worked for salt since the Iberians and by deed since 1321. The town sings habaneras its salt sailors carried back from Cuba; the sacredness is in the returning, not the brine.

A basalt dike held sacred by Cheyenne, Arapaho, and Lakota — ceremony, burial, and stone quarried for sweat lodges elsewhere. The militia that rode to Sand Creek mustered in its shadow. Contamination, not disbelief, is what finally emptied it.

The outcrop where Tenskwatawa sat and sang through the fighting of 7 November 1811, having told the warriors that the soldiers' bullets could not touch them. Prophetstown — the pan-tribal capital he and Tecumseh founded in 1808 as a religious act — burned the next day, a short walk downstream. People still climb to the rock.

Xuanzang stopped here on his way home and named the hill Little Lingshan, for the mountain he had seen in India. A Tang temple, rebuilt across dynasties; an 88 m bronze added in 1996. The same ground consecrated by three different Chinas.

The quarry of the gongshi — scholar's rocks the lake itself carves, hauled up and set in gardens for a thousand years and ranked like poems. A water venerated not for a shrine on its shore but for what it makes of stone.

A shallow pit on a sheltered eastern shore holding 15 kg of charred shell — some 30,000 to 40,000 hazelnuts, roasted c. 6000 BCE. An island with no large game, visited for its hazel woods. Whether one harvest or six returns across three millennia is disputed; either reading is a threshold.

The well at the world's end, in one telling: nine hazels leaning over it, dropping cno into the water for the salmon to eat, and cnocach — wisdom — one breath away in the mouth. Rags still tied to the branches. The Shannon runs 360 km from it.

Four hundred stalagmites broken, carried 336 m into the dark, and laid in two rings — with fire set on them. 176,500 years ago, which permits only Neanderthals. The oldest known construction, by a people who needed to make light to do it. Purpose unrecovered.

A 13 m shaft at the end of a cave holding at least 28 individuals, ~430,000 years old, and one pink quartzite handaxe of a kind found nowhere else here — named Excalibur. The best case anyone has for mortuary behaviour before our species existed.

The last address. Neanderthals held this sea-facing cave until roughly 32,000 years ago, after the rest of their world had closed. Someone cut a deep crosshatch into the bedrock — deliberate, non-utilitarian, and not ours.

Where a metre-tall human was found in 2003 and the family tree stopped being a ladder. Homo floresiensis kept this cave, and the Manggarai who keep it now ask visitors to sit through a welcome rite before entering. Two hominins, one doorway.

A chamber reachable only through a 17 cm squeeze, holding Homo naledi with a brain a third of ours. The excavators argue they buried their dead and marked the walls; the claim is disputed and unresolved. The bodies are there regardless, and nothing washed them in.

Biruté Galdikas landed here in 1971 and never left — the longest continuous study of any wild primate. Orangutans born under observation have brought their own grandchildren to the feeding platform. Attention, sustained until it becomes a relationship.

Dian Fossey's camp in the saddle between Karisimbi and Bisoke, 1967-1985. She is buried in the gorilla graveyard she dug for the ones the poachers took, next to Digit. The mountain gorilla is the only great ape whose numbers are rising.

The world's richest squirrel fauna. Eight genera of tree squirrel here, six of which never crossed the Isthmus of Kra — plus the smallest squirrel alive at 7 cm, and a ground squirrel with the bushiest tail of any mammal. An island that made a rodent into a bestiary.
ps: island

n: Central Kuroshio Current — octopus richness peak
note: Twenty-four octopus species, the highest count of any marine ecoregion — and pointedly NOT the Coral Triangle, where the cradle of marine life sits a thousand miles south. The octopuses went with the warm current instead. The obvious answer is the wrong one.
ps: marine ecoregion

note: Around 35,000 green islands, each a ring of forest a church would not let go, tended some 1,500 years. In South Gondar the arithmetic is total: 1,022 sacred groves, and they are one hundred per cent of the forest left. Everything unconsecrated was ploughed.
ps: zone (South Gondar)

note: Khasi law forest under the deity Labasa: nothing leaves, not a leaf, not a stone. The result is a dense pharmacopoeia standing at the edge of cleared hills — evidence that a prohibition can outperform a fence.

Fortified forest villages the Mijikenda abandoned as homes and kept as shrines, so the coastal forest survives only where the elders forbade the axe. The last stands of a habitat, held by a council rather than a statute.

The brightest bioluminescent bay measured anywhere: a narrow mouth traps dinoflagellates until a paddle stroke turns the water into a struck match. The trapping is the point — the glow needs the bay to stay almost closed.

Each spring, firefly squid rise from a deep submarine canyon to spawn and are washed ashore glowing blue by the million. Photophores over the whole body, in a bay shaped to deliver them to the beach. The nets go out at 3 a.m.

For two weeks a year Photinus carolinus flashes in unison across a Appalachian hollow — thousands of insects agreeing on a phase, for reasons still argued. One of very few synchronous firefly populations on Earth, and the crowd that comes to see it is now rationed by lottery.

The oldest forest known — 386 million years of it, reduced to root casts on a quarry floor. Here Archaeopteris put out woody roots eleven metres wide and began pulling carbon dioxide out of the air. Everything breathing now is downstream of that. Gilboa, 40 km west, is two million years younger.
ps: village-scale (quarry unpublished)

note: Classic ground, and the plaque says so in those words. Fourteen formations, sixty-three million years, Late Ordovician to Middle Devonian, in a completeness found nowhere else. Lyell came. Winifred Goldring — first woman to hold a state palaeontologist's post in this country — wrote the guide in 1933, and it is still the guide.

Corn Mountain. Named for nothing on its summit — named for the flats at its foot, where the Schoharie bottomland grew maize. The whole ridge was named: Rattlesnake Mountain across the water, Falling-Off where the Mohegans lived, Spring Hill above. A mountain that took its name from the foodshed it watches.

Pilgrimage for cannoli and cheesecake. Open on East 11th Street since 1894, through two world wars, the Depression, and every neighbourhood the block has been. The tin ceiling is original. People come from other boroughs and call it going home.

Where the Atlantic actually turns over. The textbook credits the Labrador Sea; direct measurement does not — the Labrador contributes some 3-4 Sv against roughly 15 Sv from the Irminger and Iceland basins, because Labrador water is too fresh to sink hard enough. Convection is not overturning. This is the water the world's heat rides on, and this is where it goes down.
ps: ocean basin

note: A cold tongue dragged up from the deep along one coast, feeding the largest single-species fishery ever recorded. A sliver of ocean, a rounding error of the surface, and it has fed more people than any other patch of water on Earth. El Nino switches it off, and everything above it starves.
ps: coastal upwelling zone

note: For much of the year the young Danube drains entirely into its own bed and vanishes. It re-emerges twelve kilometres south at the Aachtopf and leaves for the Rhine — so water that set out for the Black Sea reaches the North Sea instead. A continental divide being quietly stolen from underneath.
ps: river reach

note: The only sea with no shores — bounded by currents, floored by weed. Every American and European eel alive was born here and returns here to spawn, crossing an ocean to do it. No human has ever witnessed it. We know the nursery only because the larvae get smaller as you sail toward it.
ps: ocean gyre

note: Six hundred and fifty-five tree species in one hectare — more than the United States and Canada hold between them. The same hectare is projected to carry 100,000 insect species, the highest diversity per unit area measured for any group of life. Oil concessions cover the northern half.
ps: national park

note: One of the richest underground fungal communities mapped, and the map does not match the one above it: fungal richness runs largely uncoupled from plant richness, so the parks we drew for forests miss the fungi entirely. Under a tenth of the world's mycorrhizal hotspots have any protection at all.
ps: zone (Simien foothills)

note: A single honey fungus holding some 2,400 hectares of the Blue Mountains — the largest organism known, and arguably the oldest, at a few thousand years. It is almost entirely underground. It kills the trees it lives on. Nobody noticed it until they went looking for why the forest was dying.
ps: fungal individual, ~2,400 ha

note: Six ravens must be kept, or the Tower falls and the kingdom with it. So six are kept — fed, named, wing-trimmed, and pensioned. A superstition maintained as policy for long enough that it became one. The birds are the only reason this is not simply a castle.

An alerce in a Chilean ravine, modelled at some five thousand years — which would make it older than Methuselah, and the record still sits with Methuselah because the model has not been settled by a core. The tree is not waiting on the argument.

Eleven kilometres down, where the Pacific plate turns and goes back into the mantle. The deepest place, and also the drain — the one edge of the world where the surface is destroyed rather than made. More people have stood on the Moon than at the bottom of it.

A mountainside at 1,500 m in the Lungau, terraced into seventy ponds and raised beds, growing citrus and cherries where the textbook says only spruce. One family, half a century, no ploughing and no purchased input — a hill argued into fertility rather than forced. The authorities fined him for it repeatedly.

n: Subak of Bali (Pura Ulun Danu Batur)
note: Water temples have scheduled the rice here for a thousand years, staggering fallow across the terraces by ritual calendar. In the 1970s planners overrode it with continuous cropping; pests and water shortage followed; modelling later showed the priests' schedule had been close to optimal for both. The farmers went back to the temples. A liturgy that was also an algorithm.
ps: basin-wide subak network

note: Fields built out of the lake itself — mud and weed piled on staked wicker until the plot floats, roots let down into water that never runs dry. Among the highest yields any pre-industrial agriculture reached, and some of them are still in production six centuries on. The city drained the lake around them.

A farmer in Burkina Faso revived zai — pits dug in hardpan, filled with manure, which draw termites, whose tunnels open the crust to water. Neighbours thought him mad and burned part of it. He grew roughly forty hectares of forest on ground the Sahel had written off, and the method spread across the region.
ps: smallholding + regrown forest

note: Two thousand years of hand-cut terracing up an Ifugao mountainside, watered off the forest the same families keep above them — the woodlot is part of the machine. Maintained without mortar, entirely by the households that eat from them. Nothing here works if anyone stops.

North America's largest winter roost of crows, in a town of 33,000 that has been outnumbered four to one. A mayor fitted pickup trucks with cannons; the crows learned the trucks and left before the shots came, then returned. Counts run from 100,000 to well over 200,000.

Forty thousand crows winter here, down from seventy-five. In 2004 a bar sponsored a shoot and 150 hunters killed 340 birds in a weekend. In 2026 the same town held its first Crow Cawcus — masks, lectures, artists. Twenty years to decide the thing you cannot get rid of might be the thing you are known for.

n: Sierra Madre del Sur — the jay radiation
note: Seven genera of New World jay, and the thickest of them here: the cloud forest holds the Dwarf Jay at forty-one grams — the smallest corvid alive — and the White-throated Jay, and neither lives anywhere else. The family that gave us tool-users and funeral-keepers spent its American youth being small, blue, and Mexican.
ps: zone (cloud-forest belt)

note: The Maleku hold this mountain sacred, and did before the Spanish came, when their country ran from Rincon de la Vieja to here and Arenal and Rio Celeste were both holy ground. That country is now eleven and a half square miles, of which they own about fifteen per cent, and the volcano stands seventy kilometres outside it. Six hundred people. The sacredness did not lapse. The access did.

Hamaka: Taino, for a fish net. Columbus wrote the word down in October 1492 — cotton nets the people slept in, slung off the ground and away from what crawls. The word outlived the people who coined it. It is in every European language now, naturalised into German as Haengematte, a hanging mat, as though it had always been theirs.
ps: zone (Taino Antilles)

note: The only people known entirely from somebody else's blood. Denisovan ancestry runs highest in the living population of this island — a people who crossed deep water with strong currents to reach it, and left not one bone, not one tooth, not one tool that anyone can name. The fossil record for them here is blank. They exist because their descendants do.
ps: zone (island)

note: Jane Goodall pitched a tent here in 1960 and watched until the watching changed the definition. Termite fishing, in the first months — a chimpanzee stripping a twig to shape, which was the thing only we were supposed to do. The longest continuous study of any wild animal, and it has never stopped running.

Three figures and a pig, fifty-one thousand two hundred years ago — the oldest picture anyone has securely dated, and the oldest story. One of the figures reaches out and touches the animal's head. Nothing in Europe's ice-age caves is remotely this old. We were telling each other things on walls before we reached the places we thought we invented it.

Hammerstones on a rainforest floodplain, four thousand three hundred years old, and no human made them. The starch still on them is from nuts only chimpanzees eat; the people here lived on tubers and left none of a forager's kit. Two hundred generations of apes cracking Panda nuts in the same wood, and they are still doing it upstream.

n: Serra da Capivara — the capuchin sequence
note: Bearded capuchins crack cashews here with quartz cobbles, and under them lies three thousand years of the discarded ones — four hundred and fifty generations, the oldest non-human archaeology outside Africa. The tools change through the layers. Until about five centuries ago they were small and battered from missed strikes; then the monkeys were eating something else, and picked up something else. A material culture with a history, and not one of them ours.
ps: zone (national park)

note: The oldest crowd. Five hundred and sixty-five million years ago the seafloor here was covered in fronds that could not move, and the ash came down and held them exactly where they stood — hundreds to a slab, still in their arrangement. Three billion years of life too small to see, and then this: things with a shape, standing in a community. Nothing older has ever been found that was big enough to leave a body.

Boiled with the heat of the sun. Six kilometres of Amazon river running at ninety-five degrees, hot enough that a frog falling in is cooked before it can turn round, and the nearest volcano is seven hundred kilometres away. The Asháninka have always said the water is birthed by Yacumama, the serpent that mothers the waters; a boulder shaped like a snake's head marks the headwaters. The geologists said a boiling river without a volcano was a story. The geologists had not been.

n: Wadi Sura — the Cave of Swimmers
note: Small figures with their heads up and their legs trailing, painted on a rock face that now sees five millimetres of rain a year. Giraffe and hippopotamus on the same wall. Eight thousand years ago there was water here to do that in, and lakes two hundred kilometres south, and then the rains moved and the people went with them. The Gilf Kebir is called the Great Barrier now. It was not a barrier when this was painted.

n: Pueblito de Allende — melilite, and time zero
note: A rock came apart over a Mexican village in February 1969 and salted the desert with grains older than the Earth. Inside it, white specks of melilite and its companions: the first things that ever cooled hard out of the cloud, four billion five hundred and sixty-seven million years ago. Everything has an age measured from them. It fell five months before the Moon rocks came back, into a world that had just finished building the laboratories.
ps: point (village; the strewn field extends beyond it)

n: The Egyptian orange — cats as a shipping record
note: You can read the shipping lanes off the cats. The orange allele traces back to Egypt and spread along the Mediterranean ports — Greece, Italy, Spain, France — carried on the same hulls as the grain, because a ship that carries grain must carry something that eats mice. Haldane saw in the 1940s that the colours were a record. Todd went and counted them.
ps: zone (Nile Delta / eastern Mediterranean origin per Todd)

note: A hole three metres by four in a Caucasian pass, and beneath it the deepest place a human being has ever stood. Which of these two caves is deeper is not settled: they are four kilometres apart, and the gap between their claimed floors is smaller than the error bar on measuring either. In 2018 a flood came up the shaft behind a team and they climbed two kilometres ahead of it.
ps: point (entrance, 2,285 m asl)

note: By 1975 no living Hawaiian knew how to cross open ocean without instruments. The knowledge was still alive on a coral island of six hundred people — kept there partly because nobody had ever wanted the island enough to take it. Mau Piailug was chosen at one year old, put in tide pools to learn the feel of wind and the taste of salt, and was at sea by five. Six men in the Pacific still had this. Only he would give it away.
ps: point (island, ~600 people)

note: A man died in a gully at three thousand two hundred metres with an arrow in his back, and the ice kept him for five thousand three hundred years. We read his genome in 2012 and gave him steppe ancestors and a lighter face. We read it again in 2023, properly, and he lost both. He was dark-skinned, going bald, and almost entirely descended from Anatolian farmers. He looked, it turns out, like the mummy does.
ps: point (~3,210 m, Ötztal Alps)

note: Fifty-three per cent of every kind of whale, dolphin and porpoise on Earth uses this one country's water. And the shape of it is backwards from what you would guess: marine mammals do not crowd the tropics. They thin out at the poles, thin out at the equator, and pile up in the cold-temperate middle — which is exactly where these islands sit.
ps: zone (New Zealand EEZ)

note: Georg Steller was shipwrecked here in 1741 and described an eight-metre animal that ate kelp and could not submerge, floating in the shallows in herds. He was the only scientist who ever saw one. It was gone in twenty-seven years — the fastest a large mammal has ever been finished. Russia banned the hunting in 1755 because it was already rare. Nobody enforced it. And the genome says the dying had started before there were any people in the Pacific at all: we did not begin this. We only closed it.
ps: point (Commander Islands)

note: Seven hundred and sixty-seven animals in a thousand square kilometres of hill forest, and they are the whole species. Nobody knew orangutans lived south of Lake Toba until 1997, and nobody knew these were their own kind until 2017 — the first great ape described in ninety years, discovered already at the edge. Three thousand hours of watching and no one has seen one put a foot on the ground. There are tigers underneath.
ps: zone (~1,023 km² upland forest, three blocks)

note: The Dayak say people learned medicine from the orangutans — how to help at a birth, which leaves to use afterward. The Iban tell of a man who watched a male orangutan fetch ginger for a female in labour. In June 2022 a flanged male called Rakus, cut under the eye in a fight, stripped leaves from a liana that no orangutan here eats, chewed them without swallowing, pressed the juice into the wound with his fingers, and laid the pulp over it like a dressing. It closed in five days and never turned. The story was a hundred years older than the film of it.
ps: point (research site, ~150 orangutans)

note: Two thousand square kilometres of Sarawak forest holding some two and a half thousand orangutans, and what protects them is not the law. It is a prohibition older than the Iban have been here — they arrived two centuries ago and found the taboo waiting. A man will tell you his grandfather became an orangutan, and that for years afterward people saw one in the forest wearing the same shoulder bag. A hunter here, who hunts: for us they are a sacred animal. I never heard of anyone in this area who could do that.
ps: zone (>2,000 km², two adjoining reserves)

note: Every Ngaju village was to keep one patch of forest that could never be cleared. You know it by the big trees, the water, and a small house left by the ancestors that the spirits are said to live in. Go in without the rite and it will cost you — so nobody goes in, and the animals that feed there are not troubled. A prohibition that works as a fence because nobody thought of it as one.
ps: class (many groves across Central Kalimantan, not one site)

note: Pongo pygmaeus wurmbii. The largest population left of any orangutan, and it lives in a swamp — peat forest nobody wanted until they did. Drain it and the ground itself burns.
ps: zone (peat-swamp forest)

note: Pongo pygmaeus wurmbii. A park that runs from mangrove up to mountain, which sounds like an orangutan's whole world and is not: they mostly stay below five hundred metres and the highest one ever recorded was at fifteen hundred. Most of this park is above them. They live in the bottom of it.
ps: zone (national park)

note: Pongo pygmaeus morio. The dry-forest orangutans — the subspecies that learned to live where the fruit fails, with the heaviest jaws and the thinnest margin.
ps: zone (conservation area)

note: Pongo pygmaeus morio. A river with oil palm on both banks and the forest reduced to a ribbon. The orangutans are still there, in a corridor the width of the flood.
ps: zone (floodplain corridor)

note: Pongo pygmaeus pygmaeus. The rarest of the three Bornean kinds, in the island's northwest corner — the ones that never crossed the Kapuas.
ps: zone (transboundary highlands + lakes)

note: Pongo abelii. The last place on Earth where orangutan, tiger, rhino and elephant still share one forest — two and a half million hectares, three Yellowstones. And it is not only theirs: the water that comes off this forest is the drinking water and the farm water for four million people. The argument for keeping it does not need the orangutans at all. It has them anyway.
ps: zone (ecosystem, Aceh/N Sumatra)

note: Gorilla beringei beringei. Eight volcanoes across three borders, and one gorilla population that has never heard of any of them. The only gorilla whose numbers are going up — Virunga rose fourteen per cent in twelve years, which for a great ape is nearly unheard of.
ps: zone (three-country volcano chain)

note: Gorilla beringei beringei. The other half of the mountain gorillas — a separate forest, a separate population, cut off from the Virungas by farmland they cannot cross. Long enough apart that there is a serious argument they should be counted as their own kind entirely.
ps: zone (national park)

note: Gorilla beringei graueri. Grauer's gorilla, the largest primate alive, in the forest that sits on the coltan. The war never quite left.
ps: zone (national park)

note: Gorilla gorilla diehli. The rarest gorilla on Earth — perhaps two hundred, perhaps three — scattered across a border in a dozen fragments that barely touch. So wary of people that almost everything known about them comes from counting nests. And they are the ones that fight back: gorillas met by humans flee, as a rule. These throw sticks.
ps: zone (Nigeria/Cameroon highlands)

note: Gorilla gorilla gorilla. Forest around the lagoons and river-mouths, where a research team spent years persuading one family — the Atananga — to tolerate being watched, because almost nothing was known. Western gorillas turn out to live very differently from the mountain ones. The famous things on the beach here are the elephants, and the hippos, who surf.
ps: zone (national park, Atlantic coast)

note: Habituating a western lowland gorilla takes three years or more: the forest is dark, the leaf litter holds no tracks, and the animals walk a long way for fruit. It was done here, and everyone who built the programme says the same thing about why — the Ba'Aka trackers. Their knowledge is the reason anybody has ever watched these gorillas at all.
ps: zone (tri-national forest)

note: Pan paniscus. The longest watch on bonobos anywhere — a Japanese primatologist walked in in 1973 and the camp has never really closed, through two wars. And the people here do not hunt them, and never did, because bonobos are held to be kin — ancestors who went into the forest. They call them the people of the forest. So does Borneo, about a different ape, ten thousand kilometres away, in another language, with no possible way of having conferred.
ps: zone (Luo Scientific Reserve)

note: Pan paniscus. Not where bonobo science started — that was Wamba — but where it was tested: the forest where somebody put bonobos and chimpanzees side by side and asked what the difference actually was. Then the wars came in 1996 and again in 1998 and the watching stopped. The reserve was drawn in 2006, and the work resumed, but not on the same animals. Nobody knows those individuals now.
ps: zone (forest reserve)

note: Pan paniscus. The largest protected rainforest in Africa, and almost nobody has walked most of it. Whatever the bonobos are doing in the middle of it, they are doing unobserved.
ps: zone (national park)

note: Pan paniscus. A reserve the villages themselves drew and hold — conservation as a title deed rather than a fence.
ps: zone (community reserve)

note: Pan paniscus. Bonobos hunting monkeys, sharing meat, and meeting neighbouring groups without anyone dying — which chimpanzees do not do.
ps: zone (research site, Salonga margin)

note: Pan troglodytes verus. Where the nut-cracking is taught mother to child, and the archaeology under the anvils runs back four thousand years. The chimpanzees here have a stone age of their own.
ps: zone (national park)

note: A handful of chimpanzees in a hill forest ringed by farms, cracking nuts with stones at the edge of the village, with snare hunting going on all around them — and almost none of them ever gets caught. They break the snares. Five different males have been seen doing it, juveniles to adults, which means somebody worked it out and the rest learned it off him.
ps: point (village forest, Guinea)

note: Savanna chimpanzees who snap a branch, strip it, gnaw the end to a point, and stab it into hollows where bushbabies sleep. Three hundred hunts recorded and the females did a hundred and seventy-five of them. They are the only animals besides us that hunt with tools as a habit. And it may only work here because the males of this group let the females keep what they kill.
ps: zone (savanna mosaic, Senegal)

note: Pan troglodytes ellioti. The subspecies almost nobody has managed to study, because almost none of them will tolerate being watched. They eat army ants more than any chimpanzees on record — all year, steadily, and not as famine food but because they prefer them. Their tools run longer in the dry season, when the insects retreat deeper.
ps: zone (national park, Nigeria)

note: Chimpanzees who had never met a human being and did not run. They came down to look, and then more of them arrived to look, and the average encounter lasted two hours and sixteen minutes. Nobody had ever taught them to be afraid. Their curiosity is what got the forest protected — the researchers took it to the people who draw the boundaries and said: look what they do.
ps: zone (Nouabalé-Ndoki, Congo)

note: A forest logged for a century with the chimpanzees still in it, and a third of the adults maimed by wire set for pigs. One four-man team was pulling two hundred snares a month out of the ground. The chimps take them off each other. They also press each other's wounds, and chew leaves and lay the pulp on someone else's cut — not their own. And when one male's hands were destroyed and he invented a way to groom himself without them, the able-bodied ones copied it.
ps: zone (forest reserve, Uganda)

note: Manhattan-sized, charted since 1876, on Google Earth, in the world's coastline database, on the US military's maps. On 22 November 2012 a research ship sailed carefully up to it and found fourteen hundred metres of open water. The crew had a good giggle at Google as they went straight through the island. The geologists published an obituary for it — Sandy Island, 1876 to 2012 — in a journal of the American Geophysical Union, which is the only funeral a place has ever had for never existing.
ps: point (a place that is not there; ~1,400 m of water)

note: A town in Lancashire with a doctor's surgery, a golf club and a hairdresser, which was a field with a footpath across it. Google Maps had it from 2008. Businesses were listed there. Jobs were advertised there. The pub in the next village sold Argleton Ale and Argleton Pie, the ingredients of which were given as: nothing at all. It was marked Closed before it was deleted, which is a kinder end than most places get.
ps: point (a field)

note: Thirty-two centimetres of bronze with the sun, the moon and the Pleiades laid into it in gold — the oldest picture anyone made of the actual sky rather than the gods in it. Two looters dug it out of this hill in 1999 and sold it on; the police got it back three years later in a sting. And here is the thing nobody can get around: copper alloy cannot be dated. The disc has no age of its own. Everything we say about when it was made rests on where those two men said they found it.
ps: point (hilltop find site)

n: Madaba — the map that still works
note: A church floor in Jordan with a million stones in it, laid in the sixth century, showing a hundred and fifty towns from Lebanon to the Nile. It is oriented east, toward the altar, so the map agrees with the compass. And it is still right. It showed a colonnaded street in Jerusalem, and they dug and found it. It showed a church, and they dug and found that. In 2025 they went looking for a town because this floor said it was there, and it was. Fourteen hundred years old and still finding things.
ps: point (floor of St George's, Madaba)

note: The largest medieval map of the world that survives, and it will not tell you how to get anywhere. Jerusalem sits at the middle because that is where the middle was; east is at the top because that is where the story goes. Heaven, hell, the monstrous races, the road to salvation. It is not a bad map. It is a map of a different question — and it answers that one exactly.
ps: point (Hereford Cathedral)

note: Five tons of granite, five sides, seven hundred and sixty runes — the longest thing anyone carved in runes, and the first thing anyone wrote in Sweden. A minor king called Varin cut it around the year 800 for his son Vämod, who died young. Nine riddles. Five of them are about the sun. Three centuries earlier the sky had gone dark and half of Scandinavia had starved, and in Varin's own lifetime the sky turned red again and the summer failed and the sun went out at midday. A father buried his boy and carved, in the hardest form he had, the question of whether the light comes back.
ps: point (Rök parish, Östergötland)

note: One family's graveyard, and they have been putting people in it for two and a half thousand years. A hundred thousand graves. Seventy generations, from the man himself to descendants buried in living memory, all in one walled forest of cypress and stone. The house he died in was made a temple the following year and has been rebuilt ever since. No other family anywhere has kept a place this long.
ps: zone (183 ha, 5.6 km perimeter wall)

note: Around the year 1010 a young monk read the story of Daedalus, decided it was true, tied wings to his hands and his feet, and stepped off this tower. He flew more than two hundred metres — about fifteen seconds — and came down hard enough to break both legs, and he limped for the rest of his life. His chronicler says he mistook a fable for the truth. But Eilmer did not conclude the story was false. He concluded he had forgotten the tail. He was right about that, and he wanted to go again, and the abbot forbade it. Nine hundred years before anyone else got off the ground.
ps: point (abbey tower and landing ground)

note: The flowers on this coast make the honey poisonous, and the people here have known it for two thousand four hundred years. Xenophon's ten thousand ate it by accident in 401 BC and spent a night vomiting and unable to stand — every one of them recovered, bewildered. Three centuries later the locals set jars of it out on a mountain road for three of Pompey's cohorts, and killed them where they fell. It is still sold in the markets at Rize. A tablespoon is a dose.
ps: zone (eastern Black Sea coast: Rize, Artvin, Trabzon)

note: A rum-runner froze his toe on a dogsled run in the twenties and his brother took it off with an axe, and they kept it in a jar because it seemed a shame not to. Fifty years later a riverboat captain found the jar in the empty cabin and had an idea. You can drink it fast, you can drink it slow, but your lips have gotta touch the toe. A hundred and five thousand people have done it. The man who started it left the bar all ten of his own in his will — he didn't care about the money, his daughter said, he just wanted the fame to go on.
ps: point (Downtown Hotel, Dawson City)

note: For a thousand years, if you wanted to say someone was an idiot, you said he was from here. Cicero wrote of the Roman senate that it's all Abdera in here. Martial used the name to mean stupid. The oldest joke book on Earth is stuffed with Abderites who cannot work out what a eunuch is. And the town's most famous son is Democritus — the philosopher antiquity remembered as the one who could not stop laughing. Nobody knows which way round it went.
ps: point (Abdera, Thrace)

n: Max Planck Institute of Animal Behaviour
note: The lab that found out apes do medicine and comedy. One researcher here watched an orangutan chew a liana no orangutan eats and press it into a wound on his own face, and it healed. The same researcher watched all four kinds of great ape wind each other up on purpose — poke, snatch, stare, and do it again to get the reaction — the way a toddler does before it can talk. Which puts the joke at thirteen million years old, and us in the audience for most of it.
ps: point (institute; specific study sites not established here)

note: A third of every oak on Earth is Mexican, and two-thirds of those are Mexican and nowhere else. Red oaks and white oaks arrived here separately, twenty-five million years ago, climbed into the mountains, and — over twenty million years of never once exchanging a gene — independently turned into the same thing. Mexico put an oak branch on its own seal. It is one of the two crossed at the base.
ps: zone (montane oak belt, Sierra Madre Oriental)

n: Shillourokambos — the cat in the grave
note: Nine and a half thousand years ago somebody on this island was buried with an axe, a black stone pendant, shells, blades, ochre — and a cat. Not a cat skeleton in a grain store. A cat laid in a grave, on purpose, as a grave good, by people who had carried its ancestors here on a boat, because Cyprus never had cats until somebody brought them. The earliest proof we loved them is a funeral.
ps: point (Neolithic village, Cyprus)

note: Every humpless cow on Earth may descend from about eighty wild females, taken in one or two villages on the Euphrates, ten and a half thousand years ago. Not a slow drift across a continent — a small thing that happened in a small place and then went everywhere. The animal they took them from is gone: the last aurochs died in a Polish forest in 1627, and nobody has seen the ancestor since.
ps: point (Neolithic village, Euphrates; the domestication locus is 'one or two villages such as Dja'de')

note: The humped cattle were never the same animal. Their wild ancestors split from the humpless ones somewhere between two and three hundred thousand years ago — long before anybody thought of a fence. People tamed them here, on their own, two and a half thousand years after the Euphrates did it, and three thousand kilometres away. Then four thousand years ago the two lineages met around this valley and started mixing, and they have been mixing ever since.
ps: zone (Indus Valley; no single site established here)

note: The same story as the cattle, told again. The boars of the west and the boars of the east had been separate animals for half a million years before anybody built a pen. People here took one, and people in China took the other, a thousand miles and two thousand years apart, and neither knew. Then in the eighteenth century English breeders shipped Chinese pigs home to improve the stock, and put the two halves back together after five hundred thousand years. Some of the great English breeds are hybrids and nobody told them.
ps: zone (eastern Anatolia / northern Mesopotamia)

note: The animal that every cow on Earth came from died here in 1627. Not a species fading out unwitnessed somewhere — a named forest, a known year, the last one, a female. Polish kings had protected the herd for a century and set gamekeepers over it and fed it hay in hard winters, and it died anyway. It is one of the first extinctions anybody watched happen and wrote down. Two hundred years before the word existed.
ps: point (Jaktorów Forest, Mazovia)

n: Spencer Gulf — the country the sea took
note: Around almost the entire coast of Australia, people tell of a time when the sea came in over country that had been walked on and hunted across and named, and did not go back. Work out how much water you would have to take away for each story to be literally true, then ask the sea-level curve when the coast last looked like that. It answers: seven thousand to thirteen thousand years ago. This gulf was dry land and somebody remembers. Four hundred generations, and nobody wrote any of it down.
ps: zone (Spencer Gulf; stands for 21 sites around the whole Australian coast)

n: The Cascadia rupture — nine at night
note: Nobody on this coast wrote it down. But Japan has been logging disasters since about the year 600, and one night a sixteen-foot wave came ashore on Honshu with no earthquake under it — an orphan tsunami, they called it, a wave with no parent. Three hundred years later somebody worked backwards from the Japanese calendar and the tree rings and found the parent: nine o'clock at night, the twenty-sixth of January, 1700, a thousand kilometres of seafloor moving twenty metres. And the people here had been saying so the whole time. Thunderbird fought Whale. The ocean went out and came back. There were canoes in the trees.
ps: zone (1,000 km subduction zone, Vancouver Island to Cape Mendocino)

note: Llao, chief of the Below World, stood on Mazama and threw fire. Skell, chief of the Above World, stood on Shasta a hundred miles off and threw it back. Burning ashes fell like rain. Like an ocean of flame it devoured the forests. Fleeing in terror before it, the people found refuge in the waters of Klamath Lake. Then the mountain fell in on Llao and the high mountain was gone, and for many years rain fell in torrents and filled the great hole. There is a hole. It filled. It is the deepest lake in America, and the Klamath were here to watch it happen.
ps: point (caldera, Mount Mazama)

n: Razboinichya — the dog that led nowhere
note: Thirty-three thousand years ago somebody in these mountains had a dog. Three laboratories dated the skull separately and agreed. It is not a wolf — it looks like a dog from Greenland. And it is the ancestor of nothing: no living dog descends from it, and no dog descends from the one in Belgium either. Somebody tamed a wolf, twice, on opposite sides of a continent, and then the ice came and took both. We did this before and lost it.
ps: point (cave, Altai; coordinate interim)

note: There is a scruffy plant on the sea cliffs that is broccoli. It is also cabbage, and cauliflower, and kale, and kohlrabi, and Brussels sprouts, and collards — one species, and somebody bred a different vegetable out of every part of it: the leaves, the stem, the buds, the flower head, the terminal bud. Then three of these wild plants crossed with each other by themselves, in pairs, and made three more crops. Six species from three. A whole aisle of the supermarket is one weed off a cliff and its relatives, doing arithmetic.
ps: zone (limestone sea cliffs, European Atlantic and Mediterranean coasts; origin contested)

note: Four in five maples are Asian, and China holds about a hundred of them — more than anywhere on Earth — with the peak in this province. Nearly a quarter of China's maples are threatened. One of them, the only member of its whole branch, lives in a single hot dry river valley in Sichuan; it was written off as extinct for fifty years and turned up again in 1982. The richest place for a tree is also where the tree is running out.
ps: zone (Yunnan; richness peaks W/NW/SE Yunnan and SE Tibet)

note: Every forty-eight years the bamboo flowers. All of it, at once, across the whole state, and then it dies. The seed is enormous and the rats eat it and breed until there is no seed left, and then they come out of the forest and eat the villages. Eighteen fifteen. Eighteen sixty-three. Nineteen eleven. Nineteen fifty-nine. Two thousand and seven. You could set a watch by it, and the people here did — and when they said so in 1959 the government told them it was superstition. The relief committee they formed instead became a party, and then the party became a war that lasted twenty years.
ps: zone (Mizoram; the phenomenon extends into Manipur, Tripura, Assam and Chin State, Myanmar)

note: The carrot is eleven hundred years old. Wheat is ten and a half thousand. Cattle the same. The carrot was pulled out of the ground for the first time in about the tenth century, here — younger than the Norse in Iceland, younger than the Book of Kells. And it was purple. Before that nobody ate the root at all: it is thin and bitter and woody, and people grew the plant for its leaves and its seeds, as a herb. Someone quite recently decided the wrong end was the food.
ps: zone (Central Asia: Afghanistan / Iran)

n: Southern Bolivia — the peanut that a person made
note: Every peanut on Earth exists because somebody carried a plant. Nine thousand four hundred years ago a person picked up one wild peanut species and walked it out of its own country into the range of another, and the two crossed. The hybrid should have been sterile. Its chromosomes doubled by accident and it wasn't. That fluke is the whole crop — forty-six million tonnes a year, and in Africa and Asia more of it than any other legume on the planet. The plant that got carried is still out there. One population. Ninety-nine point nine eight per cent identical to the half of the peanut it became.
ps: zone (southern Bolivian valleys; the surviving wild B-donor population is a single accession)

note: An amateur found a skull in this cliff the size of a cow's. It is a rodent. South America was an island and the rodents here grew to the size of bison — and then Panama closed, and the cats and the bears and the hoofed things walked down from the north, and the giants went. The capybara is not the biggest rodent. It is the last one. And it still eats what its enormous cousin ate: leaves, not grass, even when the grass is right there.
ps: point (Barrancas de San Gregorio, Kiyú beach, Uruguay)

n: The Fayum — where the elephants started
note: There were more than a hundred and sixty kinds. Shovel-tuskers with spades for a lower jaw. Deinotheres whose tusks curved down out of the chin. Things of sixteen tonnes, heavier than any land mammal that has ever lived. On every continent but two. It began here, with a tapir-sized animal in a swamp that had not got round to growing a trunk yet. There are three left, and the last of the old ones died on an island in the Bering Strait while the pyramids were already standing.
ps: zone (Fayum Depression, Egypt)

note: A million pictures pecked through the red skin of the rock to the grey underneath, over fifty thousand years — the oldest gallery there is. Thylacines. A fat-tailed kangaroo. The oldest human face anyone ever drew. The work stopped in 1868 when settlers killed the Yaburara. Now the rock is dissolving, because the gas plant next door turns the rain to acid. Woodside runs it and has leave to keep running it to the 2070s. Yara, which is part-owned by Norway, makes ammonia on the other side. Perdaman is building urea and moving three rock art sites to do it. The traditional owners' own management plan says they did not want to sign and were told it was going ahead regardless.
ps: zone (~100,000 ha: Burrup Peninsula + 42 islands of the Dampier Archipelago)

note: Forty thousand years ago somebody sat down with a flint knife and a piece of mammoth tusk and carved a thing that has never existed: a person with the head of a lion. It is the oldest statue on Earth and it is a picture of something imagined. They found it in pieces on the last day of the dig, a few weeks before the war started, and the war stopped the excavation. The fragments sat in a museum in a city that was bombed flat, and survived. Thirty years passed before anyone realised they were a figure. Another twenty before the head was put back on the body. They were still finding bits of it in 2008. It took seventy years to see what it was.
ps: point (Stadel cave, Lone valley, Swabian Jura)

note: A man on a train to Bhopal in 1957 looked out of the window at some rocks and recognised them, because he had been to Spain. Seven hills, seven hundred and fifty shelters, and people living in them from the old stone age to the middle ages — the same address for a hundred thousand years. At the back of the big one, past where the light goes, somebody pecked rows of little cups into the rock. They are among the oldest marks anybody ever made on purpose. The local people always knew the place was there. The record called it Buddhist and moved on.
ps: zone (seven hills, ~10 km, 750+ shelters)

note: Her father was looking at the floor for stone tools. She was eight and she looked up. Mira, Papá — bulls. He knew what they were because he had been to an exhibition in Paris the year before, and he published, and the congress in Lisbon laughed at him and called him a forger. The reason nobody believed him is the reason it was there at all: a rockfall sealed the cave thirteen thousand years ago and the paint was too good. He died with his name still bad. Fourteen years later the man who led the ridicule published an article called A Sceptic's Mea Culpa.
ps: point (cave, Santillana del Mar, Cantabria)

n: Valcamonica — eight thousand years of the same rocks
note: The glaciers came through and polished the sandstone flat, and then people carved on it for eight thousand years and never stopped — through the stone age, the bronze age, the iron age, the Romans, and on into the Middle Ages. Hunting, boats, duels, magic, ploughed fields. And a map: paths and plots and villages, cut into a rock three thousand years before anyone drew Jerusalem. The commonest sign of all is a flower nobody can read. Lombardy put it on the flag.
ps: zone (valley, 75-90 km; carvings on ~2,400 rocks)

note: They carved boats. Ten thousand boats, on rock that was the edge of the water when they cut it and is now twenty-five metres up a hill, because the land here is still rising out of the ice. And that is how we date them: a carving cannot be older than the day its rock came out of the sea, because nobody carves underwater. The shoreline leaving is the clock. The first man to write them down, in 1627, decided bored medieval stonemasons had done it.
ps: zone (~41-45 km²; 600+ panels along a 25 km stretch of former shoreline)

n: Mal'ta — the spiral, and the twenty thousand years
note: Somebody cut a spiral into a piece of mammoth ivory here, eighteen thousand years ago. And then almost nobody drew one for twenty thousand years — two caves in France and this, in the whole Ice Age. Then the Neolithic arrives and suddenly the spiral is everywhere: Newgrange, Brittany, Malta, Sardinia, Naxos, cut into every tomb door in Atlantic Europe. Why did a shape that people barely used for two hundred centuries become the most important sign anyone had? Nobody knows. That is the honest answer and it is worth writing down.
ps: point (Mal'ta settlement, Irkutsk Oblast, Siberia)

note: Four hundred thousand years ago, on this river, somebody picked up a freshwater mussel and cut a zigzag into it. Not a person — Homo erectus. Three hundred thousand years before our species existed at all, something that was not us made a mark that was not for anything. It is the oldest deliberate pattern anyone has ever found, and it is three times older than the next one.
ps: point (Trinil, Solo River, Java; single find)

n: Esteros del Iberá — the bright water
note: The Guaraní called this place ý berá — bright water — and they called the animal that lives in it kapiÿva, master of the grasses. Fifteen thousand square kilometres of swamp and lagoon and floating island, and the capybaras sit in it in their dozens with birds on their backs. Every country in their range has its own word for them: carpincho, chigüire, ronsoco, ponche, jochi, piro-piro, capivara. You do not get seven names for an animal you do not live with. And when the Church found people here with nothing to eat in Lent, it looked at a rodent in a swamp and ruled that it was a fish.
ps: zone (15,000-20,000 km² wetland, Corrientes, Argentina)

n: Three Sisters Springs — where the mermaids go in winter
note: Below sixty-eight degrees a manatee starts to die. This spring sits at seventy-two, all year, forever, fed by twenty vents in the floor of a river seven miles long that has eighty springs in it. So every winter they come — a thousand of them into the river, five hundred and twenty-eight into this one pool on the worst night of 2014, lying on the bottom like flooded boulders waiting for Florida to warm up. The first European to write them down was Columbus, in January 1493. He thought they were mermaids and he was disappointed: not half as beautiful as they are painted. And in 2011 somebody bought the field above the spring, because it was zoned for four hundred houses.
ps: point (spring, ~57-60 acres, Crystal River NWR, Florida)

n: Kitum Cave — the mine the elephants dug
note: There is no salt in the forest, so at night the elephants walk two hundred metres into the side of a volcano, in total darkness, bumping their heads on the roof, and quarry the wall with their tusks. They have done it for thousands of years and they bring the calves and show them how. When people first found the furrows on the walls they decided ancient Egyptians had been in here with picks, hunting for gold. It was elephants. And somewhere in the dark there is a crack in the floor that the young ones fall into, so at the back of the cave there is an elephant graveyard, which is not a legend — it is an accident that keeps happening.
ps: point (cave, ~200 m deep, Mount Elgon NP, Kenya)

note: A woman left her job at Newsweek in 1968 and went to look at elephants, and picked this park for one reason: here you can see them. She started photographing their ears so she could tell them apart. When the money ran out in 1974 her colleague left and she stayed. Fifty years on, this is the longest anyone has ever watched any land mammal, and thousands of elephants here are known by name across four generations. And what she found is this: the family's memory lives in its oldest female. Where the water is in a drought. Which roar means run. So the biggest ones are not breeding stock. They are the library, and the poachers shoot the library first.
ps: zone (national park, under Kilimanjaro)

note: Turn the fronds over and they catch the moon. That is how you got home at night — you walked into the bush and you flipped the leaves silver side up behind you, and the path lit itself. The new frond comes up coiled and it is called the koru, which means loop, and it is carved on the meeting houses and cut into skin and painted on the rafters and printed on the shirts and on the tails of the aeroplanes. Its shape means perpetual movement; its inner coil means return to where you started. A whole country wears a fern. And the old stories say the fern used to live in the sea.
ps: zone (endemic range of Cyathea dealbata: North and South Islands, offshore islands, the Chathams)

note: The volcano went off. Nobody argues about that — it buried a town whole, and the wave reached Israel. What nobody can do is say when. Radiocarbon says sixteen hundred BC. The Egyptologists say fifteen hundred. A century apart, both disciplines certain, and it matters more than almost any date in archaeology because the whole Bronze Age calendar of the eastern Mediterranean is nailed to this one bang. The best evidence is an olive tree the eruption buried — and the volcano's own gas may have poisoned it into lying about the year.
ps: point (caldera, Santorini, Cyclades)

note: Three hundred elephants and more come out of the forest every dry season and stand on the bed of a reservoir a king built eighteen hundred years ago. It is the largest gathering of Asian elephants on Earth.
