And they are not coming for the water — the man who has studied them longest says they come for the grass, because new grass on drained mud has more protein in it than old grass. Which means the whole thing is an accident of irrigation: the farmers let the tank down to water the rice, and the best pasture in Sri Lanka comes up in the mud. Now a new scheme keeps the tank full all year, and the elephants are leaving. It is being ended by too much water.
ps: zone (national park, ~8,890 ha; the gathering is on the reservoir bed)

n: Chobe — the refuge they can't leave
note: This river was one leg of a migration, not a destination. Then the wars started next door and rival armies shot elephants for target practice and sold the ivory to buy guns, and the herds stopped crossing the water and stayed on the safe side. The numbers built up until this became the most elephant-crowded ground in Africa, which everyone calls a triumph and which is actually a bomb shelter. The wars are over now. But the elephants who knew the old routes are dead, and the ones alive never learned them, so they stay — and eat the place down to the roots.
ps: zone (national park, ~11,000-11,700 km²; the concentration is on the riverfront)

note: Elephants call each other by name. Not by copying the other one's voice — that is what dolphins and parrots do. They make up a sound for you that you never make yourself, and you come when you hear it, and you ignore the ones meant for somebody else. That is an arbitrary label, which is supposed to be the thing that makes language language. It took thirty-six years of recordings to prove, and it only worked because somebody had spent fifty years learning every one of their faces first.
ps: zone (Samburu & Buffalo Springs National Reserves, Kenya)

note: Sixty thousand square kilometres, and four peoples in it, not one — the Sua, the Aka, the Efe, the Mbuti — each paired with a different farming people they trade and argue and intermarry with. The Mbuti hunt with nets, which needs everybody, so nobody is in charge and everything gets shared. The Efe hunt with bows. They call the forest ndura, mother and father both, and they call themselves bamiki bandura: the children of the forest. Borneo says orang hutan about an ape. The Bakela say people of the forest about the bonobos. Here a people says it about itself. Some of them are still in there. Many are in camps near Goma, where they are treated as slaves by people who never lived anywhere that had a name like that.
ps: zone (homeland, >60,000 km²; NOT a village. Four peoples across the whole forest.)

n: Lake Eyasi — the bird that speaks your dialect
note: Nobody tamed this bird. Nobody trained it or feeds it. It finds the beehive, comes and finds a person, and talks them to the tree — and when you have taken the honey it eats the wax, which almost nothing else can digest. It is one of the only real partnerships between people and a wild animal on Earth, and it is worth a tenth of everything the Hadza eat. And the bird learns your accent. Here they whistle; in Mozambique they grunt; in Zambia they chop wood — and the birds come three times more readily to the local sound. The Hadza whistle is shaped like a bird call, because they are hunting game at the same time and do not want to frighten it. And the honeyguide is a brood parasite whose chicks kill their foster siblings, so it never met its parents and cannot have been taught any of this.
ps: zone (Eyasi basin homeland, roughly placed per the editor's ruling — NOT a village or camp location)

n: Dzanga Bai — the village of elephants
note: The elephants made this. Ten hectares of open ground held in the middle of a rainforest by nothing but elephants eating and trampling it clear, over a seam of volcanic rock that puts salt in the water — and it is big enough to see from space. Everywhere else in the Congo, forty or fifty forest elephants is the most you will ever see. Here it is the fewest. A woman sat on a platform above it for twenty-five years and learned three thousand of them by face; it took her four years to know most of them and new ones walked in every year she was there. The mothers bring the babies because the water is shallow and you can see what is coming. And between 1995 and 2010 the grown males went from a fifth of them to a tenth, while nothing happened to the females at all.
ps: point (10-ha clearing; published coordinates)

note: Kenya's biggest park is here because nobody wanted the land: too dry to farm, and the tsetse killed any cattle you walked in, so the colonial government looked at eight thousand square miles of thorn and gave it to the elephants. DAVID SHELDRICK was its first warden and its elephants come out red because they throw the iron in the soil over themselves. In 1971 nine thousand of them starved in three months — and his wife DAPHNE, who watched it, called that the natural part, because the trees that grew back had been planted by the elephants that died, in their dung. The poaching, she said, was the only unnatural thing in fifty years. It left hundreds of orphans too young to eat, and nobody on earth knew how to make milk an infant elephant could live on; she worked it out by holding the ones that didn't. And she said the ivory stockpile was why they moved David out of Tsavo in 1976. He was dead within the year.
ps: zone (Tsavo East + West, >20,000 km²; pin on Tsavo East)

n: Border Cave — the oldest thing we will call counting
note: Twenty-nine notches cut into a baboon's shin bone, forty-four thousand years ago, and everyone calls it the oldest mathematical object on Earth. It isn't. There are notched bones twice as old — we just won't call those mathematics, because we can't tell scratching from counting. This is the oldest one we are willing to count, which is a fact about us. And the twenty-nine, which is the whole reason anyone says lunar calendar, is a floor and not a total, because the bone is snapped off at one end. The man who found it never thought it was a calculator: the notches were cut with different blades, so he read it as many hands at a ceremony, each adding one. The San in Namibia still keep tallies exactly this way.
ps: point (Border Cave, Lebombo Mountains, South Africa / Eswatini border)

note: One column reads nineteen, seventeen, thirteen, eleven — every prime between ten and twenty — so the story is that somebody on this river knew about prime numbers twenty thousand years before the Greeks. Wikipedia's own sentence: this might be a coincidence. There are only four primes in that decade. The better idea belongs to a Belgian engineer who noticed the middle column is just the other two added together, and it is better precisely because it does not need anyone to have known what a prime is. And the strongest evidence isn't the bone at all — it is that the people living on this same river still say seven as six-plus-one and eight as two-fours, which is exactly how the bone is built. The bone is in Brussels.
ps: point (Ishango, Semliki river / Lake Edward, now inside Virunga NP, DRC)

n: Namakkal — the goddess who gave permission
note: The goddess here is Namagiri, and a clerk from Kumbakonam said she gave him the equations in his sleep. He wrote thousands of theorems and proved almost none of them; he simply knew. An equation, he said, has no meaning to me unless it expresses a thought of God. And in 1914, when Cambridge sent for him, he could not go — for a Brahmin the sea crossing was a caste matter, not a travel one — so he came here and asked her, and she said yes. Then Hardy, who believed in nothing, looked at the impossible pages and decided they must be true, because if they were not true no one would have had the imagination to invent them.
ps: point (Lakshmi Narasimha Swamy temple, Namakkal, Tamil Nadu)

n: Irinjalakuda — the series that does not stop
note: Sangamagrama, probably — the union of two markets. Madhava showed here that you can add numbers forever and land somewhere finite, and he could say how far wrong he was at every step, which is the part that makes it calculus and not a lucky guess. Almost everything he wrote is lost. We have him because other people quoted him.
ps: point (town; the identification of Sangamagrama is itself uncertain — see coord_status)

note: Fifteen rows of Pythagorean triples, a thousand years before Pythagoras, and one row reads 12709, 13500, 18541 — which nobody stumbles onto. What it was for is still argued: number theory, trigonometry, or a teacher setting homework. The tablet is in New York. That it came from here at all is the word of the man who sold it.
ps: zone (ancient city mound, Tell as-Senkereh — provenance asserted, not excavated in situ)

n: Phaistos — the corrections are the proof
note: Two hundred and forty-one signs pressed into wet clay with individual stamps — movable type, three thousand years early, used once, for something nobody can read. It has been decoded dozens of times and none of them held. The best evidence that it is real is that the scribe made mistakes and went back and fixed them.
ps: point (palace site; the disc was found in the basement of room 8, building 101, NE of the main palace)

n: Pforzen — the form outlives the meaning
note: The oldest line of poetry in the German language, in a warrior's grave beside his lance and his sword. Nobody agrees what it says — they scolded someone, they fought at the Ilz, they fought a whole war-band. Everybody agrees it names Aigil and Ailrun, the two on the Franks Casket. We know it is verse because it alliterates: the form survives the meaning.
ps: point (Alemannic cemetery, grave 239, Pforzen, Ostallgäu, Swabia)

note: A man in a wolf's head and pelt carrying a sword with an oath ring on the hilt, embossed into thin silver and buried with a warrior beside the church. One of the last pagan things made in Alemannia — the ring-swords stop appearing once the Christians arrive. Whatever he is, he is three centuries older than anyone who wrote the wolf-warriors down.
ps: zone (Alemannic grave near St Gallus church, Gutenstein, Stadt Sigmaringen, Kreis Sigmaringen)

n: Jayuya — zemí is not the stone
note: Zemí is not the stone. The Spanish found three-pointed stones in the cassava fields and wrote them down as idols, but the word means the force inside — and it is kin to a word for sweetness. A cemí had a name and an owner and could be stolen; the chiefs here and on Hispaniola gave them to each other. The museum is shaped like one, in a barrio still called Coabey — the Taíno word for the land of the dead.
ps: point (Museo el Cemí, Coabey barrio, Jayuya, Puerto Rico) — standing for a Greater Antillean practice, not a single findspot

note: Nobody argues about the rock. Water and time made it, and it happens to look like a person. The argument is whether somebody four hundred thousand years ago looked at it, saw a person too, and cut a few grooves to help the rest of us see it. The other side says the only people seeing a person here are us.
ps: point (river terrace deposit, north bank of the Draa, near Tan-Tan, southern Morocco)

n: Alexandria — the word that adds to the year
note: The letters add to three hundred and sixty-five, and that is the whole point: Basilides taught here that the word named the power ruling that many heavens, one for every day. It is cut into hundreds of gems across the empire, on a thing with a cock's head, a man's body and serpents for legs. Scholars called them Gnostic gems. Almost nothing else on them is Gnostic.
ps: city (Alexandria — where Basilides taught; NOT a findspot. The gems are scattered across the Roman Empire and have no single provenance)

n: Pomerania — no two faces the same
note: The ashes went into a jar with a face on it, and no two faces are the same. They gave them ears and hung real bronze earrings in them, beads of amber and glass. They gave them wrinkles. The lid is a hat. Whatever else was going on, somebody was making sure you could tell one of these people from another.
ps: region (Pomeranian culture core, southern Baltic coast — roughly placed; a practice across northern Poland, not one site)

note: A swamp meadow where three rivers meet, waterlogged the year round and mineral-rich enough that everything wades out of the forest into it — gorillas and forest elephants both. Because it is open, people can watch. Twenty-five years from a platform at the edge, every gorilla known by face, and the population doubled while western gorillas were collapsing everywhere else.
ps: point (Mbeli Bai, 12.8 ha clearing in the south-west of Nouabalé-Ndoki National Park, northern Republic of Congo)

note: The forest has roads and the elephants built them — bembo, the Baka call them, trails that cross whole watersheds, walked by everything, including people. Ask whether that is cooperation and you may have imported the question: the anthropology here argues these are people who do not trade with the forest so much as share with it, without a ledger. And some of them are also elephants.
ps: region (Baka homeland, south-eastern Cameroon — ROUGHLY PLACED CENTROID under the homeland convention; not a village, not a camp, not a trail)

n: Osun-Osogbo — when the prohibition stopped working
note: Every Yoruba town had one of these, and this is the last big one. By the 1950s the priests had gone and the felling and the fishing went on freely: the prohibition had stopped working. An Austrian woman came, was initiated, became a priestess, and spent forty years filling the forest with gods — hundreds of them, built to challenge the men buying the land. The bricklayers she taught are still carving.
ps: zone (Osun-Osogbo Sacred Grove, ~75 ha of forest along the Osun River outside Osogbo, Osun State, Nigeria)

n: Meiji Jingū — the forest that never pretended
note: The word for these — the guardian forest around a shrine — was coined by a novelist in the 1890s, and the archetype it names, a patch of untouched broadleaf wood, mostly is not there: plenty of shrine forests are planted conifer. This one never pretended. A hundred thousand trees, donated from all over the country, put in by hand. It runs itself now, in the middle of Tokyo.
ps: point (Meiji Jingū and its forest, Shibuya, Tokyo — standing for chinju no mori, a category of ~80,000 shrine forests)

note: The biggest honeybee alive builds a single comb the width of a door on a sunlit cliff, and comes back to the same rock every year. The Gurung come back too, twice a year, up rope ladders, and thank a spirit called Rangkemi. The bee carries an enzyme for the rhododendron poison, so it drinks what nothing else can — which is why the honey is mad.
ps: region (Gurung honey-hunting country, Lamjung and Kaski districts, Nepal — ROUGHLY PLACED under the homeland convention; the cliffs are traditional, named, returned to, and are not marked here)

n: Blacksburg — a hundred and thirty-three thousand acorns
note: Somebody counted: in one autumn, from one stand of pin oaks, the jays carried off a hundred and thirty-three thousand acorns — more than half the crop — and buried them up to a mile out. Squirrels bury them under the parent tree. This is why the oaks moved north after the ice as fast as seeds that blow on the wind: the forest was planted by birds who meant to come back and eat it.
ps: point (the Darley-Hill & Johnson study site, Blacksburg, Virginia — where the count was made; the phenomenon is eastern North America)

n: Bowling Green — the mind must be in a pleasurable condition
note: You can tickle a rat and it chirps at fifty kilohertz, above anything you can hear, and afterwards it comes back looking for your hand. It only works if the rat is in a good mood: make it anxious and the laughing stops — which is what Darwin said about us. The ones housed alone laugh the most. In four generations they bred a line that laughs more.
ps: point (Bowling Green State University, Ohio — where Panksepp's lab was; the finding is a laboratory result, not a place in the world)

n: Exeter — the obscenity is the reader's
note: About ninety-five riddles at the back of a book a bishop gave to his cathedral, where it still is. A dozen are filthy, and not one of them says anything: the stiff thing hanging under a man's cloak turns out to be a key. There is an onion riddle that is a joke and, elsewhere in the same book, a second onion told straight. The obscenity is the reader's. They knew that.
ps: point (Exeter Cathedral, Devon — the manuscript's home since the 11th century and still its location)

note: He was in a house here with four children and a mortgage, blocked and broke, when Harlan Ellison told him to come to Los Angeles and write television. What he wrote there was live long and prosper — said now by people who could not name him. Vonnegut turned him into Kilgore Trout. He is the one who said ninety percent of everything is crap, and his daughter points out that he was wrong about himself.
ps: point (Woodstock, Ulster County, New York — where Sturgeon lived and worked through the 1960s and 1970s)

note: They went into the desert because it had no water and nobody could follow them there. Then they built a city of thirty thousand on six inches of rain a year — dams, pipes, cisterns roofed against evaporation, catchments that took nearly every drop that fell in the basin. And they hid it. The structures were built so that only people who already knew where to look could find them. Petra was never walled.
ps: zone (Petra, southern Jordan — the basin and its catchment, not the Treasury)

n: Spirit Cave — the science caught up
note: They said in 1997 that he was theirs. The government said prove it. Twenty years, a lawsuit, and a review committee that voted six to one to send him home — the committee was advisory. In the end a Danish geneticist read his genome, with their permission and on the promise that a Native American answer meant return, and it said what they had said. He was reburied in 2018. He had been out of the ground since 1940.
ps: zone (Spirit Cave, Churchill County, Nevada — ROUGHLY PLACED; a burial site, and it has been robbed once already)

n: Tallinn — whoever is late will miss out
note: Five years out of the Soviet Union, broke, rebuilding the roads, and the president went on television and said every school would be wired. The teachers' union said it was a stupid idea. He quoted Bacon — knowledge is power — and added: whoever is late will miss out. By 1999 every school was online, and they were made to leave the labs open after hours so that anyone could walk in and use them.
ps: point (the National Library of Estonia, Tallinn — where the Tiger Leap Foundation was founded; standing for a national programme)

n: Tivoli — the Pope went inside to check
note: It plays a madrigal in five parts and nobody is touching it: falling water makes the wind, and another machine works the keys. When it was finished in 1571 the Pope came to see it and insisted on going inside to check there was no man hidden in there playing. The same water that worked it eventually furred up the pipes and it went quiet for a long time. It plays again.
ps: point (the Fountain of the Organ, Villa d'Este, Tivoli — the fountain, not the villa)

n: Nongriat — only an elder with no children may plant it
note: You plant figs on both banks and spend thirty years walking their roots out across the gorge on bamboo until they meet and fuse. Then it is a bridge, and it goes on growing — stronger every year, and it mends itself. The rule is that only an elder who has no children may plant one. He will not cross it, and nor will anyone of his.
ps: point (Nongriat, East Khasi Hills — the emblematic double-decker jingkieng jri; the practice spans two districts and 74 documented structures)

note: It is named after an antiquarian who put it in a book in 1790. Before that it was the Cockpen Tree, because people kept fighting cocks in the hole. It was probably not hollow yet when Robin Hood is supposed to have hidden inside it. Steel poles have held its arms up since 1908, and a fence has kept the visitors off its roots since the 1970s. In the spring of 2026 it did not come into leaf.
ps: point (the Major Oak, Sherwood Forest National Nature Reserve, near Edwinstowe, Nottinghamshire)

n: Dome Fuji — named for a pond weed
note: At seventy below, in no wind, the frost grows needles; then a breath of air knocks them down and they clump by static and roll, gathering snow, into balls. They last a day or two. Amundsen saw them in 1911 and had no word for them. Siple touched one in 1957 and it vanished. The men who named them were overwintering at the highest point in Dronning Maud Land, and they named them after a pond weed from a lake in Hokkaido.
ps: point (Dome Fuji station, 77°19′01″S 39°42′12″E, 3,810 m — the highest point in Dronning Maud Land)

n: Kituwah — they called it Ferguson's Fields
note: The mound stood twenty feet high and two centuries of ploughing took it down to five. The farm was called Ferguson's Fields. In 1996 the Fergusons rang up and asked whether the tribe might want to buy it, and the tribe did, with money the casino had only just started making. Then Duke Energy began clearing ground overlooking it for a substation, and was made to stop. They grow vegetables there now.
ps: zone (Kituwah, the Cherokee mother town — the 309-acre parcel on the Tuckasegee near Bryson City, North Carolina; the mound is one feature of it)

n: Noquisiyi — the weed killer started it
note: Cherokee women built it, basket of soil by basket of soil, and it has never been dug, so nobody knows how old it is. It stands on the main street. In 1946 the town's children raised money to save it from developers, and in 2012 a town employee sprayed it with weed killer to save on the mowing. That is what started the giving back. The deed was signed in 2026, with dancers.
ps: point (Noquisiyi / Nikwasi Mound, downtown Franklin, Macon County, North Carolina)

n: Judaculla Rock — the hunting laws are wearing off
note: Fifteen hundred marks on a slab of soapstone, and the Cherokee say they are hunting laws, cut by a slant-eyed giant who landed here jumping down off his mountain to chase people off his land. Nobody knows how far into the ground the rock goes. The signs beside it quote a book from 1902 because there is nothing better. It is soapstone. It is wearing away while they read it.
ps: point (Judaculla Rock, 552 Judaculla Rock Rd, Cullowhee, Jackson County, NC — one of several petroglyph boulders in a 15-acre site)

n: Teejop — three men stood on a hill
note: In 1829 three men from Michigan stood on a hill here and envisioned a bustling city; in 1836 they got one. The hill already had mounds on it — birds, bears, panthers, water spirits, a kind of earthwork found nowhere else on the planet. Of the fifteen hundred that were here, a couple of hundred are left. Bascom Hall is standing on some of the others.
ps: point (Bascom Hill, University of Wisconsin–Madison — standing for Teejop, the Four Lakes; the mounds are spread across the city and the campus)

n: Mendota — the hospital's logo is the bird it stands on
note: A bird with a six-hundred-foot wingspan lies on the lawn of the first asylum in Wisconsin, and the hospital took it for a logo. Fifty mounds were here in three groups; the eastern group — a fox, a thunderbird, a falcon — is gone. They mow the survivors along with the grass. It was the largest bird in the world until somebody measured the one at Muscoda, which is twice the size.
ps: point (Mendota State Hospital Mound Group, grounds of the Mendota Mental Health Institute, northwest shore of Lake Mendota, Madison)

n: Wanuskewin — he named it Four Stones
note: He dug here in the eighties and found nothing, but he named the place Four Stones anyway, because he thought the land was holding something. The buffalo came back in 2019, after a century gone. Eight months later they wallowed a pit in the grass and there was a carved stone under it. Three more followed, and then the knife that cut them. The elders had said the return would change history.
ps: zone (Wanuskewin Heritage Park, 600 acres on the Opimihaw Creek valley north of Saskatoon, Treaty Six Territory and Homeland of the Métis Nation)

n: The Sacramento — a crop nobody planted
note: You cannot orchard an oak — it takes twenty years to bear, and it crops well one year in three. So they burned instead: low fire, often, which the oaks survive and their competitors do not. Then they stored two years of acorns in baskets thatched against the rain. The densest people in northern California lived here, on a crop nobody planted. By 1870 the oak woods along the rivers had gone.
ps: region (the Sacramento River corridor and its valley oak groves — ROUGHLY PLACED; this is a landscape practice across a valley, not a site)

note: Nobody carved the holes. They are worn — eleven hundred and eighty-five cups in a slab of soft limestone, each one the shape of decades of women grinding acorns. Somebody also cut three hundred and sixty-three pictures into it, which is almost the only place on Earth anyone ever decorated a mortar. The stone is soft: that is why the cups formed, and why the pictures are going.
ps: point (the chaw'se — the main grinding rock — Indian Grinding Rock State Historic Park, Pine Grove, Amador County, California)

n: Begur — the first time anyone wrote the name
note: The oldest time anybody wrote this city's name down, they wrote it to say a man had died. Bengaluru kaleghadhul Buttana Setti sattam — Buttana Setti fell in the Bengaluru war — cut into a hero stone about 890. It was found in the floor of a shrine, being walked on. The city still dates itself from a fort six centuries younger.
ps: point (the Nageshvara / Panchalingeshwara temple complex, Begur, Bengaluru South)

n: Kolar — the men who dug the hole were on strike
note: Men had been taking gold out of this ground since about 200 AD, and by 1965 the hole was over two kilometres deep — deep enough that the rock above it strains out everything except the particle that goes through everything. That is where they first caught it. The physicists were writing to each other about arranging a visit. The men who dug the hole were on strike.
ps: point (Champion Reef mine, Kolar Gold Fields, Kolar district, Karnataka — the laboratory was at the 80th level of the Heathcote shaft, 2.3 km down)

n: Onondaga Lake — there is no monument
note: They buried their weapons in a hole here and planted a white pine on top, which has five needles to a bundle, one for each nation that stopped fighting. There is no monument. The ground the Peacemaker walked on is not ground — it is thirty feet of soda-ash waste, and a hundred and forty-four million tons of it went into the lake. In 2022 a pair of eagles nested on the shore for the first time in a century.
ps: zone (Onondaga Lake, Onondaga County, New York — the lake and its shore; the Superfund project alone has nine sub-sites)

note: The first person who ever agreed with the Peacemaker was a woman, and this was her town — four and a half thousand people in a hundred and fifty longhouses. In 1687 the French came for the corn and burned a million and a third bushels of it. Their own Indian allies were shocked enough to name them for it: corncutters. Then the clan mothers raised an army and threw them out. The corn grows here again.
ps: zone (Ganondagan / Gannagaro, Victor, Ontario County, New York — the hilltop town and Fort Hill, the fortified granary a mile west)

n: Kejimkujik — they decided to let them go
note: Five hundred pictures cut into slate at the water's edge — a woman's peaked cap, a caribou, a sailboat, a French soldier. You have to splash water on the rock to see them. Every winter the ice comes across and takes a little more. In the eighties the elders and the park sat down to decide how to save them, and the elders said to let them wear away, because they are not of this time. So the trail was moved and the rock was left alone.
ps: zone (the slate outcrops on the shores of Kejimkujik Lake and George Lake, Kejimkujik National Park and National Historic Site, southwestern Nova Scotia — Mi'kma'ki)

n: Fort Kent — the Pork and Beans War
note: Ten thousand Maine militia went north, Congress voted fifty thousand men and ten million dollars, and the casualty column reads none on both sides. The general the President sent had been a prisoner of war once, under the man now governing New Brunswick, and they had become friends in the cell. They talked. Nobody fired. They call it the Pork and Beans War.
ps: point (the Fort Kent blockhouse, Aroostook County, Maine — one of several thrown up along a border nobody could find)

note: The famous part is that it happened on Christmas. The unfamous part is that it happened constantly. In quiet sectors men on both sides tacitly agreed not to shoot — to fetch the dead, to mend a trench, to eat — and they had a name for it: live and let live. A historian wrote it down sixty years later. Christmas 1914 is remembered because it was too big to hide.
ps: point (Ploegsteert, Hainaut, Belgium — one named sector of a truce that ran along much of the British line; the practice ran along all of it)

note: The first man to draw it, in 1680, said the Indians had carved a battle on it. Then a president of Yale said Phoenician; a Frenchman who never saw it divided it into past, present and future; a Dane looking for Vikings found Vikings; a psychology professor found a lost Portuguese; and a book from 2002 found the Chinese. George Washington came, said it looked like the Indian carvings he knew from Virginia, and smiled.
ps: point (Dighton Rock State Park, Berkley, Massachusetts — the rock now sits in a museum; it stood in the Taunton River until 1963)

n: Wyam — four and a half hours
note: People fished here for fifteen thousand years, standing on wooden platforms out over the white water with dipnets, and in the spring ten times more water came over these falls than goes over Niagara. It was the oldest place on the continent where anybody had lived without stopping. On a morning in 1957 they shut the gates of a dam eight miles downstream, and four and a half hours later it was a lake.
ps: zone (Celilo Falls / Wyam and the villages — Wyam on the Oregon shore, S'kin on the Washington side — Columbia River, submerged since 1957)

n: Cahokia — the name on the sign is somebody else's
note: Twenty thousand people, six square miles, a hundred and twenty mounds, and one of them has the footprint of the Great Pyramid and was carried there in baskets, on foot, one load at a time. It was bigger than London. No city in the United States was as big again until 1800. They left no writing, so the name on the sign is somebody else's — a different people, who came later, and were here when the French arrived.
ps: zone (Cahokia Mounds, Collinsville, Illinois — the city covered ~6 square miles; Illinois protects about 2,200 acres of roughly 4,000)

n: Woodhenge — they ran out of trees
note: They found it because they were building a highway interchange. Somebody mapped the postholes and nothing else, and the holes turned out to be arcs of circles — five of them, each one twelve posts bigger than the last. From the middle, at the equinox, the sun comes up out of the front of Monks Mound half a mile away. The last circle was never finished. By then there were no big trees left near the city.
ps: point (the reconstructed Woodhenge III, on its original post positions, half a mile west of Monks Mound, Cahokia Mounds)

n: Poverty Point — and only then by people with farms
note: Hunter-fisher-gatherers built this: five mounds, six ridges in half-circles three quarters of a mile across, two million cubic yards of earth moved in baskets. UNESCO says nothing in North America surpassed it for two thousand years — and adds, in brackets, that when something finally did, it was built by people with farms. There are no burials in it. The name on the sign belongs to a plantation.
ps: zone (Monumental Earthworks of Poverty Point, West Carroll Parish, Louisiana — five mounds, six concentric ridges and a central plaza on a landform above the Mississippi floodplain)

n: Watson Brake — he did not believe his own numbers
note: A woman who lived nearby saw the humps after the loggers cleared the trees. Eleven mounds in an oval, older than the pyramids. The archaeologist who dated them did not believe his own numbers and did the work again, and it still said five thousand four hundred years — two thousand before anybody here farmed anything. What gave it away was the soil under the mound, which had taken millennia to turn red.
ps: zone (Watson Brake, Ouachita Parish, Louisiana — eleven mounds in an oval on the Ouachita floodplain near Monroe; ROUGHLY PLACED)

n: Estipah-skikikini-kots — a boy wanted to watch
note: The name is not about the buffalo. A young man wanted to see it happen, so he stood in a crack at the bottom of the cliff, and they found him afterwards under the pile. People had been doing this here for six thousand years. The funnel of stone cairns ran miles back into the prairie, and the men who moved the herd wore wolf skins. Get it wrong and everyone starves in the winter. The bones go down twelve metres.
ps: zone (Head-Smashed-In Buffalo Jump, Porcupine Hills, southwestern Alberta — the cliff, the kill site below it, and drive lanes running miles back onto the prairie)

n: Chavín de Huántar — knowing which is which made you a believer
note: A granite god stands in the dark at the centre, wedged between floor and ceiling so he cannot be lifted out — three thousand years, unmoved. He is cut so the lines read two ways at once: knowing which is which is what made you a believer. Twenty conch trumpets came out of the gallery around him, worn through by mouths, carried five hundred miles from the sea. Play one in these corridors and the building pulls the note into tune. Nobody can prove it was built to.
ps: zone (Chavín de Huántar, Ancash, Peru — the Old Temple, its fourteen galleries, the Lanzón in its cruciform chamber, and the Circular Plaza. CONSOLIDATED: this row now carries S5-240's subject as well.)

note: They had always said a great slide buried part of the village. In 1970 a storm cut the bank open and there were six longhouses under the mud with the baskets still in them. The story was true. A hiker told the tribe and the tribe rang the university. Eleven years, fifty-five thousand things, and the tribe kept every one. They had left in 1917, when the government told them to send the children to school.
ps: zone (Ozette, Cape Alava, Olympic Peninsula, Washington — the southernmost of the five pre-contact Makah villages; ROUGHLY PLACED)

note: People have lived up here without stopping for longer than anywhere else in this country, three hundred and fifty-seven feet above the plain. In 1599 the Spanish came for the winter grain, and afterwards Oñate had the right foot cut off every man over twenty-five. Spain banished him for it, which tells you something. Four centuries later they put up a bronze of him on a horse. Somebody took an electric saw to it and left a note: fair is fair.
ps: zone (Acoma Pueblo — Sky City / Old Acoma — on its mesa in Cibola County, New Mexico; a living pueblo, not a site)

n: Etzanoa — nobody believed him for four hundred years
note: Oñate came here in 1601, two years after he had the feet cut off at Acoma, and reported a town of twenty thousand whose houses had no end in sight. For four centuries everyone assumed he was lying. Then a professor read a new translation and thought he recognised the ground, and in 2017 a local teenager found a cannonball in it. The magnetometer showed the houses exactly where the Spanish said.
ps: zone (Etzanoa, near the Walnut and Arkansas rivers at present-day Arkansas City, Kansas — the settlement ran about five miles; ROUGHLY PLACED)

n: Torrey Pines — every tree is the same tree
note: Two groves and nothing else, a hundred and seventy miles apart. On the island the two thousand trees are so inbred you cannot tell one from another — no variation within the population at all, which the geneticist calls unprecedented. A century ago they were cleared for cattle pasture, and what survived is all the variety the species has left. Its own docents will tell you another pine may be rarer.
ps: point (Torrey Pines State Natural Reserve, the mainland grove on the coastal bluffs above Soledad Valley, La Jolla — one of the species' only two stands)

n: The Black Belt — the dirt came first
note: Seventy million years ago the sea came up to here, and the plankton fell out of it, and their shells made a chalk that weathers into a black crumbling dirt in a crescent three hundred miles long — the exact line of that shore. Cotton wanted it. So planters brought people to work it. Booker T. Washington had to explain that the belt was named for the soil first.
ps: region (the Black Belt / Black Prairie — a crescent ~300 miles long and up to 25 wide, from north-east Mississippi across central Alabama; ROUGHLY PLACED near Columbus, Mississippi, which sits in it)

n: The Cleveland Shale — nobody has found the body
note: A hotel keeper walking the shore with his son in 1867 found the plates of something with no teeth — blade-jawed, self-sharpening, the size of a room. The city is built on the sea floor it died in. They named it for him and for the curator who studied it. In a hundred and fifty years nobody has ever found the rest of one: the head was armour and the body was cartilage, and only armour keeps.
ps: zone (the Cleveland Shale Member of the Ohio Shale, exposed in the Rocky River and Big Creek valleys under the Cleveland urban area — ROUGHLY PLACED on Rocky River Reservation)

n: Nanih Waiya — never again, they wrote
note: The Choctaw came out of the ground here, or stopped walking here — the stories differ. They were the first nation removed under Jackson's act, and for a hundred and thirty years the farmers ploughed it: the four cemetery mounds and the earthwork wall are gone. It came back in 2008 and a thousand people came. The council signed a paper saying it would never be taken again. It was vandalised in 2023.
ps: point (Nanih Waiya Mound, Winston County, Mississippi — 25 ft high, 218 x 140 ft; the mound only)

n: The Grand Village — they stacked the guns on his house
note: They were the last people still building mounds when the French arrived, and the French wrote it all down — the Great Sun's house on one, the bone house of the chiefs on another. In 1730 the French and their Choctaw allies besieged them from their own village and stacked the artillery on the Great Sun's mound. Three hundred survivors were sold into the West Indies. The plantation that followed was called Fatherland.
ps: zone (the Grand Village of the Natchez / Fatherland site, 22 Ad 501 — 128 acres on St. Catherine Creek, south Natchez, Mississippi)

n: Emerald Mound — they edited a hill
note: The second biggest earthwork in the country and they did not carry it there. There was a hill; they cut the top off it and packed the sides until it was a plateau eight acres across with a pentagon's outline. Nobody lived on it — the people were in villages round about and came up. Later somebody ploughed the summit and six of the mounds on top of it went. It is named after the plantation.
ps: point (Emerald Mound / the Selsertown site, 22 Ad 504 — the platform and its two surviving secondary mounds, near Stanton, Adams County, Mississippi)

n: Hikki'ya' — they threw the grenades back
note: The Natchez who got away came here, and the French demanded them back. The Chickasaw said no, so in 1736 Bienville marched on them with six hundred Choctaw and no cannon — it never arrived from France. They already knew he was coming; they had taken the letter off a captured officer. He threw grenades and they pulled the fuses out and threw them back. In the end it cost him his governorship. Nobody knows exactly where it happened.
ps: zone (the Chickasaw villages north-west of present-day Tupelo, Lee County, Mississippi — Hikki'ya' was one of about eleven villages in a 75-square-mile area collectively called Ackia; ROUGHLY PLACED)

n: Kelleys Island — the quarry found them and the quarry ate them
note: The ice came through and left grooves in the rock like the chutes of a slide, four hundred feet long and deep enough to lose a man in. The quarrymen found them in the eighteen-thirties, taking the top off the island, and then they took the rest of them out. One is left. The rock they are cut into was a warm sea south of the equator once, and it is made of what lived in it.
ps: point (Glacial Grooves Geological Preserve, north-west corner of Kelleys Island, western Lake Erie, Ohio — the one surviving giant groove)

n: The Tombigbee — the law made them look first
note: They cut a canal from the Tennessee to the Tombigbee, and by then the law made them look before they dug. Thousands of sites nobody had recorded came up out of the ground around here. At one, on a terrace upriver, the floods and the living had alternated for so long that the whole sequence stacked in a few feet of dirt — the oldest tools at the bottom, in order, like a book.
ps: zone (the Upper Tombigbee valley around Columbus, Mississippi — the Tennessee-Tombigbee Waterway corridor and its salvage archaeology; ROUGHLY PLACED. The waterway runs 234 miles and the excavations run its length)

note: The only fern that is Iceland's own grows on one hot slope where the soil never leaves thirty degrees, and nowhere else on Earth. It could live in cold ground — they have shown that. It simply cannot get anywhere: too many of its spores come out wrong. The spring beside it is the strongest in Europe. Its water heats two towns, one of them forty miles off, and grows bananas in the snow.
ps: point (Deildartunguhver, Reykholtsdalur, western Iceland — the hot spring and its slope; the fern's entire world range)

n: Des-Cubierta — thirty-five heads and no bodies
note: Thirty-five heads and almost no bodies: bison, aurochs, red deer, two rhinoceros, the horns and antlers laid by little fires. The jaws were cut away and the tongues and eyes taken, but not the brains. A head is hardly any food. They did this for centuries. A child's teeth lie beside one of the hearths, next to a single horn. The cave's name means uncovered, and it has no roof.
ps: point (Cueva Des-Cubierta, Calvero de la Higuera, Pinilla del Valle, upper Lozoya valley, Community of Madrid — 1,112 m, an 87 m gallery)

n: Alchester — the first life anyone here can name
note: Two gateposts stayed wet for two thousand years, so the rings can tell you the trees came down between one October and the March after — eighteen months after the landing. Vespasian had the legion then. They ate local cattle and nobody knows whether they paid. A veteran stayed on and died here, and his broken headstone is the first life anyone in this county can put a name to.
ps: point (Alchester Roman fortress and town — Ælia Castra — Wendlebury parish, ~3 km south of Bicester, Oxfordshire)

n: Sharon — the rock is the water
note: A river ran here three hundred and ten million years ago, braided like the Platte, and dropped its gravel bars. Everything but the quartz has gone: the pebbles are almost pure silica, and some of them are fossils that had already been rock once and got rounded again. Nobody agrees which way the river came from. The bars are the aquifer now. This is where the water comes out of.
ps: point (Sharon, Pennsylvania — the town the formation is named for, ~20 km east of Youngstown. ⚠ THE SHARON CONGLOMERATE HAS NO FORMAL TYPE SECTION; this is the name's origin, not a reference outcrop)

n: The Yolo Bypass — the fish were built for the flood
note: The salmon evolved for a valley that flooded every winter, and ninety-five per cent of that is gone. Here the water still spreads and slows and warms, and the water fleas bloom in it, and the young fish put on five grams a day — faster than anywhere else in the Central Valley, faster than in the river itself. The ones that leave fat come back. The field is rice again by summer.
ps: zone (the Yolo Bypass — ~57,000-60,000 acres of floodway between the Sacramento River and the western valley; the Nigiri experiments are at Knaggs Ranch in its northern reach)

n: The vernal pools — they are made to wait
note: Rain sits in shallow bowls here because there is a floor of clay under the grass, and the mounds between the pools wash their dead leaves in to feed them. What swims in them has been swimming like that a very long time: the tadpole shrimp looks almost exactly like its ancestors, and it eats the fairy shrimp, which is also endangered. When the pools dry, the eggs wait. They are made to wait.
ps: region (the vernal pool country of the eastern Sacramento Valley around Roseville — a complex of seasonal pools between Mima mounds, not a single pool; ROUGHLY PLACED)

n: Chitwan — fifty centimetres for a hornet, twenty metres for a kite
note: Hundreds of them flip their abdomens in a wave that crosses the comb in less than a second, and it moves faster than they can pass it along one to the next — nobody knows how. It buys half a metre: a hornet cannot get closer than that. They measure its speed and answer in proportion. And they keep a different distance for a bird: a kite twenty metres off puts hundreds of them into the air.
ps: point (Chitwan, Nepal — where Kastberger's experimental Apis dorsata colonies were filmed; shimmering occurs across the species' South and Southeast Asian range)

n: The Maldives — they are chasing the rain
note: They lay in the puddles the rainband leaves, and the larva has six weeks to become a dragonfly before the puddle dries. By then the rain has moved on. So they follow it — up on the high winds, out over the ocean, India to Africa and back, and no one of them ever finishes the circuit. They come down here, where there is no water at all.
ps: point (Malé, Maldives — the stopover where the migration was noticed; the circuit runs India–Maldives–Seychelles–East Africa–India, 14,000–18,000 km)

n: Rapa Nui — the wanderer that stopped
note: It is the most travelled insect there is, and every language names it for going — wanderer, glider, typhoon dragonfly. It crosses three and a half thousand kilometres of open ocean while other dragonflies will not leave the shore. Island ones fly low and quit the updraughts. This is the only population on Earth that does not go anywhere. It reached the loneliest place there is and stopped.
ps: point (Rapa Nui / Easter Island, Chile — the only known non-migratory population of Pantala flavescens)

note: It is not a dragonfly — the specialists call it a griffenfly and say the popular name misleads. Most of them were the size of the ones you know. A few had wings seventy centimetres across and a body that weighed less than a beetle you can buy: ordinary insects with enormous wings, in a sky with nothing else in it. What ended them may have been the first things that could fly and eat them.
ps: point (Commentry, Allier, central France — the Stephanian Coal Measures where Meganeura was found; the holotype is in Paris)

n: Janelia — it flies the model, not the picture
note: It does not chase. It carries a model of where you are going and a model of its own body, and it flies the model — the eyes are only for when you do something it did not predict. It turns its head to hold you in the sharp part of the eye, comes up from underneath, and takes you nineteen times in twenty. To film it they had to fake a summer day, because it will not hunt in a room.
ps: point (HHMI Janelia Research Campus, Ashburn, Virginia — the Leonardo Lab, where the internal-models work was done; the behaviour occurs wherever dragonflies do)

n: Adelaide — one cell picks one fly
note: There is one cell that picks a fly out of a swarm and answers only about that one — a distractor does not dent the signal, which is more than our attention manages. But it has not stopped watching the other. It primes both, and runs a spotlight ahead of each, down where the fly is going, even when the fly goes behind something. Choose on the left, though, and the right eye goes quiet.
ps: point (the University of Adelaide — Wiederman and O'Carroll's lab, where CSTMD1's selective attention was found; the neuron is in the dragonfly)

n: Bedfordshire — it breathes harder to get away
note: It spends years down there; most of its life is the part underwater. It breathes by pulling water into its backside, and when something comes for it, it breathes harder and shoots away on the jet. Folded over its face is an arm with hooks on the end: it fires out a third of the body's length, faster than you can blink. The wings are under the skin the whole time, growing, unused.
ps: point (Bedfordshire, England — ARBITRARY; see coord_status. The larva lives in fresh water everywhere dragonflies do)

n: Kannur — the land was given to the snakes
note: The land was given to the snakes, so nobody may cut it, so it goes back to jungle. They said a termite mound in there meant water underneath — and it does, because the mound opens the soil and lets the rain down. The pond in the middle is the only water for miles in a dry month. The idols still get their garlands. The forest behind them is being paved.
ps: region (the sarpa kavu country of northern Kerala — Kannur and Kasaragod districts; ROUGHLY PLACED. There are hundreds of groves, each a few hundred square metres beside a family house)

n: Ithaca — it will do it with a lit cigarette
note: Two hundred kinds of bird do it and nobody knows what it is for. The jay picks up an ant and wipes it through its feathers, and it can tell which ants are loaded: it eats the empty ones and wipes the full ones. It will also do it with a burning cigarette. Somebody watched one do it with hot chocolate, and another with soap suds. Audubon saw turkeys rolling in an anthill in 1830.
ps: point (Cornell University, Ithaca, New York — where Eisner and Aneshansley ran the one experiment in the file; the behaviour occurs in 200+ species worldwide)

n: Muscoda — you can see it when it snows
note: A bird a quarter of a mile across, ploughed flat, and you can still see it when there is a light snow. A surveyor mapped it in 1886 and nobody read his notes for a hundred years. In 2023 a Ho-Chunk elder who had been waiting decades walked out on somebody else's farm and drew it back on the ground in lime. It is twice the size of the one they used to call the biggest in the world.
ps: zone (the Ghost Eagle, in a farm field near Muscoda, Lower Wisconsin River valley — ROUGHLY PLACED; the mound is flattened and the land is private)

n: Frank's Hill — since the last trip around the sun
note: Twelve small mounds in a line on the west ridge, which may be a calendar and may not — the archaeologists differ. The flattened bird a mile off is part of the alignment. People still climb up at the solstice to watch the sun go down, and they keep a silence for everyone who has died since the last trip around the sun. The hill is named after the man whose foundation owns it.
ps: point (Frank's Hill, southern Richland County, Wisconsin — on STH 193 just off STH 60, a few miles west of Muscoda; both mound groups NRHP-listed)

n: Móogašuc — every one of them named this bank
note: An anthropologist in Omaha once asked six nations where they came from — Ponca, Oto, Missouri, Iowa, Omaha, Sioux — and every one named this bank of red clay on Green Bay. The linguists agree. Their word for themselves means the people of the big voice; their neighbours' word for them meant stinking water. They were removed again and again for forty years and walked back every time, then bought the land with the settlers' own homestead law.
ps: zone (Móogašuc / Red Banks, east shore of Green Bay, Wisconsin — ROUGHLY PLACED; the sources put it ~12 miles north of the city, on the Door Peninsula side)

note: She was chief here. Coyote asked what living she gave her people; she said she was teaching them to live well and build good houses, and asked to do it forever. He said the world was changing and women would not be chiefs, and turned her to rock so all she could do was watch. The village she watches went under the dam in 1957. Men used to stop the train and shoot at her face; you can see where. In 1993 the Army Corps put up a gate.
ps: point (Tsagaglalal, high on the cliff face above the Horsethief Lake unit of Columbia Hills Historical State Park, Klickitat County, Washington)

n: The Chitra — the old otters teach the young ones
note: The otters do not catch the fish; they go under the weed and drive them out into the net, and they are paid in fish. Nobody teaches them — the young ones learn it by watching the old ones, so every boat carries an apprentice. It has been going two hundred years. Five families still do it. In Narail the otters went from a hundred and twenty-two to nineteen in ten years.
ps: zone (the Chitra river at Narail Sadar, Bangladesh — Malopara/Goalbari village; the practice survives here and in Khulna, near the Sundarbans)

n: Inle — the field comes up with the water
note: The water goes up a metre and a half in the rains and the fields go up with it, because the fields float: mats of weed cut off the rim of the lake, towed home, pegged to the bottom with bamboo. Fifty years to make a metre of soil. They row standing, with one leg, so they can see over their own crops. And the hyacinth they build the fields out of is the weed that is choking the lake.
ps: zone (Inle Lake, Shan State, Myanmar — ~116 km² at 880 m; about twenty Intha villages on and around it)

n: L'Anse aux Meadows — the year the circle closed
note: In 993 the sun threw something at the Earth and every tree alive wrote it down in that year's ring — in Germany, in Ireland, in Arizona, in Japan. In Newfoundland three trees carry the mark twenty-eight rings in from the bark. So somebody with an iron axe felled them in 1021, and that is the year the human species finished going round the world. The wood sat in a freezer for fifty years.
ps: point (L'Anse aux Meadows — 'the bay of the grasslands' — the northern tip of Newfoundland; the only confirmed Norse site in North America)

n: The Raimondi Stela — he felt the god under the table
note: A farmer digging a field turned it up and took it home for a table, carved side down in the yard. The Italian came by, was given a meal on it, ran his hand under the edge and felt the god. It is named after him. The carving is so light you can barely see it in the stone at all — that was the point — and you have to turn the whole thing over to see the other one.
ps: point (the Museo Nacional de Arqueología, Antropología e Historia del Perú, Pueblo Libre, Lima — where the stela is; a replica stands in the same courtyard)

n: Turpan — they put the river under the ground
note: No rivers here, and half an inch of rain in a year. So they went up to the mountains and brought the snowmelt down underneath the desert, where the sun cannot get at it — tunnels fifty kilometres long and half a metre wide, dug on your knees, dropping one metre in every thousand. China counts it with the Great Wall. There were eighteen hundred. There are two hundred. The oil wells are drinking it.
ps: region (the karez of the Turpan Basin, Xinjiang — hundreds of separate systems across the depression; also Hami. ROUGHLY PLACED on Turpan)

n: Gonabad — they call it the devil's digging
note: Three hundred metres straight down, a metre wide, by hand — then thirty-three kilometres sideways under the desert on a slope you cannot see. Seventy-five metres of one shaft was dug upwards, from the bottom; they call that the devil's digging. Seventy-three million cubic metres of soil came out and there is not a leak in it. They found the water by reading the plants. It is two and a half thousand years old and it still runs.
ps: point (the Qasabeh / Ghasabe qanat — Kariz-e Kai Khosrow — Gonabad, Razavi Khorasan, Iran; one of eleven qanats inscribed by UNESCO in 2016 as The Persian Qanat)

n: Cantalloc — a pump with nothing in it
note: Everyone comes for the lines. The holes are the achievement: spirals fifty feet across at the top, corkscrewing down to a channel, and the shape funnels the wind underground, and the wind pushes the water along. A pump with nothing in it. Some still run. You cannot carbon-date a hole, so they dated the villages it waters instead — and found the diggers were not the Spanish after all.
ps: zone (the Cantalloc puquios, ~4 km from Nazca, Peru — the best-preserved of a system running across the Nazca drainage; ROUGHLY PLACED)

n: The Hajar ash Sharqī — the system lasts as long as he does
note: The word means to divide into shares. What you own is not water but time — half an hour of it — and the thing runs day and night for ever. They told the time by a pole's shadow, and after six in the evening by the stars. The wristwatch arrived in the sixties. Eight villages still read the sky, and each has one man who knows which stars; the system lasts as long as he does.
ps: zone (the hills of the Hajar ash Sharqī near Sūr, Oman — Halam, 'Abāt and Taymā are three of the ~8 villages still timing falaj water shares by the stars; ROUGHLY PLACED)

n: Touat — the comb splits it into everybody's share
note: The water comes out of the ground and hits a stone comb, and the comb splits it into everybody's share at once — every garden drinking together, for ever, in the proportion it owns. You own a piece of the flow and the duty to keep it dug. Nobody times anything. The men who can calculate the shares and recut the comb are on the endangered list; it is their knowledge going, not the tunnels.
ps: region (the foggara oases of Touat, Gourara and Tidikelt, Algerian Sahara — ~2,000 foggaras in a crescent; ROUGHLY PLACED on Adrar/Touat)

n: Katahdin — the Penobscot are still here
note: They lived all round it and did not climb it: a thing with a moose's head and an eagle's wings lived up there and would eat you. The first white man to the top had guides who would not go all the way. Thoreau took a train and a steamer to the wilderness, hired Penobscots to guide him, and never made the summit anyway. A rich man later bought the mountain and gave it to the state, forever wild. The Penobscot are still here. It is not theirs.
ps: point (Baxter Peak, Katahdin — 5,269 ft, the highest of the mountain's five summits, in Baxter State Park, Maine; Penobscot homeland)

n: Celilo Village — a few words of farewell
note: His name meant full of knowledge. He was born at the falls, was chief there fifty years, carried copies of the treaties about with him, and never took the money. The village had already been moved three times — for a railway, a canal, a road. When the gates shut he would not go and watch. Two months later, at a hundred and two, he came back to say a few words of farewell to a waterfall that was under water.
ps: point (Celilo Village, Oregon — the village on the bank, where the 1957 Feast of the First Salmon was held; NOT the falls, which are S5-196, ~1 km downstream and under the lake)

n: The Tableland — it got through the last warm spell
note: Its cousins live from Alaska to Labrador. This one has been on top of one mountain in Maine for ten thousand years, since the ice went, and has not met another since. The mountains round about look right and have none — those ones died. It got through the last warm spell. Nobody is sure about this one. The state that lists it as endangered says it has no money to look at it.
ps: zone (the Tableland and adjacent slopes above timberline on Katahdin, 1,350–1,500 m — the entire world range of Oeneis polixenes katahdin)

note: Nobody lived here. They farmed out across the Delta and came in for the ceremonies; only the chiefs and priests stayed. Twenty-three mounds, twelve left — the road and the plough took the rest. By the nineteen-thirties they had ploughed the ramp clean off the fifty-five-foot temple mound. A garden club bought it. The river it was built to watch was a mile west when they started.
ps: zone (Winterville Mounds, Washington County, Mississippi, north of Greenville — twelve surviving mounds and two plazas on a 42-acre tract; the original centre had at least twenty-three)

n: Hester — they thought it was a golf bag
note: The river flooded, people camped, the river flooded again, for four thousand years — so the ground here is a layer cake six feet deep with the oldest at the bottom. Before they dug it, everyone thought the different spear points were different tools for different animals, like the clubs in a golf bag. They were not. They were centuries. This one terrace dates every other site in the region.
ps: zone (the Hester site, 22MO569 / 22MO1011 — a terrace of the Tombigbee near Amory, Monroe County, Mississippi; ROUGHLY PLACED)

n: The longhouse kitchen — she did his ceremony for him
note: The book about the falls opens on her, before dawn, in the longhouse kitchen: four hundred people coming, her husband the salmon chief too ill to do his part, and she is going to do it for him. She spoke for the village while the Army negotiated it away — and for the people who had no tribe to speak for them. Afterwards she went to the dedication of the power station. Her own recording is missing its first tape.
ps: point (the longhouse at Celilo Village, Oregon — where the salmon feast is prepared and held)

note: A clay compound on an island in a lake, at the top of the world. A beam in its foundation carries the sun's mark of 775 two rings from the bark, so they felled the tree in 777 — and in the spring, because the wood is all early growth and nothing grows there in a Siberian winter. Two years later they killed the khan who ordered it, for the religion it was built for. Nobody ever moved in. That is how we know what it was.
ps: point (Por-Bajin / Por-Bazhyn — a clay complex on an island in Lake Tere-Khol, Tuva, Russian Federation, near the Mongolian border)

n: Tāne Mahuta — the Lord of the Forest
note: In the beginning the Sky Father and the Earth Mother held each other so tightly that their children lived crushed in the dark between them. It was Tāne who set his shoulders against his mother and his feet against his father and pushed until the light came in. The largest kauri left standing bears his name. The Māori do not call it the oldest tree or the tallest tree; they call it an ancestor, and trace their own line back through it to the day the world was prised open. It is perhaps two thousand years old and fifty metres to the crown. A sickness is in the soil now, working toward the roots, and there is no cure — so you scrub your boots at the gate before they let you walk in and stand beneath your elder.
ps: point (Waipoua Forest, Northland, Aotearoa NZ)

n: Te Matua Ngahere — the Father of the Forest
note: A short walk from the Lord of the Forest stands the Father of it — lower, broader, older. Te Matua Ngahere is the widest kauri alive, a squat elder with a girth a ring of joined arms could not span, and it is thought to be older than the tall one, perhaps far older. Its crown is a hanging garden: perching lilies and orchids and whole other plants growing in the forks of it, a forest living in a tree, with birds nesting in what it carries. The tall one is on the postcards. This is the one the old people go quiet in front of.
ps: point (Waipoua Forest, ~1-2 km from Tāne Mahuta)

n: Jaya Sri Maha Bodhi — the oldest tree a hand ever planted
note: It is the oldest tree planted by a human hand whose planting day we can still name. In 288 BC a nun carried a branch across the sea from the tree in India the Buddha had sat beneath to wake, and a king took the branch and set it in his garden; and for twenty-three centuries since, kings and monks have watered it, walled it, hung it with gold and never once let it want. Two hundred generations have swept its terrace. Stand beneath it and you are as near to the Buddha as any living thing on earth can carry you — one cutting, one unbroken line of tending, from his shade to this.
ps: point (Mahamewna Gardens, Anuradhapura, Sri Lanka)

n: The crocodiles of Bazoulé — the animal everyone else runs from
note: The children swim in the same pond as the crocodiles, and the women wade in for water, and in living memory no one has been taken. The old people say that long ago, in a drought that was killing the village, the crocodiles led their ancestors to the last hidden water and so kept them alive; and ever since, the village feeds and guards the crocodiles, and when one dies it is wrapped and mourned and buried like a person, because it is family. To harm one is to wound your own ancestors. Across the border at Paga they say much the same, and add that each crocodile carries the soul of someone who has died. A covenant five hundred years old, between a people and the animal everyone else runs from.
ps: zone (Bazoulé, Burkina Faso; + Paga, Ghana — a regional practice, not one pond)

n: The Rainbow Serpent — the maker that keeps the water
note: Older than almost any story still told, the great Serpent came up out of the ground when the world was soft and went winding across it, and where its body dragged the rivers ran and the gorges opened and the waterholes filled and stayed full. It lives in the deep still water now. It made the country and it keeps the water, and the water is the life, so it is spoken of with care — and not all of it is spoken of at all. Nations across the whole continent know it by different names and tell it their own way; what they share is that the thing which shaped the land is not finished, and is not safely disturbed.
ps: zone (northern Australia — DIFFUSE; a continent-wide ancestral being, not a site)

n: Jonathan — the animal that has outlived the modern age
note: He was already an old animal when they shipped him to the island in 1882, which means he hatched around the time the first photograph was taken of a human face. He has outlived the invention of the telephone and the light bulb, two world wars, and something like forty of the men who governed the rock he grazes on. He is blind with cataracts now and has lost his sense of smell, and once a week a small team hand-feeds him fruit to keep him going. He does not know that he is the oldest living thing that walks on land. He crops the governor's lawn, slowly, as he has done for a hundred and forty years, while the empires come and go around him.
ps: point (Plantation House, St Helena, South Atlantic)

n: The Olive Tree of Vouves — the elder that still gives
note: The heart of it rotted away centuries ago — you can put your arm into the hollow where the oldest wood used to be — and still, every autumn, the living outer ring of the trunk sets fruit and the villagers press it into oil. It is somewhere between two and four thousand years old; no one can say closer, because there is no heartwood left to read the rings, which is only another way of saying it is too old to date. It was a wild tree once, and then a hand grafted it and made it tame, and it has been giving olives ever since — through the Minoans, through Rome, through all of it. When the modern Olympics wanted a wreath for the marathon winner, they cut the leaves from this tree: a ring of them, from a tree that was old before the first Games, laid on the head of whoever ran fastest that day.
ps: point (Ano Vouves, Kolymvari, Chania, Crete)

n: Old Tjikko — the tree that only just stood up
note: For nine and a half thousand years it was a shrub. A single Norway spruce on a cold Swedish mountain, kept low and huddled against the wind, dying back and sprouting again from the same root while the ice sheets finished leaving and the whole of written history came and went above it — Stonehenge raised, the pharaohs risen and fallen, Rome built and gone — all of it over the head of a plant that never grew taller than the snow. The trunk you can see is only a few centuries old; the root beneath it is older than farming. And only in the last hundred years, as the mountain has warmed, has the old spruce finally stood up — put out a straight trunk, five metres tall, for the first time in its long life. The warming that will remake this whole atlas is written in the posture of a tree that has stood here since the ice left.
ps: point (Fulufjället, Dalarna, Sweden — location protected; see coord status)

n: The clay lick — an animal mine for salt
note: This far from the sea the rain falls fresh, with no salt in it, and the forest that grows on that rain holds almost none either, so the animals living on the forest are starving for salt without knowing why. And the river, cutting its bend, has laid one band of clay bare in the cliff that happens to hold it. So at first light the parrots come — hundreds of them, scarlet and blue and green, up to eighteen kinds at once — and land on that single seam and eat the earth, and only that seam; they will not touch the clay a hand's breadth above it or below. A screaming kaleidoscope of birds, mining a cliff for the salt the ocean never sent them. The tapir and the deer come to the same wall at dusk, for the same reason.
ps: point (Colpa Colorado / the Tambopata clay licks, SE Peru — the phenomenon spans the western Amazon)

n: Lion Cavern — the oldest mine, dug for a colour
note: It is the oldest mine on Earth, and it was dug for a colour. Forty thousand years ago and more, people cut into this hill for red ochre and for the ore that glitters, and carried it out to grind and mix and smear on skin and stone — red for blood and birth and the holy, the sparkling kind kept for the chiefs. The San painted their rock with what came out of here, and the healers still hold the place sacred. And then, on the same mountain, in the 1960s, a company tore an open pit for iron and drank the summit down to a flooded hole in thirteen years. The little cave where people took only the colour they needed, for four hundred centuries, sits quietly beside it.
ps: point (Lion Cavern, Bomvu Ridge, Ngwenya Mountain, NW Eswatini — beside the modern open-cast pit)

n: Lemnian Earth — the healing clay dug once a year
note: Once a year, on a single morning, while the ground was still soft with dew, a priestess climbed the hill and dug — and no one else was permitted to touch the sacred earth. They carried it down, washed it, pressed it into little tablets and stamped each one with a seal: a goat, or the goddess, and in later centuries a cross. Then they sent it out across the world as medicine — the thing you took against poison, against snakebite, against the plague. Galen sailed here from Rome only to watch them dig it, and carried twenty thousand of the sealed pills home. For two and a half thousand years people trusted a stamped lump of one hillside's clay to save their lives. And when the old tablets were finally put under a microscope, it turned out they were a little bit right: the earth really does kill the germ.
ps: point (Kotsinas / the hill near ancient Hephaestia, NE Lemnos, Greece)

n: Lake Superior copper — the metal you did not have to melt
note: Everywhere else in the world, to get metal you first had to learn to melt rock — to build a fire hot enough to sweat copper out of stone. Here you did not. The glaciers had scraped the ground down to veins of copper already pure, already metal, lying in the rock and scattered through the gravel as nuggets you could pick up in your hand. So for eight thousand years the people here simply gathered it and hammered it cold into knives and spearpoints and fishhooks and beads, and traded it a thousand miles in every direction, and never needed a furnace at all. A whole age of metal, worked entirely out of what the ice had left lying on the surface.
ps: zone (the native-copper belt of the Keweenaw Peninsula + Isle Royale, Lake Superior — ~120 miles)

n: Mud-puddling — the salt the butterfly gives away
note: The butterfly on the flower is only the half of it you were meant to see. The other half is a crowd of them — males, mostly — packed wing to wing on a patch of wet mud or a heap of dung or the damp lip of a stream, drinking; and not for water, but for salt. The forest they hatched in has almost no sodium in it, and they need it badly, so they mine it from the ground, pumping stream-water through and out again by hundreds of times their own weight to strain the little salt from it. And when a male mates, he hands the salt he gathered to the female, folded into the packet with his sperm — a wedding gift of minerals, to make her eggs live. The prettiest thing in the air, come down to sip at the dirt, and giving the earth away as a dowry.
ps: zone (pantropical; representative pin, peninsular India — post-monsoon puddling of Papilionidae/Pieridae)

n: The Cingino Dam — the mountain won't give it, so they mine the wall
note: The mountain will not give them enough salt. Up here a grass-eater's diet runs short of it — sodium, and the calcium a nursing mother needs most — and there is nowhere on the bare high rock to find it. Except that engineers once poured a wall of concrete across the valley, and concrete, as it cures and weathers, sweats out a mineral salt the goats crave. So the ibex climb it. They go up a stone face that runs nearly straight to the sky, four feet on ledges no wider than a coin, their split hooves gripping like pincers, to lick the salt out of the wall — and it is mostly the mothers and their kids who make the climb, because they are the ones who need it. One slip is the end. The mountain wouldn't give it, so they mine the thing we built.
ps: point (Cingino Dam, Lago di Cingino, Valle Antrona, Piedmont, Italy — ~2,250 m)

n: Budongo — the same clay the healer digs
note: The chimpanzees of this forest go to certain holes in the ground and eat the clay, or dip a chewed-leaf sponge and drink the clay-water — for the iron and the salt in it, and to bind the poisons in the leaves they eat. And when they are ill they do something stranger: they swallow a mouthful of one particular soil just before or after a bitter medicinal leaf, and the two together fight the malaria better than either alone. The soil they choose is the same clay the human healer a valley away digs up to settle a sick child's stomach. Ape and human, with no word between them, reach into the same earth for the same medicine. And here the chimps eat more clay than they once did — because the palm that used to give them their minerals has been cut out of the forest, and the ground is what is left.
ps: zone (Budongo Forest / Sonso community, W Uganda; the behaviour spans East African forests + Virunga gorillas)

n: The Danakil salt road — the money cut from the ground
note: It is one of the hottest places anyone lives — a hundred and twenty metres below the level of the sea, the ground a blinding white crust, the air past fifty degrees — and it is made of salt. So the Afar go down into it with hand-axes and wooden poles and cut the crust into tiles, each the same size, and load them onto camels, and walk them for days up out of the furnace to the markets in the cool highlands. They have done this for a thousand years and more, and for most of that time the tiles were not only traded but spent: an amolé was money, and a man could carry his whole wealth as a stack of the ground. The camels are giving way to trucks now, and the potash companies are circling. One of the last places on earth where the money is cut straight out of the desert by hand.
ps: point (Lake Assale / Karum salt flats, Danakil Depression, Afar Region, NE Ethiopia)

n: Eating the earth — the oldest medicine, and everyone's
note: Every people that ever lived has, at one time or another, eaten the ground. Hippocrates wrote it down in Greece; Europeans ate sealed clay tablets as medicine for two thousand years and were still at it in the nineteenth century; the Andes dip their bitter potatoes in it, Aboriginal Australians turned to it in the lean months, the American South called it white dirt and West Africa calls it calabash chalk, and pregnant women the world over crave it still — for the iron in it, to bind the poisons in the food, to settle a turning stomach. It is the same reflex as the chimpanzee dipping its leaf-sponge, and the same instinct that sent a priestess up a Greek hill to dig one day a year. The notion that it is a strange habit of somewhere-else is an old colonial libel. Reaching into the earth for what the body lacks is one of the most human things there is — and always was.
ps: zone (a global human practice; representative pin, West Africa — origin and living market tradition)

n: Big Bone Lick — where everything came for the salt
note: A little salt spring bubbles up here out of an ancient buried sea, and for tens of thousands of years everything that needed salt came to it. In the Ice Age that meant mammoths and mastodons and giant ground sloths and long-horned bison, and a great many of them mired in the soft mud and died and were kept there, until the ground was a boneyard of monsters. Then the deer and the bison came, and the Shawnee and the Delaware, who boiled the brine down to salt and hunted the animals the salt drew in. And when settlers dug up the huge bones and sent them to the naturalists of Europe, the bones did something nobody expected: they proved that whole kinds of animal can simply be gone. The place where the idea of extinction was born is a salt lick — a spot where the earth offered salt, and everything alive came to take it.
ps: point (Big Bone Lick, Boone County, northern Kentucky)

n: Baltic amber — fossil sunlight the sea gives back
note: It is fossil sunlight — resin that bled from pine forests forty million years ago, buried under a sea, and washed back up as gold. After the autumn storms it comes ashore on the Baltic beaches, and people walk the tideline gathering it, as they have since the Stone Age; now and then a piece holds an insect that was alive before there was anyone to wonder at it. They made it into beads and amulets and carried it a thousand miles south — Baltic amber has been lifted out of the tombs of pharaohs and the shaft-graves of Troy. The Lithuanians say each tear-shaped piece is the sea-goddess weeping for the mortal man she loved, whose amber palace the thunder-god smashed to bits. Fifteen thousand years of people picking treasure up off a beach — given back by the sea, one storm at a time.
ps: zone (the Amber Coast / Sambia Peninsula and the SE Baltic shore — Kaliningrad, Lithuania, Gdańsk)

note: In a small adobe church in the New Mexico hills there is a little round pit in the floor, and the dirt in it is holy. Pilgrims come by the tens of thousands, some walking hundreds of miles at Easter, and they kneel and scoop the reddish earth — to rub it on whatever hurts, and, in the old way, to mix it with water and drink it. The walls of the little room are hung with the crutches of people who say they left them behind. The Pueblo knew this ground was a healing place long before the church stood on it; the Spanish who came named it for a healing Christ they had carried up from Guatemala. The dirt in the pit, they say, never runs out.
ps: point (El Santuario de Chimayó, Chimayó, northern New Mexico)

n: Esquipulas — the clay tablets they bless and eat
note: Four million people a year come to a white basilica in the Guatemalan hills to stand before a Christ carved from wood gone black with age and candle-smoke — and, while they are there, to buy a little tablet of pale clay, stamped with a holy image and blessed by the Church, and eat it, for their health. They call the clay tierra santa, holy earth, and the tablets benditos.
