It is the very same thing the ancient Greeks made on their island and sold across the Roman world — sacred stamped earth, taken as medicine — done again, unbroken, in the Catholic Americas. The healing here is credited to the Black Christ, and to the sulphur springs that rise nearby, and to the clay you can hold in your hand.
ps: point (Basilica of Esquipulas, SE Guatemala)

n: Chaco — the clay the Andes learned from the parrots
note: High in the Andes the wild potatoes are bitter with poison, and for at least two and a half thousand years the people here have known the trick: dip them in clay. They dry a pale earth, mix it with water and a little salt into a paste, and eat the toxic tubers dipped in it — and the clay grips the poison and carries it through, so a plant that would sicken you becomes dinner. They eat it for a sour stomach too. And the story they tell of how they learned it is the best part: they say they watched the parrots do it — the macaws that come down in their hundreds to the clay banks of the lowland rivers — and copied the birds. One animal watching another, and reaching into the same ground.
ps: zone (the Altiplano of Peru and Bolivia; archaeological evidence at Chiripa, Lake Titicaca)

n: Winning the turf — the fuel a country cut from underfoot
note: A country that had felled its forests found its fuel underfoot instead. A bog is dead plants pressed down under water for ten thousand years, halfway to being coal, and you can cut it out of the ground with a spade shaped for the work — the sléan — and dry it in the wind and burn it. For centuries the whole of rural Ireland went out to the bog in early summer to 'win the turf', a family and its neighbours slicing and stacking the dark sods together, and the smell of a turf fire is still the smell of home to people who left the island a hundred years ago. But the bog is also one of the greatest stores of carbon there is, and cutting it lets that carbon loose — so the law is closing the bogs now, one by one. The earth offered the fuel; taking it costs the sky.
ps: zone (the raised and blanket bogs of Ireland; also the Scottish crofting bogs / Hebrides)

n: Melos obsidian — the edge we crossed the sea for
note: There is a black glass the volcanoes make, and nothing else in the world takes an edge like it — sharper than any steel, sharp enough that surgeons still cut with it. On one small island in the middle of the Aegean the ground is full of the stuff, and people wanted it so badly that they learned to cross the open sea to reach it. In a cave on the Greek mainland there is obsidian from that island lying in layers thirteen thousand years old — which means that before farming, before pottery, before almost anything, someone was already building a boat, sailing out of sight of land, and coming home with the glass. The earth offered an edge, and to reach it we invented the voyage.
ps: point (the obsidian sources of Melos/Milos, Cyclades; the glass reached the mainland by sea)

n: Gomantong — the nest of spit, taken at the risk of your life
note: The most costly food that people take from any animal is a bird's spit. In the great caves here a small swift builds its whole nest out of its own saliva — drawn into threads and woven into a little translucent cup on the cave roof, dried hard — and for more than a thousand years men have climbed to the roof of the cave on ladders of bamboo and ropes of rattan, in the dark, high enough to die from, to lift the nests down. They are simmered into a soup the Chinese have prized for centuries as medicine and luxury both, worth its weight in silver. Only certain families may climb, and only twice a year, when the birds can spare the nests. A gift of the cave that has to be taken at the risk of your life.
ps: point (Gomantong Caves, Sabah, Borneo, Malaysia)

n: Bracken Cave — the sky's droppings, that fed the fields and loaded the guns
note: The largest gathering of warm-blooded animals anywhere on Earth is not a herd you can see by daylight — it is twenty million bats packed into one Texas cave, pouring out at dusk in a column that turns the sky black. Beneath them lie ten thousand years of their droppings, a hundred feet deep, and a whole hidden world lives on it: beetles past counting working the guano, and everything that eats the beetles. The people who held the cave mined that floor for two things — to feed their fields, because nothing makes a crop grow like it, and, in the war, to make gunpowder, because it is rich in the salt that makes powder burn. The same soft black earth that fed the corn also loaded the guns. The sky gives, one bat at a time.
ps: point (Bracken Cave, Comal County, Texas — outside San Antonio)

n: Lourdes — the plain water and the faith poured into it
note: A poor girl of fourteen, out gathering firewood, saw a lady in the rock who told her to dig in the mud at the back of the cave and drink. She dug with her hands and dirty water welled up; the next day it ran clear, and it has run ever since. That was all it took. The water has nothing special in it — it has been tested and tested, and it is only water, cold and clean — but a girl dug it out of the ground because a vision told her to, and now four million people a year come to drink it and bathe in it, and of the thousands who have said they were healed, the doctors have looked hard and cannot explain seventy of them. Plain water, and the faith poured into it.
ps: point (Grotto of Massabielle, Sanctuary of Our Lady of Lourdes, SW France)

n: Zamzam — the well that has never run dry
note: The most-drunk holy water on earth rises from a well beside the Kaaba, and the story is that it once saved a child. Abraham left Hagar and the baby Ishmael alone in a barren valley, and when their water ran out she ran seven times between two hills searching for more, and a spring broke open in the sand beneath the baby. She scrabbled to bank it in, saying zam, zam — stop, stop — and gave the well its name. Four thousand years on, the pilgrims still run those seven lengths between the hills, and still drink the water standing, and still carry it home in bottles to the sick who could not make the journey. Millions drink from it every year, and it has never once run dry.
ps: point (Zamzam Well, Masjid al-Haram, Mecca, Saudi Arabia)

n: The Amrit Sarovar — the pool the city is named for
note: A whole city is named for a pool of water called the nectar of immortality. Four and a half centuries ago a Sikh guru dug a great tank here and set a temple low in the middle of it — a door on each of the four sides, so that anyone of any caste or creed could walk in — and the pool around it, the amrit, the nectar, is held to heal the body and cleanse the soul. Pilgrims come from all the world to step down into it and dip beneath the water. And beside the pool the largest free kitchen on earth feeds anyone who is hungry, whoever they are, the same idea as the four open doors made edible. The water heals, they say. The welcome is the point.
ps: point (Amrit Sarovar, Sri Harmandir Sahib / Golden Temple, Amritsar, Punjab, India)

n: The clootie wells — give your sickness to the water
note: By certain springs in the Celtic countries there stands a tree hung all over with rags — thousands of strips of cloth, bright, then faded, then rotted to grey ghosts fluttering on the branches. You come with an illness, dip a strip of cloth in the well, wash the sore place with it, and tie the rag to the tree; and as the cloth rots away over the months, your sickness is meant to rot away with it. The wells were holy long before the churches came — a goddess, or a spirit of the water, lived in them — and the churches, unable to stop the practice, gave each well a saint instead. The same impulse hangs prayer-flags in Tibet and rags on the shaman-trees of Siberia. To give your sickness to the water, and let the earth take it back.
ps: zone (the clootie / rag-well tradition of the Celtic nations; flagship Munlochy, Black Isle, Scotland)

n: Whenua Hou — the island kept empty so a parrot can stay a parrot
note: The fattest parrot in the world cannot fly, comes out only at night, freezes when it is frightened, and booms like a slow heartbeat across the valley to call for a mate it may not find for years. It nearly finished them: by the time anyone counted properly there were fifty-one left on Earth. So they were carried one by one to a scatter of islands with no cats or stoats or rats, where every bird has a name and a radio on its back and a ranger who knows it — and slowly, boom by boom, egg by egg, they are coming back, past two hundred and fifty now. A writer came to see them before they were gone and taught the world to love the ridiculous, doomed, wonderful thing; the people of the island call it a treasure, and say they are a part of it and it a part of them. An island kept empty of everything that would eat a parrot, so that a parrot may go on being a parrot.
ps: point (Whenua Hou / Codfish Island, west of Rakiura / Stewart Island, New Zealand)

note: A glacier spends an age grinding a valley down toward the sea; the sea floods in; and you have a fjord — a wall of rock plunging a kilometre into black water, a waterfall every mile, the light changing by the minute. The whole crinkled coast of Norway is made this way, and it is so absurdly, deliberately beautiful that a comic novelist gave the credit to an alien. In The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy the fjords were the work of one Slartibartfast, a maker of planets who loved the little fiddly bits of coastlines and won an award for Norway — 'lovely crinkly edges' — and signed his name, it is said, deep inside one of the glaciers. The geologists explain that it was ice. The fjords do rather look like someone was showing off.
ps: zone (the West Norwegian fjords; anchored near Sognefjord / Nærøyfjord)

n: Highgate — the grave you find by the pot of pens
note: His headstone says only his name, his dates, and one word: Writer. But you will know the grave by the jar of pens standing in front of it — pens, because he wrote, and pens because somewhere in his books there is a planet where all the lost ballpoints of the universe secretly go to live. People bring them from all over the world and stand them in the little pot; they leave towels too, and coins, and once a small toy dolphin, so long and thanks for all the fish. He died at forty-nine, far too young, having taught a whole generation not to panic, to always know where their towel is, and that the answer to life, the universe and everything is a perfectly ordinary number. Every year on the day, his readers carry a towel through the streets in his memory.
ps: point (Douglas Adams's grave, Highgate Cemetery East, London)

n: Lake Maxinkuckee — the lake he kept inside his head
note: A man who spent his life writing that the great American disease is loneliness had, as a boy, the opposite of loneliness, and it was here. On this small Indiana lake his family kept a cottage in a row of cottages, and every one of them was full of relatives, and he wandered between them as through a village that all belonged to him. The lake taught him the shape of the world: five miles long, its shore a closed loop, so that whichever way a boy walked along its edge he would always, in the end, come home. He said it was heaven. He said it would be his lake as long as he lived, and that he had no wish ever to go back — because he carried the real one, not the dusty thing you could see from an aeroplane, safe and whole inside his head.
ps: point (Lake Maxinkuckee, Culver, Marshall County, Indiana)

n: Florida, Missouri — the man who came and went with the comet
note: He came in with the comet, and he said he would go out with it, and he did. Halley's Comet swings past the sun once in a long human lifetime — about seventy-five years — and it came in 1835, and two weeks later a baby was born in a two-room Missouri cabin who would grow up to be Mark Twain. In 1909, old and grieving, he said: 'I came in with Halley's Comet in 1835. It is coming again next year, and I expect to go out with it. The Almighty has said, no doubt: now here are these two unaccountable freaks; they came in together, they must go out together.' The comet returned in April 1910. He died the next day. One man's whole life, measured end to end by a clock that ticks in the dark between the planets.
ps: point (Mark Twain's birthplace, Florida, Missouri)

n: A field near Innsbruck — where the idea fell out of the sky
note: A middle-class English boy, raised in his grandmother's house — which was also an animal shelter, so that his whole childhood wheezed with hay fever — took a year off before university and hitchhiked across Europe on almost no money, with a battered paperback called the Hitch-Hiker's Guide to Europe. One night in Austria he lay down drunk in a field outside Innsbruck and looked up at more stars than a city boy had ever seen, and the thought drifted down onto him: somebody ought to write a Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy. He was never quite sure afterwards that it had really happened that way — he was very drunk — but the idea stuck, and years later it became a radio show, and then the funniest book ever written about the end of the world. Every year now his readers come to this field, carrying a towel.
ps: point (a field outside Innsbruck, Austria — the exact spot is unrecorded)

n: St John's, Cambridge — the club that rejected him taught him the trick
note: He came to this college for one reason, and it was not the English degree. Cambridge had the Footlights — the comedy club that had produced Monty Python — and a middle-class boy who had won his place here on a scholarship, with a school essay that somehow yoked together the Beatles and William Blake, wanted into it more than he wanted anything. They turned him down. He found them aloof and rather pleased with themselves, so he went off and made his own comedy with two friends, and joined instead the humbler society that performed in old people's homes, and only clawed his way into the Footlights at last, two years later, by sheer stubbornness. He did almost no studying — three essays in three years, he liked to say — and left with a middling degree. The club that shut him out had, without meaning to, taught him the one thing he needed: if they won't have you, do it yourself.
ps: point (St John's College, University of Cambridge, England)

n: The Bayeux Tapestry — they marvel at the star
note: In the spring of 1066 a star with a burning tail hung over England, and everyone knew it meant something. Seventy metres of embroidered cloth, sewn soon after, shows a crowd pointing up at it in alarm, and the stitched Latin says only ISTI MIRANT STELLA — these men marvel at the star. Beneath it the new king Harold sits uneasy on his throne, and a ghost-fleet of empty ships waits at the edge of the picture. Everyone read the omen to suit themselves: the English as heaven's anger at Harold, the Normans across the water as heaven's blessing on their invasion. Within the year Harold was dead with an arrow in his eye and a Norman duke wore the crown. It was the same star that comes every seventy-six years — Halley's Comet — sewn into wool six hundred years before anyone knew that.
ps: point (the Bayeux Tapestry, Bayeux, Normandy, France)

n: The Bloomfield River — the serpent that comes back
note: In an ordered, predictable sky, a comet is a wrongness, and across Aboriginal Australia it was mostly read as a warning — of sickness, of death, of evil magic loose in the world. In the Central Desert a comet was a spear, hurled across the heavens by a sky-being; on one island in the Gulf it was, more bluntly, a testicle trailing smoke. And along the Bloomfield River in Queensland the law-carriers of the Kuku-Yalanji keep stories that some researchers believe may hold a buried memory of Halley's Comet itself — a great serpent-shape returning to the sky across a human lifetime, again and again, longer than any one person could hold, so that only the story could carry it. Whether or not that is so, the pattern is the point: a people watching the sky closely enough, for long enough, to notice what comes back.
ps: zone (Aboriginal comet traditions; anchored on the Bloomfield River, Kuku-Yalanji country, Queensland)

n: Mawangdui — the people who wrote the comet down
note: The Chinese wrote the comet down. When a broom star swept across the sky it was a vile omen — an emperor would die, a war would come, the crops would fail — so the court kept astronomers watching the heavens the way a city keeps watch on its walls, and they recorded every one, apparition after apparition, for more than two thousand years. In a tomb at Changsha, sealed in the second century before Christ, someone left a book of silk painted with rows of comets, each shape labelled with the disaster it foretold — the oldest comet-atlas in the world. It is because of these patient, frightened records that we can look back and pick out one particular broom star returning again and again on its long loop: the comet we now call Halley's, first written down here in 240 BC.
ps: point (Mawangdui Han tombs, Changsha, Hunan, China)

n: The wake of the comet — twice a year you can stand in its dust
note: The comet itself comes only once in a long lifetime, but it leaves a wake, and the Earth sails through that wake twice a year. Halley runs backwards around the sun, against the traffic of the planets, so our orbit cuts across its shed trail of ice and dust in two places — and each spring, in early May, that debris burns up as the Eta Aquariid meteors falling out of the Water-Bearer, and each October the Orionids streak past the shoulder of Orion. They are among the fastest shooting stars in the sky, and they leave glowing trails that hang for whole seconds after they are gone. The comet is out beyond the planets now and will not be back until 2061 — but twice every year, if you go somewhere dark and look up, you can stand in its dust.
ps: zone (a sky-wide phenomenon; radiants in Aquarius in May, Orion in October)

n: 1910 — the first end of the world delivered by newspaper
note: In 1910 the oldest fear of comets met the newest machines, and the whole world panicked at once. Astronomers had learned to read a comet's light, and found in the tail Halley was about to trail across the Earth a whisper of cyanogen — a cousin of cyanide. It was a harmless trace in something thinner than any vacuum, but the newspapers printed COMET'S POISONOUS TAIL, and the terror ran around the planet. People sealed their doors and stuffed their keyholes; con men sold anti-comet pills and gas masks and bottles of clean air; a broker sold comet insurance; German farmers left their fields unplanted; bartenders swore that enough whisky in the blood would keep the gas out. The comet passed. Everyone lived. It was, perhaps, the first end of the world delivered by mass media — and it would not be the last.
ps: point (Yerkes Observatory, Williams Bay, Wisconsin — where the cyanogen was announced; the panic was global)

n: Tenochtitlan — the fire hung in the sky before the fall
note: Ten years before the ships came, they said, a tongue of fire stood in the eastern sky at midnight — narrow at the top, broad at the base, burning so bright it was like dawn in the middle of the night — and it hung there for a whole year while the people wailed. The king, Moctezuma, watched it from his palace roof and was afraid. He called his star-readers; when they swore they had seen nothing, he had them shut up to starve, and sent instead for the old astronomer-king of Texcoco, who told him plainly that everything he had foretold was now coming to pass. And it did: within the decade the strangers landed, and the empire fell. But the writers who set the omen down did so long afterward, in the language of the conquered — so that no one can now be sure whether the fire in the sky came before the catastrophe, or was hung there afterward to give the catastrophe a shape.
ps: point (Tenochtitlan — the Aztec capital, now Mexico City)

n: The West Mariana Ridge — the Sargasso of the Pacific eels
note: Where do eels go to breed? Aristotle thought they rose from the mud; for two thousand years no one could find an egg or a womb in them, and a young Freud cut open four hundred of them hunting for testicles and gave up. The Atlantic ones, we eventually learned, all swim to the Sargasso Sea to spawn and die. But the eels of Japan and China and Korea vanished into the Pacific, and their nursery stayed hidden for another sixty years — until a scientist followed ever-smaller, glass-clear, leaf-shaped larvae westward across the ocean to a chain of undersea mountains near the Marianas. There, on the dark of the new moon, just south of a line where two kinds of salt water meet, every eel that ever grew fat in those far rivers comes three thousand kilometres to spawn once and die. In 2009 he finally caught the eggs. No one has ever watched them do it.
ps: zone (Japanese eel spawning area, western side of the West Mariana Ridge, western North Pacific)

n: The Octopus Garden — a field of pearls in the midnight zone
note: Two miles down off the coast of California, in the cold and permanent dark, an extinct volcano leaks a little warmth into the water — and thousands upon thousands of pale octopuses have found it. They come here only to breed. The mothers turn upside down over their eggs, folding their arms into a ball, so that from above they look like a field of pearls scattered on the black seafloor; and there they stay, fanning clean water across the eggs and swatting away the shrimp and snails that would eat them. In the cold, such eggs can take years to hatch — but the volcano's warmth roughly halves the wait, and so the mothers gather at the warm rock to give their young the best chance. It is the largest gathering of octopuses ever found, perhaps twenty thousand of them, and no one knew it was there until 2018. When the eggs hatch, the mothers die.
ps: point (the Octopus Garden, base of Davidson Seamount, off Monterey, California)

n: Khejarli — a chopped head is cheaper than a felled tree
note: Three hundred years ago, in a desert village in Rajasthan, the king's men came to cut down a grove of khejri trees for firewood to burn lime for a palace. The trees were sacred to the Bishnoi, whose faith of twenty-nine rules forbids the harming of any tree or any animal, and a woman named Amrita Devi put her arms around a trunk and said: if a head is cut to save a tree, it is a good bargain. They cut off her head, and then her three daughters'; and the trees still stood, so more Bishnoi came, from village after village, and each one embraced a tree and was killed — until three hundred and sixty-three of them lay dead in the grove. Only then did the shamed king call it off, and forbid forever the cutting of a Bishnoi tree or the killing of a Bishnoi animal. They were hugging trees to save them two and a half centuries before the world had a word for it.
ps: point (Khejarli village, near Jodhpur, Rajasthan, India)

n: Dhauli — the emperor who carved a change of heart into rock
note: An emperor conquered a kingdom called Kalinga and, by his own count, left a hundred thousand dead — and the scale of it broke something open in him. On the rocks above the battlefield, and on rocks and pillars all across his empire, Ashoka had carved, twenty-three centuries ago, a change of heart. No living thing, he wrote, is to be slaughtered here or offered in sacrifice. He stopped the royal hunt; he ended the killing of animals for his own kitchen and turned away from eating them; he made a long list of creatures that must not be killed at all — parrots, geese, bats, turtles, squirrels, deer, and all four-footed things that are neither useful nor eaten; he forbade the burning of the forests they lived in, and had healing herbs planted and wells dug for the beasts as well as for the people. It is the oldest writing in India, and among the first laws anywhere to say that an animal's life is worth the protecting.
ps: point (Dhauli, Odisha — the Kalinga edict site; the edicts stand empire-wide)

n: Palitana — the city where no life may be taken
note: On a hill in Gujarat stand more than eight hundred temples, built over nine centuries, and the whole hill is so holy that no one — not even a priest — may sleep on it, and no one may carry food up its three thousand seven hundred steps. It belongs to the Jains, whose central rule, ahimsa, is to harm no living thing at all: who sweep the ground before they sit and strain the water before they drink, lest they crush some small life. In 2014 the monks of Palitana went without food until the town below agreed — no meat, no fish, no eggs to be sold or eaten anywhere within it, and nothing to be killed, not even a fish pulled from the water. It became the first city on Earth where taking an animal's life is against the law. Not everyone in the town chose it, and that argument is part of the story too.
ps: point (Palitana, Bhavnagar district, Gujarat, India)

n: Grime's Graves — where people first went down for the stone
note: You climb down a ladder into a green field pocked with four hundred hollows, and you are not very far down — nine metres, the height of a house — but you are five thousand years back. Neolithic people dug these shafts through the white chalk to reach a seam of jet-black flint, the good flint, flawless and glassy, that would flake to an edge you could shave with; they hacked it out in the dark with picks made from the shed antlers of red deer, and you can still see the scars their picks left in the walls. The chalk they dug through is the pressed white bodies of sea-creatures from a hundred million years before them, and the flint itself is the ghost of ancient sponges turned to stone. In one shaft that reopened on its own a few years ago, two antler picks lie exactly where a miner set them down and never came back. The Anglo-Saxons who found the pocked field long after thought it must be a god's graveyard, and named it for Woden. It was only ever a mine — the place people first went down into the earth, here, to take what it gave.
ps: point (Grime's Graves, Breckland, near Brandon/Thetford, Norfolk, England)

n: Kanku — the shelter that broke away
note: North of the opal town, a low escarpment crumbles into a field of flat-topped hills that seem to have broken away and wandered off across the plain, banded in rust and ochre and white. To the Antakirinja Matu-Yankunytjatjara, whose country this is, the place is Kanku — shelter — and it has always sheltered them: the land, the food, the plants, the animals. Dreaming stories run through these hills and out across a thousand kilometres of desert, and the hills are a teaching place for young men, and a source of the coloured ochres worn in ceremony; much of what the stories say is not for outsiders to be told, and that restraint is part of the respect the place is owed. The whole park is a registered sacred site, and its traditional owners hold native title to seventy thousand square kilometres around it. The miners came for what was under the ground. This was always about the ground itself.
ps: zone (Kanku-Breakaways Conservation Park, ~25 km N of Coober Pedy, South Australia)

n: The opalised sea — the deep-time dead turned to jewels
note: A hundred million years ago this desert was the floor of a shallow inland sea, and small monsters swam over it. When the sea dried, silica seeped down into the mud and the buried bones and slowly, atom by atom, replaced them — so that here, almost alone on Earth, the dead of that ancient sea did not merely fossilise but turned to precious opal, their skeletons remade in fire-coloured stone. In 1987 an opal miner's pick struck one of them: a whole small sea-reptile, seal-sized, its every bone opalised, with an opal fish still in its opal stomach. Someone named it Eric. When it came up for sale and the jewellers gathered to break it apart and sell it by the carat, the schoolchildren of Australia put their coins together, bought it, and gave it to a museum, whole. The earth here gives up its deep-time dead as jewels — and once, a country decided that some jewels are worth more left as bones.
ps: point (the opalised Eromanga Sea, Coober Pedy opal fields, South Australia; 'Eric' now at the Australian Museum, Sydney)

n: The chickadee's memory — a bird that lives by remembering
note: A chickadee weighs about as much as two sheets of paper, and it does not fly south. It survives the northern winter instead by remembering — because in the autumn it hides food, seed by seed, in thousands of separate places across the forest (some birds make five thousand hidden stashes in a single day), and through the killing months it goes back and finds them. Scientists who watched inside the tiny brain found that each time the bird hides a seed, a unique flash of cells fires like a barcode, and that same flash lights up again when it returns for that exact seed — a way of remembering single moments that has been seen in no other animal. The colder and harsher the winters a chickadee's family comes from, the bigger its memory-centre and the sharper its recall. And on the worst nights it lets its own body go cold, dropping its temperature to save the little fuel it has, shivering in the dark, remembering where breakfast is.
ps: zone (species-wide; the montane & boreal conifer forests of North America — anchored representatively in the Sierra Nevada)

n: The Wyalusing monument — for one species to mourn another
note: There were once more of these birds than any other on Earth — three to five billion of them, one in every four birds on the continent — and when a flock passed it took days, a river of wings from horizon to horizon that turned noon to dusk and made the children run indoors. In one human lifetime we shot and netted them all. The last wild one fell in 1902; the very last of the whole kind, a hen named Martha, died trembling in a cage in a Cincinnati zoo on the first of September, 1914, having never laid a fertile egg. On a ridge in Wisconsin above two great rivers, people later raised a small stone to her kind — the first time our species ever built a monument to a creature it had wiped out. The naturalist who spoke for it wrote the line that makes the stone worth visiting: for one species to mourn the death of another is a new thing under the sun.
ps: point (Passenger Pigeon Monument, Wyalusing State Park, Sentinel Ridge, Wisconsin)

n: Arslanbob — the forest that feeds the village
note: In the mountains of southern Kyrgyzstan stands the largest walnut forest on Earth, some of its trees a thousand years old and taller than houses, and every autumn the whole village goes up into it to live. Nearly three thousand families climb to their ancestral patches of forest with their animals and their bedding and stay for two months, gathering walnuts from dawn, sleeping in shelters among the trees, coming down only to empty their sacks — and the mountain fills with campfires and shared food and singing, a carnival of harvest. They say the forest was planted by a holy man the Prophet sent to find the perfect place for it; they say a lion-hearted hero named Arslanbob died among these trees and his handprints are in them still; they say Alexander the Great carried these nuts home, which is why half the world still calls the walnut the Greek nut. However it began, the arrangement has held for a very long time: the forest gives, and the village, in return, keeps the forest.
ps: zone (Arslanbob walnut forest, Jalal-Abad region, southern Kyrgyzstan)

n: The dove — holy to nearly everyone, everywhere
note: No other bird has been holy to so many peoples for so long. In Mesopotamia the dove belonged to Ishtar; in Greece the white dove drew the chariot of Aphrodite; a dove brought back the olive branch that told Noah the flood was ending; a dove came down out of heaven as the Holy Spirit; and pigeons, it is said, nested at the mouth of a cave to hide the Prophet Muhammad from the men hunting him. The same soft grey city bird you wave off a windowsill has carried, in nearly every faith around the inland seas, the whole weight of the sacred — love, peace, the soul, the messenger between earth and heaven. At the blue-tiled shrine of Hazrat Ali in northern Afghanistan, thousands of snow-white doves live in the courtyard and are fed by the children, and the people say the place is so holy that any grey pigeon who comes to join them turns pure white within forty days.
ps: point (Shrine of Hazrat Ali / the Blue Mosque, Mazar-i-Sharif, Afghanistan — anchoring the dove as sacred bird across cultures)

n: Tsikilili the truth-teller — the honest little bird
note: In the Cherokee country of the Smoky Mountains there was once an ogress with skin of stone and a long sharp finger like an awl, who killed people to eat their livers. When the people trapped her at last in a pit, their arrows only shattered on her stone hide, for they did not know where she kept her heart. A titmouse flew down and sang a song they heard as heart, heart, so they shot at her breast — and missed, because her heart was not there; and in their anger they cut out the titmouse's tongue, and to this day it is called the liar. Then a small quiet chickadee came and lit on the ogress's right fist, the one she held closed, and the people understood, and struck there, and killed her — for that was exactly where her heart was hidden. Ever since, the Cherokee have called the chickadee the truth-teller, and when it comes and sings near your house, they say someone you love is coming safely home.
ps: zone (Cherokee story-landscape, Great Smoky Mountains, western North Carolina / eastern Tennessee)

n: Karyai — the girl who became the walnut tree
note: The Greeks had a story for the walnut tree. Carya was a princess of Laconia whom the god Dionysus loved; when her jealous sisters tried to keep them apart, the god drove the sisters mad — they fled to the mountain and turned to stone — and Carya herself he changed into a walnut tree, karya. The people built a temple to Artemis the Lady of the Walnut-Tree, and every year the young women of the town danced there for her with baskets balanced on their heads. Those dancing girls were the karyatides, the maidens of Karyai — and when, later, architects wanted a column in the shape of a woman bearing a weight with quiet grace, they carved her in exactly that pose and gave her exactly that name. The stone maidens who hold up the porch of a temple on the Athenian acropolis are called caryatids to this day. A girl became a tree; the dancers became stone; and the stone still carries the roof.
ps: point (Karyai / Caryae, Laconia, Greece — sanctuary of Artemis Caryatis; the caryatids at the Erechtheion, Athens)

n: The kabutar khana — a handful of grain is not a small thing
note: In the crowded heart of Indian cities there are marked squares where, every morning, people come to pour out grain for the pigeons — the kabutar khana, the pigeon house. For the Jains especially, and for many Gujarati families, feeding the birds is not kindness but duty: their faith forbids harming any living thing, and to give food to a small hungry creature is to grant it the gift of fearlessness, and to earn merit for one's own soul. They tell an old story of a king who, to save a single dove from a hawk, offered the hawk his own flesh instead, weighing it against the tiny bird — and who fed the pigeons ever after. For more than a century wealthy men built these feeding-places across the city. Lately the city has tried to close them, fearing the birds spread disease, and now the people who feed the pigeons and the people who want them gone argue in the streets and the courts — because here a handful of grain is not a small thing at all.
ps: point (Dadar Kabutar Khana, Mumbai — the Indian kabutar-khana tradition)

n: The pigeon towers of Isfahan — palaces built for a bird's leavings
note: Out on the plains around Isfahan stand hundreds of round brick towers, tall as houses and finer than most houses, ringed with little doorways and topped with cupolas — and every one of them was built for pigeons. Not to eat them: in Iran the pigeon is not eaten. The towers were palaces raised for wild pigeons to come and live in, thousands to a tower, purely so the birds would leave their droppings behind — for pigeon dung is the richest fertiliser there is, and the famous melons of Isfahan grew fat on it, and the tanneries softened their leather with it. So the farmers gave the birds a beautiful home and asked for nothing but what the birds would leave anyway. There were three thousand of these towers once; a few hundred still stand, emptied now that fertiliser comes from factories — great echoing honeycombs raised, at bottom, on the courtesy of asking a pigeon to stay rather than taking anything from it.
ps: point (the pigeon towers / borj-e kabutar of Isfahan, Iran)

n: Nag Hammadi — the gospels hidden in a jar
note: A farmer digging for the soft earth he spread on his fields struck a sealed clay jar at the foot of a cliff, and hesitated — there might be a genie inside — then hoped for gold, and broke it open. Out came not gold but books: thirteen leather-bound volumes of papyrus, and in them the gospels the Church had lost, or buried, or burned. For these were the scriptures of the Gnostics, who held that salvation came not through belief but through secret knowledge; that the god who made this world was a lesser, blundering god; and that a spark of the true light lay trapped inside every person, waiting to wake. Sixteen centuries before, when the bishops drew up the list of which books were holy and which were heresy, some monk near here could not bring himself to burn these — and hid them in the ground instead, where they outwaited the whole of Christendom until a man looking for fertiliser found them.
ps: point (Nag Hammadi / base of the Jabal al-Tarif cliff, Upper Egypt)

n: Yazd — the fire that has never gone out
note: There is a fire in the middle of Iran that no one now living ever saw begin, because it has been burning, without a single interruption, since the year 470. For fifteen hundred years the priests of the old Persian faith have fed it and never let it die — carrying it from temple to temple, hiding it in remote villages when armies came — so that the flame burning today is the very same flame, unbroken, that was kindled while the Sassanid kings still ruled. To the Zoroastrians the fire is not a god but the purest sign of God: light itself, and truth. It is tended by hand several times a day, and has been, by a chain of hands reaching back past the fall of empires. Behind glass, in a bronze vessel, one of the oldest flames on earth quietly goes on burning.
ps: point (Atash Behram, Fire Temple of Yazd, central Iran)

n: Sky burial — the last gift is the body
note: When a Tibetan dies, the body is carried up to a high bare place and given to the vultures. It is not cruelty, and it is not only that the ground is too hard and the wood too scarce to do otherwise; it is meant as the last and highest act of generosity. The person is gone — the body left behind is only an empty boat — so it is offered, flesh and bone, to the great birds that circle down and carry it up into the sky, and in giving itself away the dead feeds the living one final time. The vultures are held sacred for this, sky-beings who bear the soul on toward its next life. The same thought raised the towers of silence of the Zoroastrians far to the west, where the dead were laid out for the same birds so as not to defile the pure earth or fire — a practice failing now, because the vultures themselves are dying out. To be eaten, in these places, is not the end of dignity but its proof.
ps: zone (Tibetan sky-burial charnel grounds — anchored at Drigung Til Monastery, Tibet; + the Zoroastrian towers of silence)

n: Qumran — the oldest Bible, in a jar
note: A boy chasing a lost goat along the cliffs above the Dead Sea threw a stone into a cave to flush the animal out, and heard instead the sound of a pot breaking. Inside were tall sealed jars, and inside those, wrapped in linen, were scrolls — the oldest Bible on earth. A desert community of Jews, austere and certain the end of days was near, had hidden their whole library here in jars as the Roman legions came to crush their revolt, about the year 68; and there it stayed, in the driest place in the world, for nearly two thousand years. When scholars unrolled the scrolls they found copies of almost every book of the Hebrew scriptures a thousand years older than any known before — the same words, kept safe in the dark, waiting under the stones the whole time anyone was arguing over them.
ps: point (Qumran caves, NW shore of the Dead Sea, Judean Desert)

n: Bethany beyond the Jordan — where he went into the water
note: On the far bank of the Jordan, in the green tangle of reeds along the river, a wild man in camel-hair who ate locusts and honey stood in the water and called people to wash themselves clean of their old lives. They came out to him in the wilderness, and he pushed them under the slow brown river and lifted them up changed; and one day a carpenter's son from Nazareth came down to the water, and John baptised him too, and — the story says — the sky opened and a dove came down. That was the beginning of everything that followed. Pilgrims have waded into this reach of the Jordan for the better part of two thousand years to be dipped where he was dipped, and they wade in still — the same reeds, the same slow water, the same old wish to come up out of the river someone new.
ps: point (Al-Maghtas / 'Bethany beyond the Jordan', E bank of the Jordan River, Jordan)

n: Nazareth — can anything good come out of it
note: It was a nothing little village — a few hundred people scratching a living in the hills of Galilee, so unregarded that people made a joke of it: can anything good come out of Nazareth? — and a boy grew up there, the carpenter's son, and it was here, they say, that an angel came to his mother before he was born to tell her who he would be. From this obscure hill town came the man around whom the largest religion on earth would gather. Now the village is a city, and a great church stands over the cave they hold to be the house where the angel spoke, and pilgrims from every country climb the hill to stand in the ordinary place where, in the telling, the whole enormous story quietly began. The good that came out of Nazareth is still being counted.
ps: point (Nazareth, Lower Galilee — the Basilica of the Annunciation)

n: Bokonon — the religion that admits it is lies
note: On a poor invented island in the Caribbean, in a book by Kurt Vonnegut, two shipwrecked men decide they cannot make the people any richer, so they resolve to make them happier instead — by giving them a religion. The religion is Bokononism, and its founder tells you on the very first page that all of it is lies — foma, he calls them, harmless untruths — and that you should close the book at once. But you are meant to live by the lies that make you brave and kind and healthy and happy. Its worshippers press the soles of their bare feet together to mingle their souls; they speak of the karass, the secret team of strangers you were born to do God's will with, and the granfalloon, the proud false team — a nation, a club — that in truth means nothing at all. A made-up faith, in a made-up place, in a story about the end of the world — and it says something true about why people believe anything: not because it is so, but because it helps.
ps: zone (fictional — the island of San Lorenzo, Caribbean; Vonnegut's 'Cat's Cradle')

n: The Miyawaki forest — a wild wood you can plant in a decade
note: A Japanese botanist noticed that the only truly wild forest left in his crowded country was the little untouched grove around each old shrine — dense, dark, many-layered, all natives, centuries deep — and he worked out how to grow one from scratch, fast. You find the trees that belonged in that exact place before people cleared it; you feed the soil; and then you plant them absurdly close together, dozens of kinds crammed into a plot the size of a tennis court, so they must race one another upward for the light. In twenty or thirty years, instead of two hundred, you have a small wild wood — denser, greener, louder with birds and insects than the tidy plantations around it. They are going in now in car parks and schoolyards and stripped-out lots all over the world: pocket-sized pieces of the forest that would return if we simply left, only hurried along by a hundred willing hands.
ps: zone (the Miyawaki method — now global; origin Japan, anchored at Yokohama)

n: The Future Library — a library you plant
note: In a forest north of Oslo there is a forest inside the forest: a thousand young spruce, planted in 2014, that no one may cut until the year 2114. Every single year until then, one writer hands over a brand-new book — sealed, unread, unpublished — into a quiet wood-panelled room in the city library, where the manuscripts wait in a hundred glass drawers you can see into but not open. In 2114 the trees will be felled and pulped into paper, and all one hundred books, written across a century by authors mostly long dead by then, will be printed and read for the very first time. The first of them called it a vote of confidence that there will still be a world, and readers, in a hundred years. The trees are growing the paper. The writers are writing for a reader who is not yet born. It is a library you plant.
ps: point (Future Library, Nordmarka forest, north of Oslo, Norway)

n: The cloud forest — the wood that drinks the sky
note: High on a tropical mountain, in the band where the rising air cools into permanent cloud, there grows a forest that drinks the sky. The trees stand with their heads in the fog, and every leaf, every hanging moss, every one of the thousands of little plants riding on their branches combs the mist for water — catching it sideways, straight out of the cloud — so that even in the dry season a steady drip falls through the canopy from no visible rain. A bare hillside in that same fog would stay parched; it is the forest itself that wrings the water out of the air and pours it, slow and clean, into the streams that whole cities drink from far below. Only a thin sliver of the earth is high enough and wet enough for this. And as the world warms, the clouds are lifting, rising up off the peaks — and the forests that live inside them are beginning, quietly, to go thirsty.
ps: zone (tropical montane cloud forest; anchored at Monteverde, Costa Rica)

n: Totoro's forest — the spirit that saved a real wood
note: Studio Ghibli's films are full of the old Japanese feeling that the forest is alive with spirits — that something benevolent and enormous lives in the great trees, and will keep the world in balance if only people leave it room. When Hayao Miyazaki made My Neighbor Totoro, he set it in the gentle wooded hills near his own home, where farmers and woods and rice paddies had lived together for a thousand years — and then watched those very hills begin to vanish under the spreading city. So he did the one thing a forest spirit could not: he helped start a fund, put in his own money from the film, and asked the millions of people who loved Totoro to help buy the woods back, parcel by parcel, and keep them. They did, and they still do — clearing the trails, tending the paddies. A made-up spirit of the forest turned out to be the thing that saved a real one.
ps: point (Sayama Hills / Totoro no Mori, Tokorozawa, Saitama, Japan)

n: Death Stranding — the game that dreamed this map
note: There is a game that imagines almost exactly this: the world has ended, not with a bang but with a kind of unravelling, and the survivors are scattered in sealed bunkers across a ruined continent, too afraid of the rain and the ghosts outside to reach one another. And the hero who saves them is not a soldier. He is a porter — a deliveryman — who saves the world by walking across it with a pack on his back, carrying what people need from one lonely settlement to the next, tying the broken places back together one crossing at a time, until the scattered survivors are a civilisation again. Its maker built the landscape out of Iceland's empty moss and black lava, and called it a newborn Earth. He said the oldest tools were the stick, to push bad things away, and the rope, to pull good things close — and that this was a story about the rope. It is, more or less, the same story as this atlas: that after the end, the way back is to carry things to one another.
ps: zone (fictional — a post-'Death Stranding' America, built from the landscape of Iceland)

n: The empty house — where Kojima's whole body of work begins
note: When he was thirteen his father died, and the house went quiet. His parents had watched a film together every single night — the children were not allowed to go to bed until it had ended — and now his mother went out to work, and he came home from school to an empty house and switched on the lights and the television so it would not feel so alone. He had wanted to make films; instead he grew up to make games, and every one of them, in the end, is about the same thing: people shut away from one another — in bunkers, on battlefields, across the border of the dead — straining to reach each other down a radio line or a rope. He has said that even now, checking into a hotel, the first thing he does is turn on the TV, to fill the silence. The lonely boy in the empty house never quite left. He just spent his life building ways for the isolated to find one another, and handed them to millions of strangers who felt, playing, a little less alone.
ps: point (the Kansai region — Kojima's childhood home; exact house unrecorded)

n: Night and Fog — the film his father made him watch
note: His father had wanted to be a sailor. As a boy he climbed onto the rooftops of Tokyo to watch the bombers come over and the fires start below — full of admiration for the soldiers and sickened by the war, both at once. So when his own son was still a child, he sat him down in front of Night and Fog, the unbearable film about the death camps, and made him watch the whole of it. It was far too much for a boy to understand. But it took. Nearly every game the son would ever make is, underneath the sneaking and the gadgets, one long argument against war — and against the bomb above all. He built into one of them a secret ending that no single player could ever unlock alone: it would only play if every player in the world laid down their nuclear weapons at once, and left them down. A father who hated war handed his son a horror he was too young for, and the son spent his life turning it into a plea.
ps: point (thematic — Tokyo, where his father witnessed the wartime air raids; the anti-war inheritance)

n: Ludens — a flag, not a spear
note: In 2015 the company he had worked for erased his name from the last game he made there, cancelled the one he was dreaming of next, and — it is said — would not let him walk up to collect his own award. So he left, and began again on his own, and chose an emblem for the new studio: a lone figure in a spacesuit, standing somewhere far out beyond the Earth at the edge of the unknown, holding not a weapon but a flag, to mark only that a person had reached this far. He called it Ludens, from an old idea that the truest name for our kind is not the one who knows but the one who plays — that from the moment we are born we invent games and stories and tools for the sheer joy of it, and share them, and that this making-for-its-own-sake is the deepest thing about us. From Sapiens to Ludens. It is about the most hopeful thing a person can say after being told they are finished: that the point was never to conquer anything. It was to plant the flag, and to play.
ps: point (Kojima Productions, Shinagawa, Tokyo)

n: Miyazaki Airplane — the beautiful machines that made the war
note: His father's family ran a factory that made one part — the rudder — for the Zero, the beautiful, deadly fighter at the heart of Japan's war. So the boy grew up on aeroplane money, in love with flight before he could draw, and he never stopped: almost every film he would ever make has something lifting into the sky — a witch on a broom, a girl on a glider, a pig in a seaplane, a castle walking on the wind. But he was a pacifist to the bone, and the machines he loved best were the machines that had burned the world. When he was small the firebombs came, and his family, who had a truck because of the factory, drove out through the flames while other people begged to climb aboard; he never forgot it, and said he made his films so that it would not have to happen again. At the very end he told the story straight: a gentle, short-sighted man who wanted only to make beautiful planes, and made the ones that killed. He loved them. He knew what they were. He could not do both, and he could not stop doing either.
ps: point (Miyazaki Airplane factory, Kanuma, Tochigi Prefecture, Japan)

n: The Brooklyn candy store — where the Foundation began
note: The magazines sat on the rack in his family's candy store, and the boy was forbidden to read them — until he pointed out that the science-fiction ones had the word Science right in the title, and so must surely be educational. He won that argument, and read everything, and grew up to write, among some five hundred other books, the one where a mathematician works out a science for predicting the whole future of civilisation — and foresees that the great galactic empire is about to fall, and thirty thousand years of darkness are coming. He cannot stop the fall. But he calculates that if a small band of people set themselves to gather and keep all of human knowledge in one place, they can shorten the dark age from thirty thousand years down to a single thousand. He called their refuge the Foundation, and their great book the Encyclopedia Galactica. It is, more or less, the reason a person might sit down and make an atlas of where people could live after the worst had happened.
ps: point (Brooklyn — the family's candy store; exact store unrecorded)

n: Story of Your Life — the grief you would choose again
note: He works as a technical writer near Seattle and has published only a couple of dozen stories in thirty years, each one built like a watch — and one of them is this. A woman learns the language of a visiting alien race whose minds hold past and future at once, and as she learns it her own mind begins to work the same way, until she can see her whole life laid out before her — including the daughter she has not yet had, and the accident that will take that daughter young. She sees the grief coming, all of it, from the very beginning. And she chooses it anyway: chooses to meet the man, to have the child, to love her all the way to the end she already knows is waiting. It is a story about whether you would still say yes to a life if you could see every sorrow in it in advance — and it answers, quietly, yes. Which is very close to the question an atlas like this one is really asking.
ps: point (Seattle / Bellevue area — where Ted Chiang lives and writes)

n: Pig Pen Sedaris — the humorist and the bin lorry
note: One of the funniest writers alive moved to a beautiful stretch of the English countryside, was appalled by the litter along its lanes, and began picking it up — not now and then, but for three to eight hours a day, walking twenty-odd miles with a bag of other people's rubbish, day after day after day. He was angry, he said, at the people who threw it from their cars, and just as angry at the people who walked past it every day; so he picked it up himself. He did it so relentlessly that the local council — apparently not quite realising they were honouring a world-famous author — thanked him in the only way that seemed to fit: they named a garbage truck after him. It has a pink pig painted on the side, and it says Pig Pen Sedaris. Of all the prizes a great writer can win, there is something almost holy about the one that means: this person loved a place enough to keep it clean with his own two hands.
ps: point (Horsham District / Pulborough, West Sussex, England — where Sedaris litter-picks)

n: Beth Abraham — the people who woke up
note: In a chronic ward in the Bronx a young doctor found a room of people who had not moved in decades. They were survivors of a sleeping-sickness that had swept the world after the First War, and it had frozen them mid-gesture — silent, statue-still — for forty years, written off and parked and forgotten. He did not think they were gone. He thought whole people were still in there, dormant, like volcanoes only sleeping. In 1969 he gave them a new drug, and they woke: stood, spoke, wept, danced, remembered, after forty lost years. It did not last — the drug turned unstable and one by one they slipped back under — but he had sat with each of them for hours and written down not their symptoms but their lives, because he believed the person mattered more than the disease. He called the book Awakenings. It is one of the most humane things a doctor ever wrote: proof that a life can still be in there, in the most blighted and forgotten body, waiting.
ps: point (Beth Abraham Hospital, the Bronx, New York)

n: Ask the next question — the Q with an arrow
note: He was born here under another name, and remade himself — a new name at eleven, and then a life spent asking things other people had quietly agreed not to ask. Late in his life he distilled his whole way of being into a single mark, which he wore around his neck and signed at the end of his name: a Q with an arrow shot through it. It meant: ask the next question. Whatever anyone tells you is the answer, ask the question that comes after it, and then the one after that, and never stop — because nothing is ever absolutely so. He turned that arrow on every rule people take for granted, gently, just to see what it was really made of. It is a good symbol for a book that tries to work everything out from first principles: not a full stop, but an arrow, always pointing on to the next question.
ps: point (Staten Island, New York — birthplace of Edward Hamilton Waldo / Theodore Sturgeon)

n: More Than Human — the lonely who become one
note: His masterpiece is about six broken, lonely, cast-off people — an idiot who is not quite an idiot, a runaway girl, a pair of children who vanish and reappear, a baby who thinks like a machine — who find one another and slowly understand that together they are a single new creature, the next thing after human: they call it homo gestalt. Apart, each is a freak nobody wants; joined, they are more than any of them could ever be alone. It is the tenderest idea in science fiction, and it was his true subject his whole life long — love as the thing that welds the lonely into something stronger than they started as, and the misfit as the seed of whatever comes next. He wrote it in prose that scans almost like verse, and he ended his days out here in Oregon, still — his daughter says — quietly defying his own famous law, since far more than a tenth of what he made was very good indeed. Everything in the book, through the bleakest cold, points toward love.
ps: point (Eugene / Springfield, Oregon — where Sturgeon spent his last years and died, 1985)

n: Cohoes — where Kilgore Trout lived in a basement
note: In a run-down mill town on the Hudson, in the books of Kurt Vonnegut, there lives a science-fiction writer named Kilgore Trout. He has written a hundred and seventeen novels and two thousand stories, every one of them stuffed with enormous, world-changing ideas, and not one of them has ever really been read — his publishers print them only as padding between the pictures in dirty magazines, and don't even tell him where. He lives alone in a basement and installs aluminium storm windows for a living. Vonnegut built him, fish-name and all, out of his friend Theodore Sturgeon — Sturgeon to Trout, one fish for another — the real half-forgotten genius given a shadow who could not catch a single break. And then the strangest thing happened: a real writer published a real novel under Trout's made-up name, and for a while the imaginary hack had a book on real shelves in the real world, exactly as if he'd climbed up out of the page. He is the patron saint of every unread idea — which makes this as good a place as any to salute him.
ps: point (Cohoes, New York — Kilgore Trout's basement in Vonnegut's later novels; a fictional resident of a real town)

n: Hole N' The Rock — the home a man dug out of a cliff
note: A man looked at a blank red sandstone cliff in the Utah desert and decided to live inside it. For twelve years he drilled and blasted and hauled the rock out by hand — fifty thousand cubic feet of it — until he had hollowed a fourteen-room house into the solid stone, with a fireplace whose chimney climbs sixty-five feet up through the cliff, shelves cut straight from the walls, and a bathtub sunk in the rock. His family moved in; his wife stayed on there for years after he was gone; and the two of them lie buried in the ground beside the door. It sounds like madness, and it half was — but the desert outside can hit a hundred and ten degrees, and inside the rock it never does. That is the oldest trick there is: when the world above is too hot or too cold to bear, the earth a few feet down stays even, and always has, and always will.
ps: point (Hole N' The Rock, ~12 mi south of Moab, San Juan County, Utah)

n: The yaodong — thirty million people who live in the earth
note: On the high yellow plateau of northern China, where the soil is a hundred metres of wind-blown dust — soft enough to carve with a spade, firm enough to hold a roof — people have lived inside the ground for four thousand years. They cut their homes into the hillsides, or dig a great square pit straight down into a flat field and hollow the rooms out sideways from its walls, so that from above you see only a courtyard sunk in the earth with the sky in it and smoke rising out of the ground. They are called yaodong, house-caves, and thirty or forty million people still live in them — the largest number of people on Earth who make their homes in the living land. And they are comfortable: two metres of earth over your head keeps the bitter winter out and the fierce summer heat off, with no furnace and no fan. When the plateau bakes, the family a spade's depth down stays cool. The oldest houses on the plateau may turn out to be the ones best suited to what is coming.
ps: zone (the yaodong of the Loess Plateau; anchored at Yan'an, Shaanxi, China)

n: Kissonerga — the first hole dug down to the water
note: Ten and a half thousand years ago, on a dry hillside on Cyprus, some of the first people ever to farm the island did a thing no one had needed to do before: instead of carrying their water up from a spring or a river, they dug straight down through the rock — eight metres into the ground — until they reached the water hiding beneath their feet. It is the oldest well anyone has ever found: a narrow shaft cut through limestone to the top of the buried water, the water table, which no one could see but someone reasoned had to be there. It is a small hole and an enormous idea. To follow the water you have to wander; to dig a well is to declare that you are staying. Nearly every settled town on Earth since is built on the same gamble these farmers made first — that the ground will hold water, and that you can reach it.
ps: point (Kissonerga-Mylouthkia, near Paphos, western Cyprus)

n: Atlit-Yam — the well the sea came up to drink
note: Off the coast of Israel, nine thousand years ago, there was a village on the shore, and in it someone lined a well with stone — the oldest stone-lined well ever found — and drew sweet water from it. Then, slowly, the thing the village could never have imagined happened: the sea rose. The Mediterranean crept inland, the water under the ground turned brackish, and the well that had given fresh water began to give salt. You can read the whole slow disaster in the layers of silt that filled it — the very moment the water went bad. The people left; the sea closed over the houses; and the village of Atlit-Yam lies quiet on the seabed now, its round stone well still standing among the drowned foundations, ten metres down. It is one of the oldest lessons the atlas holds: that a place can do everything right, and still lose to the water.
ps: point (Atlit-Yam — submerged Neolithic village off Atlit, near Haifa, Israel)

n: Eythra — the oldest woodwork on Earth
note: The oldest surviving piece of built woodwork anywhere in the world is not a temple, or a house, or a boat. It is the lining of a well. Seven thousand years ago the first farmers of central Europe, clearing the forests around what is now Leipzig, dug down to the water and shored the shaft with a neat box of split oak beams, jointed together with a care that astonished the archaeologists who found them — because the wood, sealed underwater below the water table, had never rotted. Ring by ring, the oaks could be dated to the very year they were felled: around 5100 BC. Nobody had thought the first farmers were such good carpenters. It turns out that the same people who first learned to make the ground grow food also, at the very same moment, learned to reach down for its water and to work its trees — farmer, well-digger and joiner, all one person, at the beginning.
ps: point (Linear Pottery culture wells, Greater Leipzig region — Eythra / Altscherbitz, eastern Germany)

n: Mount Lykaion — where the first werewolf was made
note: On the wind-scoured summit of the wolf-mountain in Arcadia, at an altar of heaped ash a thousand years deep, the werewolf was born — not as a monster in a story, but as a punishment and a rule. The old king Lycaon, the legend went, served the gods a dish secretly made of human flesh, to test whether they would know; and Zeus turned him on the spot into a wolf. His name is the root of the word lycanthrope, wolf-man, to this day. And they said that at the secret sacrifice here, any man who swallowed the one scrap of human meat hidden in the stew would turn into a wolf for nine years — and become a man again only if, in all that time, he tasted no human flesh at all. For a long while scholars thought this was just a horror story told about a wild and backward place. Then the diggers reached the bottom of the ash, and found, laid in the middle of the altar, the bones of a sacrificed child.
ps: point (the ash altar of Zeus Lykaios, summit of Mount Lykaion, Arcadia, Greece)

n: Jürgensburg — the werewolf who fought for the harvest
note: In 1692, in a country court in what is now Latvia, an old man of eighty stood accused of being a werewolf — and cheerfully agreed that he was one, and could not see the problem. He was, he explained, a Hound of God. Three nights a year he and the other werewolves changed their shape and went down into Hell, where the witches and the Devil had carried off the seed-grain and the fruitfulness of the fields; and there they fought them, with iron whips, and took the harvest back, so that the crops would grow and the people would not starve. If the werewolves lost, there was famine; because they mostly won, there was bread. The judges tried and tried to make him say he served the Devil. He would not: the werewolves, he insisted, were the dogs of God, and when they died they went to Heaven. So they flogged him and drove him over the border, still arguing. He is the one werewolf in all the old records who was, by his own account, on our side.
ps: point (Jürgensburg, Swedish Livonia — now Zaube, Latvia)

n: Igbo Landing — the water will carry us home
note: In 1803, on a tidewater creek in Georgia, a shipload of Igbo people — newly stolen from their homes and sold — rose up and took the boat, and drowned the men who had bought them. And then, rather than be marched onto a plantation, they turned and walked together into the water, singing, until the creek closed over them. The words they sang, people remembered, were that the water had brought them and the water would carry them home. Out of that day grew the most beautiful story the enslaved ever told about themselves: that Africans could fly — that some had never lost the old power, and that when the field and the whip became too much to bear, a person could rise straight up off the ground and fly back over the ocean to Africa, home. It was not true the way a fact is true. It was true the way a soul is true. They could not be kept, and they would not be owned, and one way or another they were going home.
ps: point (Igbo Landing, Dunbar Creek, St. Simons Island, Glynn County, Georgia)

n: The Maici — the people who believe only what they have seen
note: Along a small river in the Amazon live a people who will not tell you a story that no living person has witnessed. They have no myth of how the world was made, no god in the sky, no heaven waiting, no account of anything before the memory of someone still alive — because none of it can be seen, and what cannot be seen, or told by someone who saw it, is not worth saying. A missionary came to give them Jesus; they asked if he had met the man; he admitted he had not; and they lost interest, kindly, and went on with their morning. And yet their world is thick with spirits — not far-off and theological, but right there: one bright morning the whole village stood on the bank calling out to a spirit named Xígagaí, who was standing on the white sand across the water and threatening them, while the missionary and his small daughter looked and looked and saw only an empty beach. Their sacred is not the deep past or the far sky. It is whatever is on the beach this morning. They may be the only people on earth who kept just the part of the holy you can point at.
ps: point (the Pirahã villages of the Maici River, Amazonas, Brazil)

n: Gamla Uppsala — the temple with the golden chain
note: For a long age, the heart of the old Swedish religion stood on this plain: a temple hung about with a chain of gold that flashed for miles, and inside it the three gods on one throne — Odin for war, Thor for the storm and the famine, Freyr for the marriage bed. Beside the temple grew a single great tree that never lost its green, and under it a well; and the people understood the tree to be the World Tree itself and the well the well of fate, so that to stand here was to stand at the still centre where the seen world and the unseen one touched. Every ninth year the whole country came, and for nine days they killed nine males of every kind of living thing — horses, dogs, and men — and hung the bodies in the grove beside the temple, until every tree in it was reckoned holy from the dead it held. Then the new faith came and burned the temple and built a church on the very spot, because that is what you do with a place too sacred to leave in someone else's hands.
ps: point (Gamla Uppsala, just N of modern Uppsala, Sweden — the temple site + Royal Mounds)

n: Ukonsaari — the island you must not leave empty-handed
note: In the far north of Finland, in a stark clear lake near the top of the world, a steep rocky island rises straight out of the water: Ukonsaari, the island of Ukko, the thunder god. The Sami who fished this lake would row out to it and leave an offering — meat, a coin, a scrap of silver, cheese — before a hunt or a journey; and the whole of their country was like this, scattered with holy stones and hills and springs they called sieidi, each one a place where the world would make a bargain with you. The bargain was real and it went both ways: you gave the sieidi its due and it gave you fish and luck, and if it failed you, you could scold it, or punish it, or break it, for not keeping its side. Then the missionaries came and called all of it witchcraft. They burned the drums the noaidi used to travel between the worlds — of thousands, some seventy survive, in glass cases far from home — and they threw down the sieidi. Later the dam-builders finished what the missionaries began, and drowned whole valleys of holy ground under still grey water. But the island is still there, in the middle of the lake. And people still row out. And they still do not come empty-handed.
ps: point (Ukonsaari / Ukko's Island, Lake Inari [Inarijärvi], Finnish Sápmi)

n: The machines that thought in water
note: Twice in the last century, people built machines that did their thinking in water. In Moscow in 1936 an engineer named Lukyanov filled a wall of glass tubes and taps with water and used the rising and falling levels to solve the fearsome equations that govern how heat spreads and cracks concrete — because water, flowing, obeys the very same mathematics as heat, so a puddle can do a physicist's arithmetic. Thirteen years later, in London, an economist stood up a six-foot tangle of tanks and pipes and coloured water and called it the whole British economy: spending ran downhill, taxes were pumped back up to the treasury, and when you opened a valve to cut a tax you could watch the money slosh through the country in real time and see, with your own eyes, what your policy would do. One machine computed concrete and the other computed capitalism, and both did it the way a river does — by letting the water find its level. It turns out the world is full of things that will do your sums for you, if you can work out which liquid to ask.
ps: point (dual — the MONIAC at the LSE, London; and Lukyanov's Water Integrator, Moscow)

n: Antikythera — the computer at the bottom of the sea
note: In 1901 sponge-divers working a Roman wreck off a tiny Greek island hauled up, among the bronze statues, a corroded lump the size of a shoebox. It sat ignored in a museum until someone noticed a gear tooth in the rock. Inside was a machine two thousand years old: thirty-odd bronze gears, their teeth filed one by one, that a person turned with a crank to make the heavens move. Set the date, and it showed you where the sun and moon and planets stood, gave you the phase of the moon, and — years in advance — named the very days the sun and moon would go dark in eclipse; it even counted down to the next Olympic Games. Nothing so intricate would be built again anywhere on earth for fourteen hundred years. Someone in the ancient world understood the sky so exactly that they could pour it into brass and carry it in a wooden box — and then the box fell into the sea, and the knowledge to make another was simply lost, for a thousand years and more, as if it had never been.
ps: point (the Antikythera wreck, off Antikythera island, Greece; the mechanism rests in Athens)

n: Al-Jazari — the man who gave machines a soul
note: Eight hundred years ago, in the land between the rivers, a court engineer built machines that moved by themselves — and wrote a book so that others could build them too. He made an elephant that told the time, with a scribe inside and a bird on top and a serpent that tipped a ball every half-hour; he made a little brass band on a boat — a harpist, a flute-player, two drummers — whose beats you could change by moving pegs, so that it is remembered now as the first thing a person ever programmed. He made a peacock that poured water for you to wash your hands, and offered you the soap, and then the towel. Nearly all of it ran on water: the slow, patient fall of water through hidden bowls and floats was the power and the clockwork both. He called his book The Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices, and he drew every gear himself, and he built his machines to delight as much as to serve — which is why he is sometimes called the man who first gave a machine a soul.
ps: point (the Artuqid court, Amid / Diyarbakır, Upper Mesopotamia [the Jazira], SE Turkey)

n: Dujiangyan — the waterworks that has run for two thousand years
note: Twenty-three centuries ago a governor in Sichuan looked at a river that flooded his people every spring and starved them of water every summer, and instead of damming it, he learned it. He and his son built, in the living stone and the current itself, a thing with no wall and no gate: a long stone snout in mid-river, shaped like a fish's mouth, that splits the water in two — sending the flood down one channel and the farm-water down the other — and does it one way in the wet season and another in the dry, all on its own, by the shape of the riverbed alone. A curve in the bank flings the silt aside so the channel never clogs. A narrow neck cut through a mountain doles out exactly as much water as the plain can drink. It has needed no engine and no dam in all that time. It turned Sichuan into the richest farmland in China, and it is pouring the same water onto the same fields this very afternoon, twenty-three hundred years on — because the men who made it did not try to beat the river. They agreed with it.
ps: point (Dujiangyan, on the Min River where it enters the Chengdu Plain, Sichuan, China)

n: The women who were the computers
note: Before a computer was a machine, it was a person — usually a woman, usually paid almost nothing, doing by hand the arithmetic the men had no patience for. At an observatory in Massachusetts a whole room of them sorted the stars: hundreds of thousands of pinpricks on glass photographic plates, measured and catalogued one by one. One of them, Henrietta Leavitt, working through plates of a distant cloud of stars for thirty cents an hour, noticed that a certain kind of star blinks more slowly the brighter it truly is — and realised this gave her a ruler. Line up how fast such a star blinks against how bright it merely looks, and you can tell how far away it is. It was the first way anyone had ever found to measure the distance to another galaxy; it is, essentially, how we learned the size of the universe. She was a computer, in the old sense, and she computed the cosmos. The men whose names went on the papers had her do the sums.
ps: point (Harvard College Observatory, Cambridge, Massachusetts)

n: The quipu — the empire written in knots
note: The Inca ran an empire of millions — its census, its taxes, its storehouses, its armies, its calendars — without a single written word. They kept it all in knots. A quipu is a cord with many strings hanging from it, and the knots along each string, by their kind and their spacing, count in ones and tens and hundreds and thousands, so that a whole province's grain, or the tally of its men, hangs there to be read by a running finger. Specialists called knot-keepers made them and read them, and a runner could carry a valley's whole year of accounts in a bundle you could hold in one hand. The Spanish, who could not read them, thought them primitive, and burned most of them — and yet we still cannot fully decode the ones that survive, for the strings may hold not only numbers but names and stories: a written language made of knots, whose key we lost before we understood it was one. A thousand of them still hang in the world's museums, keeping their counts, waiting.
ps: point (Cusco / the Andes — the Inca heartland; quipu span Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, Chile, Argentina)

n: The slime mold that drew the Tokyo railway
note: There is a creature with no brain, no nerves, and no parts — it is one single cell, though it can spread across a dinner plate — and it is very good at a problem that keeps human engineers up at night.
