Scientists laid oat flakes on a wet tray in the exact pattern of the cities around Tokyo, set the slime mold down on the flake that stood for Tokyo, and let it grow. It oozed out in every direction, found all the food, and then thinned itself back until what remained was a web of living veins joining the flakes — and the web it made was almost exactly the Tokyo rail map, as efficient and as fault-proof as the one a nation of engineers had taken decades to perfect. It solves mazes the same way, withdrawing from every dead end until only the shortest path is left. It does this with no thoughts at all, only the blind arithmetic of where the food is and how the flow should run. It is a useful thing to keep in mind, in an atlas full of clever people: the cleverest answer to a hard problem is sometimes reached by something that cannot think.
ps: point (representative — the Tokyo rail experiment; Physarum polycephalum is a global organism)

n: The plant that counts to five
note: In a few damp acres of the Carolinas, and nowhere else on Earth, grows a plant that can count. The Venus flytrap will not waste its snap on a raindrop, so it waits: when an insect brushes one of the little trigger-hairs inside the trap, the plant fires off an electrical pulse — very like the ones in your own nerves — and remembers it, for about twenty seconds. One touch, nothing. A second touch inside that window, and the trap slams shut in a tenth of a second. But it keeps on counting. Three touches, four, five, as the trapped insect struggles, and the plant begins to pour out the juices that will digest it — more struggling, more counting, more digestion. It has no brain and no nerves and no notion that it is doing arithmetic; it counts with waves of calcium and the memory of a pulse. Counting, it turns out, is older than brains. A green thing in a bog worked it out long before we did.
ps: point (the Green Swamp / coastal Carolinas — the Venus flytrap's tiny native range, ~within 75 mi of Wilmington, North Carolina)

n: The colony that computes without a mind
note: No single ant knows the way. But a colony of them, each blindly dropping a little scent as it walks and preferring whatever path already smells strongest, will between them find the shortest route from the nest to the food — every time — because the short path gets walked more often, so it smells stronger, so it gets walked more often still. Nobody decides; the answer simply accumulates in the dirt. Bees do something even stranger. When a swarm needs a new home, a few hundred scouts fly out, and each that finds a good hollow comes back and dances for it — dances harder for a better one — and other bees go and look and return and dance too, until one site has gathered enough dancers to tip the whole swarm into flying there. They choose the best home available more than four times in five, with no leader, no vote-counter, and not one bee who has seen all the choices. A mind is not the only thing that can decide. A crowd of simple creatures, each following simple rules, can compute an answer none of them could reach alone — which is a hopeful thing to keep in mind, for a species that has to decide big things together.
ps: point (representative — honeybee swarm-decision experiments, Appledore Island, Maine; ant + bee collective computation is worldwide)

n: Península Valdés — where you can watch the whole life
note: Each winter, when the far south turns cold, about two thousand southern right whales leave the icy water near Antarctica and swim north to two calm gulfs cupped by a peninsula of Patagonian cliff. They come to do everything that matters: to court, to mate, and to give birth in water warm and sheltered enough for a newborn to learn to breathe. This is the one place on Earth where you can stand on a beach and watch the whole life of a great whale unfold close to shore — the vast dark mothers, each known by the rough pale pattern on her head as surely as by a face, resting in the shallows while their calves practise the surface; the males rolling and calling in the courtship crowds. A hundred years ago we had very nearly killed them all. They came back to the same gulfs their grandmothers used, because a whale remembers where it was born, and returns to give birth there in turn.
ps: point (Golfo Nuevo & Golfo San José, Península Valdés, Chubut, Patagonia, Argentina)

n: San Ignacio — the lagoon of the whales that came back
note: Every gray whale on the eastern side of the Pacific spends its summer feeding in the cold at the top of the world, off Alaska, and then swims the length of a continent — one of the longest journeys any animal makes — down to a handful of shallow, salt lagoons on the desert coast of Baja California, to give birth. A century and a half ago a whaler named Scammon found these lagoons and very nearly emptied them; gray whales were killed here by the thousands, in the exact water where they came to be born. And now the strangest gentle thing in the natural world happens here. The mothers, entirely of their own accord, bring their new calves right up to the little open boats, and lift them to be touched. Nothing makes them do it. The animals we hunted to the edge, in this very nursery, return to it every winter and let human hands rest on their young. It began with a single whale, in nineteen seventy-two, who chose to come close. The others have been coming close ever since.
ps: point (Laguna San Ignacio & Laguna Ojo de Liebre / Scammon's, El Vizcaíno, Baja California Sur, Mexico)

n: Silver Bank — where the humpbacks are born hungry
note: In the open Atlantic north of the Dominican Republic there is a drowned plateau of old coral, shallow enough to calm the sea above it, and every winter it holds the largest gathering of humpback whales on Earth — as many as five thousand, come from the cold feeding grounds off New England, Canada, Iceland, clear across the ocean. They come to sing, to spar, to mate, and to give birth. And here is the hard, astonishing fact of it: not one of them eats. There is no food for a humpback on the bank. The great males sing for weeks on an empty stomach; a mother gives birth and nurses a calf that drinks hundreds of pounds of milk a day, all of it drawn from a body that is slowly starving, until she has lost a third of her weight — and then she turns and swims fifteen hundred miles back to the cold, still fasting, her baby at her side. Half the calves will not see a year. They are born, every one of them, into a journey that begins hungry and does not stop.
ps: point (Silver Bank / Banco de la Plata, Sanctuary for Marine Mammals of the DR, N of the Dominican Republic)

n: The Auau Channel — the humpbacks that fill the winter sea with song
note: In the shallow blue channel that Maui and Lanai and Molokai make between them, the North Pacific humpbacks come each winter — thousands of them, a great share of the whole ocean's population — down from the cold feeding water off Alaska, three thousand miles away, to breed and to bear their young in the warm lee of the islands. Put your head under the water anywhere in the channel and you will hear them: the long, structured, endlessly revised song of the males, the most elaborate piece of music any animal makes, rising up through the hull of a boat and into your chest. Every male in the ocean sings very nearly the same song each season, and every season it changes, a little at a time, all of them together — a slow hit tune passed whale to whale across the whole North Pacific. They come here to sing it, and to make the next generation that will one day sing it too.
ps: point (the Auau Channel, between Maui / Lanai / Molokai, Hawaiian Islands)

n: The nursery of the whale that is almost gone
note: Off the coast of Georgia and northern Florida, in shallow water a few miles out, is the only place left in the world where the North Atlantic right whale is born. There are about three hundred and seventy of them alive — perhaps seventy mothers among them — and each winter the pregnant ones make the long swim down from New England and Canada to have their calves here, in the warm, in the shallows, as their kind has done since long before there was a Georgia or a Florida to name the coast. The whalers called them the 'right' whales because they were the right ones to kill: slow, close to shore, and they floated when they were dead. We stopped hunting them a lifetime ago, and still they die — struck by the ships that cross their nursery, or dragged under by the fishing ropes they cannot see or shed. Almost none of them now die of old age. Every calf born in this water is counted, and named, and watched over, because the arithmetic is close, and the whole future of a creature that has swum these seas for millions of years may come down to whether enough of them can simply be born here, and not be killed, in this one stretch of sea.
ps: point (the SE US calving ground, off Georgia & NE Florida — critical habitat, Cape Fear NC to below Cape Canaveral FL)

n: Vava'u — where the calves learn to swim
note: In the warm clear water of the Tongan islands, the humpbacks of the whole South Pacific come each winter to give birth — and to teach. A newborn humpback is not yet strong enough for the open ocean, so the mother lingers here in the sheltered blue for months, in the lee of the coral islands, while the calf swims and dives and builds the muscle it will need for the long cold road back to Antarctica. Tonga is one of the very few places on Earth where a person may slip into the water beside them, and be looked at, unhurried, by an animal the size of a bus with a two-week-old baby at her side. It is worth remembering, if you go, that you are a guest in a nursery — and that the whales are working.
ps: point (Vava'u archipelago, Kingdom of Tonga, South Pacific)

n: Abrolhos — the reef that keeps the calves
note: Off the coast of Bahia, where Brazil's continental shelf broadens out and the country's largest coral reefs break the swell, the humpbacks of the South Atlantic gather each winter to breed — and mothers with newborns crowd into the shallowest, calmest water of all, close in behind the reef, where the surf is gentle and the sharks and the shoving males cannot easily follow. More of the whales here have calves at their sides than at almost any other breeding ground on Earth; this is, above all, a nursery. A century of whaling had cut this population to a few hundred animals. They now number in the tens of thousands, and every winter they return to the same warm reef to make the next generation — one of the great quiet recoveries of the age.
ps: point (Abrolhos Archipelago / Abrolhos Bank, off southern Bahia, Brazil)

n: Sainte-Marie — the whale road of the Indian Ocean
note: Along the eastern edge of Madagascar, past the long thin island of Sainte-Marie and into the deep sheltered pocket of Antongil Bay, the humpbacks of the southwest Indian Ocean come up each winter from the cold Southern Ocean to court and to calve. They have done it far longer than there has been a name for the island or a boat to watch from: the same warm bay, the same few months, the mothers holding their calves in the calm water while the males sing below. It is one of the oldest commutes on the planet — a road with no marks on it, worn only into the memory of the animals, and followed, year upon year, without fail.
ps: point (Île Sainte-Marie / Nosy Boraha & Antongil Bay, eastern Madagascar)

n: Walker Bay — the town with a whale crier
note: Each winter the southern right whales come up from the sub-Antarctic to the sheltered bays of the southern Cape, and they come so close to the little town of Hermanus that a man is employed to walk the cliff path and blow a horn of dried kelp to tell people where they are — a whale crier, perhaps the only such job in the world. In the calm water below, and along the wide reserve down the coast, the mothers rest with their calves near enough to shore to be watched from dry land: the vast smooth backs with no fin, the rough pale crowns of callosity by which each one is known. They cross thousands of kilometres of cold ocean to give birth in this one warm curve of bay, and the town has arranged its whole year around their coming, the way a coastline learns the habits of whatever returns to it.
ps: point (Walker Bay, Hermanus, + De Hoop Nature Reserve, Western Cape, South Africa)

n: The Head of the Bight — a hundred whales below the cliff
note: Where the great arc of the Australian Bight meets the raw edge of the Nullarbor, the land ends in a wall of pale cliff and the Southern Ocean rolls straight in. Almost the entire southern right whale population of Australia — nineteen in every twenty — comes each winter to this one stretch of sheltered water beneath the cliffs to calve. Stand at the top of the Bunda Cliffs in July and you may see a hundred of them at once, resting in the swell far below: mothers and their new calves, the whole surviving future of the Australian right whale gathered into a single bay at the bottom of a continent. They feed nowhere near here — their food is a thousand kilometres south, in the cold — so they come only to do this one thing, and when it is done, they leave.
ps: point (Head of Bight, Great Australian Bight, South Australia)

n: The Three Forks — the meeting of the middle waters
note: The Osage do not call themselves the Osage; that is a French mishearing. They call themselves the Children of the Middle Waters — because in the beginning, they say, the People of the Sky came down from among the stars and met the People of the Earth, and where the two joined they became one people, in the middle: between sky and ground, between water and land. So a place where waters meet is not, to them, only a place. It is the shape of who they are. And here, in the green bottomland where the Arkansas gathers in the Verdigris and the Grand, three rivers come together at once — three roads of moving water, joined by the older roads worn overland to reach them — a crossing already ancient seven thousand years before there was a map to mark it. A people born of a meeting, at the meeting of the waters. When the traders came, they saw a place to grow rich; when the treaties came, they saw a place to take. But before it was any of that, it was the middle of the world — which is a thing the Osage knew before anyone told them otherwise.
ps: point (the Three Forks confluence of the Arkansas, Verdigris & Grand/Neosho rivers, NE Oklahoma; nr Muskogee / Fort Gibson)

n: The Law of the Tongue — the whales who hunted with us
note: For as long as the Thaua people can remember — long before any white man came — the killer whales of Twofold Bay were their brothers. So when whalers arrived and the Thaua men went to work the boats, something happened that has happened nowhere else on Earth: the orcas and the whalers hunted together. Each winter a pod would find the great baleen whales migrating past, herd them into the bay like dogs working sheep, and then one big bull — for thirty years it was a whale the men called Old Tom — would race to the whaling station and slap his tail on the water, again and again, until the men launched their boats, and lead them out to the trapped whale, sometimes towing the very rope. And when the whale was killed, the men left it in the water a day so the orcas could take first the lips and the tongue, which was their share; that was the law, and both sides kept it for generations. Old Tom's skeleton is in a museum now, the teeth worn smooth from the ropes he pulled. The people whose brothers the whales were still call them by that name.
ps: point (Twofold Bay / Eden, New South Wales, Australia)

n: Laguna — the dolphins that tell the fishers when to throw
note: In a lagoon in southern Brazil, for more than a hundred and forty years, wild dolphins and human fishers have fished as partners. The men stand waist-deep in the murky water with their throwing-nets and wait, because on their own they cannot see the mullet. Then the dolphins come, driving the fish in from the sea toward the line of men — and at the exact moment, each dolphin gives a signal, a sudden hard dive, that means: now, throw here. The nets come down, the school scatters, the men fill their baskets and the dolphins snap up the fish that break away. The fishers know the dolphins by sight and by name, and the good ones — Scooby, Caroba — work the same beach for fifteen years. Neither taught the other on purpose. The dolphins learned it from their mothers and the men from their fathers, two lines of teaching running side by side down the generations, until one beach holds a single custom shared by two species that cannot exchange a word — and never needed to.
ps: point (Laguna, Santa Catarina, southern Brazil)

note: For thirty years a scientist and a parrot worked at the edge of what a bird was believed able to do, and together they moved it. The parrot, a grey named Alex, learned to name a hundred things, and seven colours, and five shapes, and to count to six, and even to add — but the astonishing part was that he seemed to understand. Shown two objects, he could say what was the same about them and what was different. Asked how many were blue, he answered. Asked which was bigger, he knew. He grasped the idea of none — of zero — which a human child does not reach until it is four. He grew bored, and demanded to be taken back to his cage, and asked for things that were not in the room. Every night the scientist said goodnight, and Alex answered; and on the last night of his life, not knowing it was the last, he said what he always said: 'You be good. I love you. See you tomorrow.' In the morning he was gone. A brain the size of a walnut had spent thirty years quietly taking apart the idea that only we can think.
ps: point (Irene Pepperberg's lab, greater Boston — MIT / Harvard / Brandeis, Massachusetts)

n: Prairie-dog town — the language that describes you
note: On the high plains of the American West live small burrowing rodents who appear, if you listen closely enough, to be talking about you. For thirty years a biologist recorded the alarm calls of prairie dogs — the sharp 'chee-chee' they bark when a predator crosses their town — and found that they are not one call but many: a different bark for a hawk than for a coyote, for a dog, for a human. And inside the call for 'human' is more: the animals seem to pack into the layered overtones of a single chirp how big you are, and what colour your shirt is, and whether you are carrying a gun. They can, it appears, even describe a thing that is not there — a trick of language we had thought was ours alone. Whether to call it a language at all is still argued. But the next time the barking runs ahead of you down the burrows of a prairie-dog town, consider that the town may be passing your description along, so that everyone underground already knows what is coming, and what it is wearing.
ps: point (Gunnison's prairie-dog range, Colorado Plateau nr Flagstaff, Arizona)

n: The whales of Dominica — the alphabet we are learning to read
note: Off the island of Dominica live families of sperm whales, the largest predators on Earth, and they talk to one another in clicks — patterns of them, like Morse code, called across miles of dark water, that biologists call codas. For a long time we could hear the codas but not read them. Now, for the first time, machines built to find patterns have been turned on the whales' clicks, and they have begun to find structure inside: rhythm and tempo, subtle stretches and extra beats, combining in ways that look less like a handful of fixed signals and more like the beginnings of an alphabet. No one yet knows what the whales are saying. But we are, right now, in the first years of the first serious attempt in history to cross the water and understand another species in its own voice, rather than teaching it ours. If it works, this century will be remembered as the one in which humanity finally learned it was not the only thing talking.
ps: point (the resident sperm whale families off Dominica, eastern Caribbean)

n: Pigeon Forge — the town named for a bird that is gone
note: Every year some ten million people drive into a little valley in the Tennessee Smokies — to the go-karts and the outlet malls and the theme park that Dolly Parton built — in a town called Pigeon Forge. Almost none of them know they are saying the name of a ghost. The forge was a real iron forge, raised on the river in 1817. And the river is the Little Pigeon, and it was named for the passenger pigeons: the birds that once came through these mountains in flocks so vast they snapped the limbs off the beech trees they roosted in and turned the noon sky dark for hours as they passed. There were billions of them; no bird on the continent was more numerous. And then, inside a single human lifetime, we shot and netted them down to nothing, until the very last one died in a zoo in 1914. The pigeons are a century gone. But their name is still here — painted on ten thousand signs above the traffic, spoken by millions who have never seen the bird and never will. A whole vanished species, remembered by accident, in the name of a town that forgot why.
ps: point (Pigeon Forge, on the Little Pigeon River, Sevier County, Tennessee)

n: The UN Detention Unit, The Hague
note: In a Dutch suburb where children play outside the gate stands a prison built for the worst people alive: men who ordered genocides, who made rape a weapon of war, who sent children to kill. They are given clean cells, books, a kitchen where old enemies cook side by side. It can look like the failure of justice. It is its summit. A court that tortured them would become them, so this one refuses, and extends to the author of a massacre the dignity he denied to everyone. No one here has earned it; that is the whole meaning. To hold that no person, however monstrous, falls outside the circle of humane treatment is the hardest vow a civilisation makes, and here, against every hunger for revenge, it is kept. This is what mercy looks like when it is owed to no one, and given anyway.

n: The milpa — three plants that feed each other
note: Three plants that need each other: maize standing tall for beans to climb, beans pulling nitrogen from the air to feed the maize, squash sprawling below to shade out weeds and hold the soil damp. Nobody buys fertiliser for a milpa; the field makes its own. The Maya have grown these three together for thousands of years and speak of them as a single thing, and the ground is left richer, not poorer, for the growing. It is the oldest rebuttal to the idea that feeding people must cost the earth — a garden built so each plant hands the next what it lacks, running on sunlight, rain, and the intelligence of the arrangement. The whole science of the closed loop is already here, in a cornfield that fixes its own nitrogen.

n: The Chagga home gardens — a forest you can eat
note: On the wet southern slopes of Kilimanjaro the Chagga grow their food in layers, the way a forest does: coffee in the shade of bananas, bananas under scattered timber trees, beans and taro and herbs threading between, fodder grass at the roots for stalled cattle whose manure goes back to the beds. Nothing is a field; everything is a garden with a canopy. Some of these plots have fed the same families for centuries on ground that never rests and never fails, because the layering means something is always growing, always dropping leaves, always feeding the layer below. It is one of the densest continuous food systems on Earth, and from above it looks like the mountain forest it replaced — which is the point.

n: Kasugayama — the forest no one has cut in twelve centuries
note: Behind the great shrine at Nara stands a hill of forest that no one has cut for twelve hundred years. In 841 the court forbade the felling of trees and the taking of animals on Kasuga's mountain, and the ban simply held — reign after reign, war after war — until the ordinary forest of central Japan had vanished everywhere else and this one grove of it remained: primeval, unlogged, the trees grown to sizes the rest of the country forgot. A shrine forest, the word goes: the wood that guards the god and is guarded in turn. It is proof of something easy to doubt — that a culture can decide a piece of old forest is not for using, and mean it long enough for the meaning to become the trees themselves.

n: Parque de la Papa — a thousand potatoes kept alive
note: High above the Sacred Valley, six Quechua communities keep something no seed bank can: over a thousand kinds of potato, alive, in the ground, grown out year after year on terraces their ancestors cut. Not a museum of varieties but a working library — pink ones and black ones and twisted ones, each suited to a particular altitude, a particular frost, a particular way of being cooked, each kept because the mountain throws a different disaster every year and diversity is how you are ready for all of them. They have even walked home varieties that had drifted into a distant gene bank, returning them to the soil. This is genetic wealth held the only way it stays alive: as food someone still bothers to plant.

n: Qingtian — the rice and the fish that keep each other
note: In the terraced valleys of Qingtian the rice and the fish have kept each other for more than a thousand years. The fish move through the flooded paddy eating the insects and weeds that would otherwise need poison, and their waste feeds the rice; the rice in turn shades the water and shelters the fish from the sun. One flooded field yields two foods — grain and protein — and asks for neither pesticide nor fertiliser, because each creature does a job the farmer would otherwise have to buy. The world's soil scientists gave this the very first place on their list of agricultural systems worth saving. The farmers of Qingtian simply call it how you grow rice, and have called it that since before the practice had a name anywhere else.

n: Gotsch's syntropic farms — hurrying the forest back
note: A Swiss plant breeder bought five hundred hectares of dead Bahian pasture in 1982 — land the neighbours had written off as ruined — and grew a forest that feeds people. Ernst Gotsch farms by hurrying succession along: he plants everything at once and densely, the fast crops and the slow trees together, then prunes hard and lets the cuttings feed the soil, so the system builds canopy and fertility in the same motion. No bought inputs go in; the springs that had dried came back, the rain returned, and the ground now holds more of what plants need than it did untouched. He gave it a name — syntropy, the opposite of running down — and proved on degraded land that a farm can add to the living world instead of subtracting from it. The oldest wisdom, written out as a method.

n: Robert Hart's forest garden — the food-forest in the cold
note: On a small plot on Wenlock Edge, an Englishman spent the last decades of his life proving the layered food-forest of the tropics could be built in a cold country too. Robert Hart stacked his garden the way a wood is stacked — a canopy of fruit trees, a lower layer of nuts and bushes, herbs and roots and climbers filling every gap down to the ground — so that a plot the size of a suburban lawn produced food at every height with almost no digging and no bare earth. It was tiny, and it was a manifesto: that even in Shropshire drizzle you could grow the way a forest grows, in perennial layers that feed themselves, and never break the soil again. Much of the temperate world's forest-garden movement traces back to that one small, crowded plot.

n: Martin Crawford's forest garden — a wood you can eat, measured
note: At Dartington in Devon, Martin Crawford planted a two-acre wood you can eat. Thirty years on it is a thicket of some hundreds of species — fruit and nut trees over shrubs over perennial vegetables over ground-cover and roots — that yields food across the whole year and asks for no ploughing, no annual sowing, almost no weeding, because a mature forest garden suppresses its own weeds and feeds its own soil. He runs it as a research trust, measuring what works in a temperate climate and teaching it outward, so the knowledge does not die with one gardener. In a country of mown grass and bare tilled fields, it is a quiet argument for the opposite: land that produces more the longer you leave its structure alone.

n: Knepp — control used to hand control back
note: For generations Knepp was a failing farm on heavy Sussex clay, losing money against the soil every year — until its owners did the unthinkable and stopped farming it. They took the fences down, put out old breeds of cattle and pigs and ponies to roam as wild grazers once did, and let the land decide what it wanted to be. Within twenty years the bare fields had become a mosaic of scrub and grassland and wood that drew back birds England thought it was losing — nightingales, turtle doves, storks, the great purple emperor butterfly — in numbers seen almost nowhere else. Knepp is control used to hand control back: a demonstration that the richest thing a piece of land can do is sometimes simply to be released, and watched.

In a village built by hand on the shore of Lake Selingue, five hundred farmers, fishers and herders from eighty countries gathered in 2007 and named their movement for a woman. Nyeleni was a Malian peasant who out-farmed the men who scorned her, remembered for coaxing a wild grain — fonio — into a crop her people still eat centuries on. The movement that carries her name makes a single claim: that the people who grow the food, not the markets that price it, should decide who eats. The loop power keeps cutting, tied back together on purpose — brick by brick.
ps: point (Nyeleni village / Selingue, Mali)

n: The Great Oxidation — the air, and the world it cost
note: Two and a half billion years ago a microbe in the sea learned to split water with sunlight and breathe out oxygen. For ages the rock and the ocean drank the gas as fast as it was made; then the sinks filled, and it climbed. It was poison — it rusted the oceans, corroded nearly everything then alive, tore the methane blanket off the sky and dropped the world into an ice that reached the equator. The old anaerobic life was dethroned into the mud and the deep, where its children still live and still refuse the air. And that waste gas is the air you are breathing now. The most creative thing life has ever done and the first world it ever ruined were the same act.
ps: point (Hamersley banded-iron ranges, Pilbara, W. Australia); event was planetary
